" } , { " role " : " assistant " , " content " : " 4 " } , ] formatted = tokenizer . apply_chat_tem plate ( messages , return_tensors = " pt " ) labels = formatted . clone () # Mask prompt tokens ( set to -100 so cross - entropy ignores them ) prompt_len = len ( tokenizer . apply_chat_template ( messages [:1]) ) labels [: , : prompt_len ] = -100 loss = lm_model ( input_ids = formatted , labels = labels ) . loss # === 3. Value Head ( PPO Critic ) === # Adds Linear ( hidden_size -> 1) on top of the LM backbone value_model = o e r s M h u a . from_pretrained ( model_name , torch_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , device_map = " auto " , ) # value_model . v_head : Linear ( hidden_size -> 1) # Returns both LM logits AND per - token value estimates inputs = tokenizer ( " Explain quantum computing " , return_tensors = " pt " ) lm_logits , loss , values = value_model ( ** inputs , return_dict = False ) # values shape : ( batch , seq_len , 1) -- scalar estimate per token # === 4. Reward Head ( Reward Model ) === # Classification head : Linear ( hidden_size -> 1) on last token reward_model = o e r u e s i i . from_pretrained ( model_name , num_labels =1 , torch_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , device_map = " auto " , # single scalar output ) # Scores entire sequence by pooling the last token hidden state inputs = tokenizer ( " Good response here " , return_tensors = " pt " ) reward_score = reward_model (** inputs ) . logits # shape : ( batch , 1) Listing 1.3: Loading and using different prediction heads with HuggingFace. Weight Tying: LM Head = Embedding Matrix Transposed Most modern LLMs tie the LM head weights with the input embedding matrix: lm_head.weight = model.embed_tokens.weight. This means the LM head is not separately learned layerit reuses the embedding table. Benefits: fewer parameters (V saved), better generalization, and the geometric structure of the embedding space directly determines token probabilities. You can verify this in HuggingFace: model.lm_head.weight is model.model.embed_tokens.weight returns True for most models. 57 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 1.5 Optimization Theory for LLM Training
sections.content: Training large language model means finding the set of parameters θ (billions of weights) that minimizes the loss function L(θ) typically the negative log-likelihood of the next token. This is an optimization problem in extraordinarily high-dimensional space, and the algorithm used to navigate this space determines whether training succeeds, diverges, or stalls.

sections.title: 1.5.1 Gradient Descent: The Foundation
sections.content: What is Gradient? The gradient θL is vector that points in the direction of steepest increase of the loss. Each component tells us how much the loss would change if we slightly increased θi parameter θi. To decrease the loss, we move in the opposite direction: where η > 0 is the learning rate the step size. This is gradient descent [77]. θt+1 = θt ηθL(θt) (1.10) Figure 1.8: Gradient descent: starting from random initialization θ0, each step moves the parameters in the direction that reduces the loss, with step size controlled by the learning rate η. The process converges toward (local) minimum. Why Full Gradient Descent is Impractical. Computing the exact gradient requires evaluating the loss over the entire training dataset (trillions of tokens for LLMs). This is computationally prohibitive single gradient step would require full pass over all data. Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). The solution: estimate the gradient from small random subset (mini-batch) of the data [78]: θL(θ)

sections.title:
1
B
sections.content: B i=1 θℓ(θ; xi) where is the batch size (typically 1K4M tokens for LLMs). The mini-batch gradient is noisy but unbiased estimate of the true gradient. Why Mini-Batch SGD Works Computational efficiency: Each step costs O(B) instead of O(Ntotal). With = tokens and 15T total tokens, each step is 4 billion cheaper. Noise as regularization: The stochastic noise helps escape sharp local minima, finding flatter regions that generalize better. GPU utilization: Mini-batches are large enough to saturate GPU parallelism (matrix 58 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems multiplications become compute-bound rather than memory-bound). Convergence: Theoretically converges to local minimum at rate O(1/ ) (slower than exact GDs O(1/T ), but each step is millions of times cheaper). From SGD to Adaptive Methods. While SGD with momentum works well for vision models (CNNs), LLM training requires adaptive optimizers algorithms that maintain per-parameter learning rate.

sections.title: 1.5.2 Why Vanilla SGD Fails for LLMs
sections.content: Stochastic Gradient Descent updates weights as: SGD Problems for LLMs θt+1 = θt ηθL(θt) Different gradient scales per layer: Early layers in transformer have much smaller gradients than later layers (vanishing gradients). single learning rate η is too large for some parameters and too small for others. Sparse gradients: Embedding layers receive gradients only for tokens in the current batch. Most embedding rows have zero gradient. SGD with momentum wastes momentum on zero-gradient rows. Saddle points: High-dimensional loss landscapes have many saddle points. SGD can stall; adaptive methods escape faster. Sensitivity to learning rate: SGD requires careful tuning; 2 change in η can cause divergence.

sections.title: 1.5.3 Adam – Adaptive Moment Estimation
sections.content: Adam [79] maintains per-parameter estimates of the first moment (mean of gradients) and second moment (uncentered variance of gradients).

sections.title: Adam Update Equations
sections.content: Given gradient gt = θL(θt), hyperparameters β1, β2, ϵ, η: Step 1 Update biased first moment estimate: Step 2 Update biased second moment estimate: mt = β1mt1 + (1 β1)gt Step 3 Bias correction: Step 4 Parameter update: vt = β2vt1 + (1 β2)g2 ˆmt = mt 1 βt 1 , ˆvt = vt 1 βt 2 θt+1 = θt η ˆmt ˆvt + ϵ Typical values: β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.95 or 0.999, ϵ = 108, η = 104 to 105. 59 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems What Each Term Does mt (momentum): Exponential moving average of gradients. Smooths out noisy gradient estimates. β1 = 0.9 means the current gradient contributes 10% and the history contributes 90%. vt (adaptive LR): EMA of squared gradients. Parameters with consistently large gradients vt). Parameters with small gradients get larger get smaller effective learning rate (η/ effective LR. This is the key to handling different gradient scales per layer. ˆmt, ˆvt (bias correction): At = 1, m1 = (1 β1)g1 is much smaller than the true mean. 1) corrects this initialization bias. Without it, early steps are too small. Dividing by (1 βt ϵ (numerical stability): Prevents division by zero. Also acts as floor on the effective learning rate.

sections.title: 1.5.4 AdamW – Decoupled Weight Decay
sections.content: AdamW [80] fixes subtle but important issue with how weight decay interacts with adaptive optimizers. Why L2 Regularization = Weight Decay in Adam With L2 regularization, the loss becomes + λ regularization gradient is scaled by the adaptive factor 1/ ˆvt: 2 θ2, so the gradient is gt + λθt. In Adam, this θt+1 = θt η ˆmt + λθt ˆvt + ϵ Parameters with large vt (large gradient variance) get less regularization. This is not what we want weight decay should be uniform. AdamW Decoupled Weight Decay AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017) applies weight decay directly to the parameters, outside the adaptive scaling: θt+1 = θt η ηλθt ˆmt ˆvt + ϵ ˆvt. This gives uniform regularization across all The weight decay term ηλθt is not divided by parameters regardless of their gradient history. Typical value: λ = 0.1 for LLM training. Always Use AdamW Never Plain Adam for LLMs The difference between Adam and AdamW is subtle but matters. With Adam + L2, the effective weight decay is stronger for parameters with small gradient variance (e.g., biases, LayerNorm parameters) and weaker for parameters with large gradient variance (e.g., attention weights). AdamW gives the intended uniform regularization. Most frameworks default to AdamW; doublecheck your optimizer class. 60 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 1.5.5 Learning Rate – The Most Important Hyperparameter
sections.content: Typical Learning Rates by Training Phase Phase Typical LR Notes Pretraining (from scratch) Continued pretraining SFT (supervised fine-tune) LoRA fine-tuning 1e-4 to 3e-4 1e-5 to 1e-4 1e-5 to 2e-5 1e-4 to 3eLarge model, large batch Smaller LR to preserve knowledge Standard range Higher LR for adapter weights For RL learning rates (PPO, DPO, GRPO) see 11.15.

sections.title: 1.5.6 Learning Rate Warmup
sections.content: Why Warmup is Necessary At the start of training, vt (the second moment estimate) is initialized to zero. After bias correction: ˆvt = vt/(1 βt 2). At = 1 with β2 = 0.999: ˆv1 = v1/(1 0.999) = 1000v1. This means the effective learning rate is η/ However, if the first gradient is unusually large (common at initialization), the second moment estimate can be dominated by this outlier, causing erratic early steps. Warmup mitigates this by starting with very small LR and gradually increasing it, giving vt time to accumulate reliable estimate. 1000v1 much smaller than intended. Linear warmup: ηt = ηmax t/Twarmup Typical warmup duration: 15% of total steps for pretraining; 310% for fine-tuning (shorter runs need proportionally more warmup) For SFT: 50200 warmup steps is typical

sections.title: 1.5.7 Learning Rate Schedules
sections.content: Figure 1.9: Common learning rate schedules. All include linear warmup phase. WSD (Warmup-StableDecay) is the emerging standard for pretraining. (a) Constant. Simplest schedule. Good for short fine-tuning runs where you want to avoid over-decaying the LR. Risk: no annealing means the model may not converge to the sharpest minimum. 61 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems (b) Cosine Decay. ηt = ηmin + 1 2 (ηmax ηmin) 1 + cos !! Twarmup Twarmup π Standard for pretraining and SFT. Smooth decay avoids abrupt LR changes. ηmin is typically ηmax/10. (c) Linear Decay. Simpler than cosine, similar empirical results. Preferred when you want predictable LR at any step. (d) WSD Warmup-Stable-Decay. The new standard for large-scale pretraining [25, 81]. Three phases: 1. Warmup: Linear ramp to ηmax (15% of steps) 2. Stable: Constant ηmax for the majority of training 3. Decay: Fast cosine or linear decay to ηmin (last 1020% of steps) Key advantage: the stable phase allows checkpointing at any point and continuing training. The decay phase can be applied at the end of any run. (e) Cosine with Restarts (SGDR). Periodic restarts reset the LR to ηmax. Can help escape local minima. Less common for LLMs; more useful for smaller models.

sections.title: Gradient Clipping
sections.content: Gradient clipping rescales the gradient if its global norm exceeds threshold: gt gt min (cid:18) 1, (cid:19) τ gt where τ is max_grad_norm (typically 1.0). Gradient Clipping vs. LR Reduction Gradient clipping and reducing the learning rate both limit the size of parameter updates. The difference: clipping preserves the direction of the gradient (just scales the magnitude), while smaller LR scales all updates uniformly. Clipping is better for handling occasional large gradients without slowing down normal training steps. Putting It Together: HuggingFace Optimizer Configuration The following snippet shows how the concepts from this sectionAdamW with decoupled weight decay (1.6.6), cosine learning-rate scheduling with linear warmup (1.6.7), and gradient clipping (1.6.8)come together in practice using the HuggingFace transformers library. from transformers import TrainingArguments , Trainer from transformers import _ i _ e e _ h _ m import torch # --- Option 1: Using TrainingArguments ( recommended ) --- training_args = Train ingArguments ( output_dir = " ./ checkpoints " , # AdamW optimizer ( decoupled weight decay , S1 .6.6) optim = " adamw_torch " , 62 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems learning_rate =2 -5 , adam_beta1 =0.9 , adam_beta2 =0.999 , adam_epsilon =1 -8 , weight_decay =0.01 , # peak LR after warmup # first moment decay # second moment decay # numerical stability # decoupled L2 penalty # Learning rate schedule ( S1 .6.7) lr_s cheduler_ty pe = " cosine " , warmup_ratio =0.1 , # cosine decay to 0 # 10% of steps = linear warmup # Gradient clipping ( S1 .6.8) max_grad_norm =1.0 , # clip by global L2 norm # Mixed precision ( S1 .6.9) bf16 = True , # use BFloat16 on Ampere + GPUs # Training duration num_train_epochs =3 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =8 , d t _ u a n _ p =4 , ) trainer = Trainer ( model = model , args = training_args , train_dataset = dataset , ) trainer . train () # effective batch = 8*4 = # --- Option 2: Manual control ( for custom training loops ) --- from torch . optim import AdamW # Separate weight - decay groups ( don regularize biases / norms ) no_decay = [ " bias " , " LayerNorm . weight " , " layernorm . weight " ] param_groups = [ { } , { } , ] " params " : [ for , in model . named_parameters () if not any ( nd in for nd in no_decay ) ] , " weight_decay " : 0.01 , " params " : [ for , in model . named_parameters () if any ( nd in for nd in no_decay ) ] , " weight_decay " : 0.0 , optimizer = AdamW ( param_groups , lr =2 -5 , betas =(0.9 , 0.999) ) # Cosine schedule with linear warmup total_steps = len ( train_dataloader ) * num_epochs warmup_steps = int (0.1 * total_steps ) scheduler = _ i _ e e _ h _ m ( optimizer , num_warmup_steps = warmup_steps , num_training_s te ps = total_steps , ) # Training loop with gradient clipping for batch in train_dataloader : outputs = model (** batch ) loss = outputs . loss loss . backward () # Clip gradients before optimizer step torch . nn . utils . clip_grad_norm_ ( model . parameters () , max_norm =1.0) 63 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems optimizer . step () scheduler . step () optimizer . zero_grad () Listing 1.4: Complete optimizer configuration combining AdamW cosine schedule and gradient clipping. Practical Tips Weight decay exclusion: bias terms and layer-norm weights should not be regularized they have few parameters and regularizing them hurts performance [80]. Warmup ratio: 510% of total steps is standard; too little warmup with high LR can destabilize early training. Gradient accumulation: simulates larger batches on limited GPU memory; clipping applies to the accumulated gradient. BF16 vs. FP16: prefer bf16=True on Ampere+ GPUs (wider dynamic range avoids loss scaling); fall back to fp16=True on older hardware.

sections.title: 1.5.9 Mixed Precision Training
sections.content: BF16 vs. FP

sections.title: Dynamic range
sections.content: FP32 BF16 FP16 8 8 5 23 7 1038 to 1038 Same as FP32 (same exponent) 6 105 to 65504 BF16 Over FP16: Why Range Beats Precision in LLM Training BF16 has the same exponent range as FP32, so it can represent the same range of values (just with less precision in the mantissa). FP16 has much smaller dynamic range gradients or activations that exceed 65504 cause overflow (NaN/Inf). This is why FP16 training requires loss scaling (multiplying the loss by large constant to keep gradients in FP16 range), while BF16 training typically does not. A100 and H100 support BF16 natively; use BF16 unless you have specific reason for FP16. Loss Scaling (FP16 only). 1. Multiply loss by scale factor (e.g., = 215) 2. Compute gradients in FP16 (scaled by S) 3. Before optimizer step, divide gradients by 4. Check for overflow (NaN/Inf); if found, skip step and reduce 5. If no overflow for consecutive steps, increase FP32 Master Weights. In mixed precision training, weights are stored in FP32 (master copy) and cast to BF16/FP16 for the forward/backward pass. The optimizer step is done in FP32. This is important because: Small gradient updates (θ θ) would be lost in BF16 precision (7 mantissa bits 0.8% relative precision) 64 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems FP32 master weights ensure accurate accumulation of small updates over many steps Memory cost: 2 weight storage (FP32 + BF16 copy) When FP32 Master Weights Are Critical FP32 master weights are most important for: Long training runs (many small gradient steps accumulate) Small learning rates (updates are tiny relative to weight magnitude) For short SFT runs with large LR, BF16-only training (no FP32 master weights) often works fine and saves memory. For RL training, FP32 master weights are essentialsee 11.15. Mixed Precision in Practice: HuggingFace # === HuggingFace TrainingArguments ( simplest approach ) === from transformers import TrainingArguments # BF16 on Ampere + GPUs ( A100 , H100 , RTX 30 xx /40 xx ) args_bf16 = Train ingArguments ( output_dir = " ./ out " , # BF16 forward / backward ; FP32 master weights bf16 = True , bf16_full_eval = True , # also use BF16 during evaluation # No loss scaling needed -- BF16 has FP32 - equivalent range ) # FP16 on older GPUs ( V100 , T4 , RTX 20 xx ) args_fp16 = Train ingArguments ( output_dir = " ./ out " , fp16 = True , fp16_full_eval = False , # Loss scaling is automatic via PyTorch GradScaler # FP16 forward / backward # keep eval in FP32 for accuracy ) # === Manual PyTorch AMP ( for custom training loops ) === import torch # Setup ( PyTorch 2. API ) use_fp16 = not torch . cuda . is_bf16_supported () scaler = torch . amp . GradScaler ( " cuda " , enabled = use_fp16 ) optimizer = torch . optim . AdamW ( model . parameters () , lr =2 -5) dtype = torch . float16 if use_fp16 else torch . bfloat16 # only needed for FP16 for batch in train_dataloader : optimizer . zero_grad () # Autocast : run forward pass in reduced precision with torch . autocast ( " cuda " , dtype = dtype ) : outputs = model (** batch ) loss = outputs . loss if use_fp16 : # FP16 path : scale loss to prevent gradient underflow scaler . scale ( loss ) . backward () scaler . unscale_ ( optimizer ) torch . nn . utils . clip_grad_norm_ ( model . parameters () , 1.0) scaler . step ( optimizer ) scaler . update () # skips step on overflow # adjust scale factor # unscale before clipping else : # BF16 path : no scaling needed loss . backward () torch . nn . utils . clip_grad_norm_ ( model . parameters () , 1.0) 65 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems optimizer . step () scheduler . step () Listing 1.5: Mixed precision training with HuggingFace and manual PyTorch AMP. Key Differences: BF16 vs. FP16 in Code BF16: just wrap with autocast(dtype=torch.bfloat16)no scaler needed. Simpler code and more numerically stable. FP16: requires GradScaler to prevent gradient underflow. The scaler dynamically adjusts multiplier; if overflow is detected (NaN), the optimizer step is skipped and the scale is reduced. Gradient clipping + FP16: you must call scaler.unscale_(optimizer) before clip_grad_norm_, otherwise youre clipping scaled gradients (wrong threshold). Memory savings: % reduction in activation memory (activations stored in 16-bit); weight memory depends on whether you keep FP32 master copies.

sections.title: Diagnosing Training Instability
sections.content: # Monitor these metrics to diagnose optimizer issues : # 1. Gradient norm -- should be < max_grad_norm most of the time # 2. Loss scale ( FP16 ) -- should be stable , not constantly decreasing # 3. Parameter update norm -- should be << parameter norm import torch def og_opti ize r_stat ( model , optimizer , step ) : # Gradient norm ( before clipping ) total_norm = 0.0 for in model . parameters () : if . grad is not None : total_norm += . grad . data . norm (2) . item () ** total_norm = total_norm ** 0.5 # Adam second moment stats ( proxy for adaptive LR ) v_norms = [] for group in optimizer . param_groups : for in group [ params ]: state = optimizer . state [ ] if exp_avg_sq in state : v_norms . append ( state [ exp_avg_sq ]. mean () . item () ) print ( " Step { step }: grad_norm ={ total_norm :.3 } , " " mean_v ={ sum ( v_norms ) / len ( v_norms ) :.6 } " ) # Red flags : 66 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems # grad_norm >> 1.0 repeatedly -> reduce LR or increase warmup # grad_norm == 0.0 -> gradient vanishing or wrong loss # loss_scale decreasing -> FP16 overflow , switch to BF16 # very small -> Adam not warmed up yet , extend warmup The Learning Rate is the Most Important Hyperparameter In practice, getting the learning rate right matters more than any other hyperparameter. rule of thumb for LLM fine-tuning: Start with the values in the table above If loss diverges (increases after initial decrease): LR is too high If loss decreases very slowly and plateaus early: LR is too low If loss is unstable (oscillates): LR is too high or warmup is too short The second most important hyperparameter is batch size (affects gradient noise and effective LR via the linear scaling rule). Everything else is secondary.

sections.title: 1.6 Flash Attention – Algorithm and Hardware Awareness
sections.content: Flash Attention [7, 82] is one of the most impactful algorithmic innovations in deep learning since the transformer itself. It does not change the mathematical result of attention it computes exactly the same output but it restructures the memory access pattern so that the GPUs limited fast SRAM does all the heavy lifting, cutting HBM footprint from O(n2) to O(n) and delivering 24 end-to-end wall-clock gains on typical workloads.

sections.title: 1.6.1 The Standard Attention Memory Problem
sections.content: Standard scaled dot-product attention is: Attention(Q, K, ) = softmax ! QKT dk

sections.title: Standard Attention Memory Complexity
sections.content: For sequence length and head dimension d: Q, K, Rnd: O(nd) memory = QKT Rnn: O(n2) memory the bottleneck = softmax(S) Rnn: another O(n2) = Rnd: O(nd) At = 8192, = 128, BF16: the attention matrix alone is 81922 2 134 MB per head. With 32 heads, that is 4.3 GB just for one layers attention scores. Why O(n2) is Catastrophic The attention matrix must be written to HBM (it does not fit in SRAM for long sequences), then read back for the softmax, then read again for the product. Each of these HBM round-trips is slow. For = 32768 (32K context), the attention matrix is 327682 2 2 GB per head completely infeasible to store. 67 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 1.6.2 The Flash Attention Key Insight – Tiling and Online Softmax
sections.content: The core insight is: we never need the full matrix in memory at once. We can compute the output block-by-block if we use the online softmax trick. Online Softmax. Recall that softmax requires global maximum for numerical stability: softmax(xi) = exim exj , = max xj The trick: we can update the running maximum and normalization factor as we process new blocks, without ever materializing the full row. Online Softmax Update Rule Given running state (mold, ℓold, Oold) and new block of scores snew: 1. mnew = max(mold, max(snew)) 2. ℓnew = emoldmnew ℓold + _jes_new,jm_new 3. Onew = 1 ℓnew (cid:16) em_oldm_new ℓ_old O_old + es_newm_new _new (cid:17) This is mathematically equivalent to computing softmax over all blocks at once.

sections.title: 1.6.3 The Flash Attention Algorithm
sections.content: Flash Attention Forward Pass Block Tiling Setup: SRAM size . Block sizes Br = M/(4d), Bc = min(M/(4d), d). 1. Divide into Tr = n/Br blocks Q1, . . . , QTr 2. Divide K, into Tc = n/Bc blocks K1, . . . , KTc 3. Initialize output Rnd, running max Rn, running sum ℓ Rn (all in HBM) 4. Outer loop over = 1, . . . , Tc: (a) Load Kj, Vj from HBM to SRAM (b) Inner loop over = 1, . . . , Tr: i. Load Qi, Oi, mi, ℓi from HBM to SRAM ii. Compute Sij = QiKT iii. Apply online softmax update to get new mi, ℓi, Oi iv. Write Oi, mi, ℓi back to HBM (stays in SRAM) / 5. Return Key: Sij (the attention tile) is computed and discarded in SRAM. It is never written to HBM.

sections.title: Flash Attention
sections.content: O(n2) Memory (HBM) HBM reads/writes O(n2d) O(n2d) FLOPs 1 Speedup O(n) O(n2d/M ) O(n2d) (same) 24 68 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems In the forward pass, the total FLOPs remain O(n2d) identical to standard attention. Flash Attention gains speed entirely by slashing slow HBM traffic, not by reducing arithmetic. (The backward pass actually performs more FLOPs due to recomputation, but the wall-clock time is still lower because the saved memory bandwidth dominates.)

sections.title: 1.6.4 Flash Attention 2 – Better Parallelism
sections.content: Flash Attention 2 [82] made three key improvements: 1. Reduced non-matmul FLOPs: The original FA had unnecessary rescaling operations in the inner loop. FA2 restructures the loop to minimize these. On A100, Tensor Core matrix multiplications outpace scalar operations by roughly 16, so even small fraction of non-matmul work in the inner loop becomes the latency bottleneck. 2. Better parallelism across sequence dimension: FA1 parallelized over batch and heads only. FA2 also parallelizes over the query sequence dimension, enabling better GPU utilization for long sequences with small batch sizes. 3. Causal masking optimization: For autoregressive (causal) attention, roughly half the blocks are fully masked. FA2 skips these blocks entirely, giving 2 speedup for causal attention vs. bidirectional.

sections.title: 1.6.5 Flash Attention 3 – Hopper Architecture
sections.content: Flash Attention 3 [83] is designed specifically for H100 and exploits three Hopper-specific features: TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator): H100 has dedicated hardware unit for asynchronous bulk data movement between HBM and SRAM. FA3 uses TMA to overlap data loading with computation, hiding memory latency. Warp-specialization: FA3 assigns different warps to different roles (producer warps load data via TMA; consumer warps compute MMA). This is software pipelining technique that keeps both the memory system and Tensor Cores busy simultaneously. FP8 support: H100 supports FP8 (E4M3/E5M2) Tensor Core operations at 2 the throughput of BF16. FA3 supports FP8 attention with per-block quantization to maintain accuracy. FA3 achieves up to 75% of H100 theoretical peak for FP16 attention, compared to 35% for FA2.

sections.title: 1.6.6 Flash Attention 4 – Blackwell Architecture
sections.content: Flash Attention 4 [84] targets NVIDIAs Blackwell GPUs (B200/GB200), which double Tensor Core throughput to 2.25 PFLOP/s (BF16) while non-matmul units (exponential, shared memory bandwidth) scale at slower rate. This asymmetric hardware scaling means that the bottleneck shifts: on Blackwell, attention is limited not by matmul but by the softmax exponentials and shared memory traffic surrounding them. FA4 addresses this with four key techniques: Fully asynchronous MMA pipelines: Blackwells MMA instructions are fully asynchronous (unlike Hoppers wgmma which still blocked on completion). FA4 redesigns the pipeline to overlap MMA, TMA loads, and softmax rescaling across larger tile sizes, keeping all hardware units saturated. Software-emulated exponential: Instead of calling the hardware ex2 unit (which is the throughput bottleneck), FA4 emulates ex using polynomial approximations executed on the much faster Tensor Cores themselves. This trades extra matmul instructions for exponential-unit stalls. 69 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Conditional softmax rescaling: Standard FlashAttention rescales the running max every tile. FA4 skips the rescaling when the new tiles max does not exceed the running max (common in practice), saving both register shuffles and synchronization barriers. Tensor Memory + 2-CTA MMA mode (backward pass): The backward pass uses Blackwells Tensor Memory (a per-SM scratchpad larger than shared memory) and 2-CTA cooperative mode that fuses dQ accumulation across two thread-block clusters, halving shared memory round-trips. FA4 Implementation: CuTe-DSL FA4 is the first FlashAttention version written in CuTe-DSL, Python-embedded domain-specific language for GPU kernels (part of CUTLASS 4.x). CuTe-DSL compiles 2030 faster than C++ CUTLASS templates while retaining full control over register allocation and pipeline scheduling. This dramatically lowers the iteration time for kernel development. Results. On B200 with BF16 head-dim 128 (causal, seq-len 8K): 1613 TFLOP/s 71% of Blackwell peak utilization 1.3 faster than cuDNN 9.13 (NVIDIAs proprietary fused kernel) 2.7 faster than Triton on the same hardware HardwareSoftware Co-evolution The FlashAttention series illustrates key principle: each GPU generation shifts the bottleneck, demanding new algorithmic ideas rather than just re-compilation. A80 memory bandwidth limited (FA1/FA2: tiling + recomputation). H100 data movement limited (FA3: TMA + warpspecialization). B200 non-matmul compute limited (FA4: software-emulated exp + conditional rescaling). Understanding where the hardware bottleneck lies is the prerequisite for writing efficient kernels.

sections.title: 1.7 Pretraining: Best Practices
sections.content: Pretraining is the most expensive phase of LLM developmentconsuming millions of GPU-hours and requiring careful orchestration of data, compute, and hyperparameters. This section distills key lessons from Llama-3 [25], Chinchilla [85], and GPT-4 [23].

sections.title: 1.7.1 Training Objective
sections.content: All modern decoder-only LLMs use causal language modeling (CLM): LCLM =

sections.title:
1
T
sections.content: T t=1 log Pθ(xt x<t) This simple objectivewith enough data and scaleproduces emergent capabilities (in-context learning, reasoning, instruction following) without explicit supervision [21].

sections.title: Pretraining Data Recipe
sections.content: Scale: 115 trillion tokens for frontier models (Llama-3: 15T tokens) Sources: Web crawl (80%), code (10%), books/papers (5%), curated (5%) Deduplication: MinHash + exact substring dedup reduces memorization [86] 70 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Quality filtering: Perplexity-based classifier, heuristic filters (length, language ID, toxicity) Data mixing: Temperature-weighted sampling across domains; upweight code and math for reasoning

sections.title: 1.7.3 Scaling Laws
sections.content: Hoffmann et al. [85] showed that compute-optimal training requires balancing model size and data size D: Nopt C0.50, Dopt C0.50. 70B model is compute-optimal at 1.4T tokens. In practice, models are over-trained (more tokens than Chinchilla-optimal) because inference cost scales with model size, not training tokenssmaller over-trained models are cheaper to deploy.

sections.title: 1.7.4 Key Hyperparameters
sections.content: Table 1.10: Pretraining hyperparameters from published models. Setting Llama-3 405B Tokens Batch size (tokens) Peak LR Schedule Weight decay Context length 15T 16M 8e-5 WSD 0.1 8192 Llama-3 8B Qwen-2.5 72B Mistral 7B 15T 4M 3e-4 WSD 0.1 8192 18T 4M 3e-4 Cosine 0.1 409632K 8T 4M 3e-4 Cosine 0.

sections.title: Pretraining Pitfalls
sections.content: Loss spikes: Sudden loss increases from bad data batches or numerical instability. Llama-3 reports rolling back to checkpoints and skipping offending batches. Memorization: Model regurgitates training data verbatim. Fix: deduplicate aggressively; monitor extraction attacks. Context length: Training on short sequences then deploying at long context fails. Use continued pretraining on long documents + RoPE scaling.

sections.title: 1.8 Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
sections.content: SFT transforms pretrained language model into an instruction-following assistant by training on curated promptresponse pairs. This is the bridge between raw language modeling and RLHF.

sections.title: 1.8.1 SFT Objective
sections.content: The loss is identical to CLM, but computed only on response tokens: LSFT = 1 y t=1 log Pθ(yt xprompt, y<t) Prompt tokens provide context but receive no gradient (labels set to 100).

sections.title: 1.8.2 Data Quality: The LIMA Principle
sections.content: Zhou et al. [87] demonstrated that 1,000 carefully curated examples can match models trained on 50K+ noisy examples. Key requirements: 71 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Diversity: Cover QA, summarization, code, math, creative writing, multi-turn dialogue Correctness: Every response must be factually accurate and well-formatted Length balance: Mix short (1-sentence) and long (multi-paragraph) responses Decontamination: Remove overlap with evaluation benchmarks

sections.title: 1.8.3 Training Configuration
sections.content: from trl import SFTTrainer , SFTConfig sft_config = SFTConfig ( # Pack short examples into full sequences output_dir = " ./ sft_output " , max_seq_length =4096 , packing = True , learning_rate =2 -5 , lr_s cheduler_ty pe = " cosine " , warmup_ratio =0.1 , weight_decay =0.01 , max_grad_norm =1.0 , num_train_epochs =3 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =4 , d t _ u a n _ p =8 , bf16 = True , gr ad ie nt _ ch ec kp oi n = True , ) trainer = SFTTrainer ( model = model , args = sft_config , train_dataset = dataset , processing_class = tokenizer ) trainer . train ()

sections.title: 1.8.4 Efficient Training Solutions
sections.content: Standard HuggingFace training leaves significant performance on the table. Several libraries provide drop-in efficiency gains for SFT workloads: Liger Kernel [88]. An open-source set of Triton-fused kernels from LinkedIn that replace standard PyTorch operators during training. Key fusions include: Fused Cross-Entropy: Merges the final linear projection, softmax, and loss computation into single kernelavoids materializing the full (batch seq vocab) logit tensor. Fused RMSNorm / SwiGLU / RoPE: Eliminates intermediate memory allocations for common LLM building blocks. Chunked operations: Processes large tensors in tiles to keep peak memory bounded. Result: 20% higher throughput and up to 60% memory reduction with one-line integration (apply_liger_kernel_to_llama()). Compatible with FSDP, DeepSpeed, and LoRA. Unsloth [89]. specialized fine-tuning library that combines custom CUDA/Triton kernels with aggressive memory optimization: Manual backpropagation through LoRA layers (avoids autograd overhead). 4-bit QLoRA with fused dequantizationtrains 70B models on single 48 GB GPU. Intelligent RoPE and attention kernel fusion specific to each architecture (Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma). Result: 25 faster than vanilla HuggingFace + PEFT, with 6070% less VRAM. Particularly impactful for single-GPU and consumer-hardware workflows. 72 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems torchtune [90]. Metas native PyTorch fine-tuning library (development wound down in 2025), designed around composability rather than monolithic abstractions: Pure PyTorchno trainer class; recipes are readable single-file scripts. Native integration with torch.compile, FSDP2, and activation checkpointing. First-class support for QLoRA, full fine-tuning, and knowledge distillation. Built-in quantization-aware training (QAT) for post-training compression. Result: Comparable speed to custom solutions but with full debuggability and no framework lock-in. Choosing an Efficiency Stack Quick LoRA/QLoRA on 1 GPU: Unsloth (fastest time-to-train, minimal setup) Multi-GPU full fine-tune: TRL/DeepSpeed + Liger Kernel (best throughput at scale) Research / custom training loops: torchtune (transparent, hackable, native PyTorch) These are complementary: Liger kernels can be used inside both TRL and torchtune workflows.

sections.title: 1.8.5 Best Practices
sections.content: Table 1.11: SFT training guidelines.

sections.title: Packing
sections.content: Concatenate multiple short examples into one sequence (separated by EOS). Avoids padding waste. NEFTune [91] Add uniform noise to embeddings (α = 5). Improves MT-Bench by 515% at zero cost. Chat template Always use the models native template. Mismatched templates

sections.title: Epochs
sections.content: degrade quality. 23 for large datasets; up to 5 for small (<10K) curated sets. Overtraining causes format memorization.

sections.title: SFT Is Not Enough
sections.content: SFT teaches format and basic instruction following, but cannot reliably teach: preference (which response is betterneeds RLHF/DPO), refusal (when not to answerneeds safety training), calibration (saying dont knowneeds RL with truthfulness rewards), or complex reasoning (multi-step chainsneeds RL with verifiable rewards). The full pipeline is: Pretrain SFT RLHF/DPO.

sections.title: 1.9 LoRA and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
sections.content: Full fine-tuning of 70B model requires storing 70B trainable parameters plus their optimizer states (560+ GB of memory). LoRA [92] (Low-Rank Adaptation) provides way to fine-tune with <1% of the parameters while achieving comparable quality.

sections.title: 1.9.1 The LoRA Insight
sections.content: LoRA Core Idea Instead of updating full weight matrix Rdd, learn low-rank perturbation: = + α BA, Rdr, Rrd 73 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems is frozen (no gradients, no optimizer states) Only and are trained: 2 parameters instead of d2 At rank = 16, = 4096: LoRA adds 2 4096 16 = 131K params per layer vs. 16.8M for full matrix α/r scaling controls the magnitude of the update Why Low-Rank Works Aghajanyan et al. [93] showed that fine-tuning operates in very low-dimensional subspace the intrinsic dimensionality of the fine-tuning task is much smaller than the models parameter count. 175B models fine-tuning task may have intrinsic dimensionality <10,000. LoRA exploits this directly: rank constrains the update to an r-dimensional subspace per weight matrix. Figure 1.10: LoRA decomposes the weight update into two small matrices A. The original weight remains frozen; only and receive gradients. At inference, the product BA can be merged into with zero overhead. Why the α/r Scaling Matters Without scaling, doubling the rank would roughly double the magnitude of = BA (more columns in contribute to the sum). This means changing rank would also change how much the model is perturbedyoud need to re-tune the learning rate every time you adjust r. The α/r factor normalizes the update magnitude so that it stays approximately constant regardless of rank: = + BA α Fix α, sweep r: The effective update magnitude stays α regardless of rank. You can try {8, 16, 32, 64} without re-tuning LR. Common practice: Set α = (so α/r = 1) or α = 2r (so α/r = 2). This is convenient default where the scaling factor is small integer. Why not just tune LR? You could, but α/r provides rank-independent knob. Teams can share LR recipes across experiments with different ranks. rsLoRA insight [94]: At high ranks (r 64), empirical evidence shows α/ is more r, not r. stable than α/r, because the variance of BA scales with 74 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 1.9.2 LoRA Hyperparameters
sections.content: Choosing LoRA hyperparameters correctly is critical the wrong rank or alpha can either under-fit (too constrained) or waste memory (too expressive). Hyperparameter Typical Values Guidance Table 1.12: LoRA hyperparameter guide. (rank) 8, 16, 32, lora_alpha 16, 32 (often = or 2r) target_modules q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj lora_dropout 0.00. bias "none" Learning rate 1e-4 to 3e-4 Higher = more capacity but more memory. Start with 16. Controls update magnitude via α/r scaling. All attention projections. Add gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj for full coverage. Regularization. Usually 0.05 for small datasets. Training biases adds minimal params but rarely helps. Higher adapters update). than full fine-tuning (only

sections.title: Rank Selection Rules of Thumb
sections.content: r=8: Simple tasks (single-domain chat, classification). Very memory-efficient. r=16: General-purpose fine-tuning. Good default. r=3264: Complex tasks (math, code, multi-turn reasoning). Approaches full fine-tune quality. r=128+: Diminishing returns; consider full fine-tuning or QLoRA with higher rank. Key indicator: If training loss plateaus well above full fine-tune loss, increase rank.

sections.title: 1.9.3 LoRA Variants
sections.content: Table 1.13: LoRA variants and their innovations.

sections.title: When to Use
sections.content: DoRA [96] QLoRA [95] 4-bit quantized base + LoRA in BF16. NF4 data type + double quantization. Decomposes into magnitude and direction; LoRA updates direction only. Different LRs for A/B (ηB = ληA, λ 16). AdaLoRA [98] Dynamic rank budget across imporLoRA+ [97] (SVD-based rsLoRA [94] VeRA [99] LoRA-FA instead of α/r. layers tance). Scales by α/ Stable at high ranks. Shared frozen random A, B; trains diagonal scaling only. Freezes after init; only trains B. Halves LoRA memory. Fine-tune 70B on single 48GB GPU. Better generalization for reasoning. Free 2% gain; no extra cost. Very tight compute budget. When using 64. Extreme param efficiency. Memory-constrained scenarios. 75 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Key Extensions Explained DoRA Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation. DoRA [96] observes that full finetuning tends to change the direction of weight vectors more than their magnitude. Standard LoRA conflates both. DoRA decomposes each weight column into magnitude = col and direction ˆV = W/W col, then applies LoRA only to the direction: = ˆV , ˆV = + BA + BAcol Magnitude is separate learnable vector (one scalar per column). This consistently outperforms LoRA by 13% on reasoning and instruction-following benchmarks with no additional inference cost (merged at deployment). 76 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems LoRA+ Asymmetric Learning Rates. Hayou et al. [97] show that matrices and in LoRA have different optimal learning rates. Since is initialized to zero, it starts in very different regime than (initialized from (0, σ2)). Setting ηB 16 ηA improves convergence speed and final quality by 2% free gain requiring only one-line config change: # LoRA + in PEFT : set different LRs per matrix i e _ u _ a e = [ { " params " : [ for , in model . named_parameters () if " lora_B " in ] , " lr " : 2e -4 * 16} , # matrix : higher LR { " params " : [ for , in model . named_parameters () if " lora_A " in ] , " lr " : 2e -4} , # matrix : base LR ] VeRA Vector-based Random Matrix Adaptation. VeRA [99] takes parameter efficiency to the extreme: instead of learning and B, it freezes them as shared random matrices across all layers and only trains two diagonal scaling vectors db Rr and da Rd: = diag(db) diag(da) This reduces trainable parameters by 10 vs. LoRA (only + params per layer) while achieving 9095% of LoRA quality. Best for scenarios where you need hundreds of task-specific adapters with minimal storage.

sections.title: QLoRA Memory Savings
sections.content: 70B model full fine-tune: 140 GB (weights) + 280 GB (optimizer) + 140 GB (gradients) = 560 GB (7 A100-80GB). 70B QLoRA (r=16, all linear layers): Base model in NF4: 70B 0.5 = 35 GB LoRA adapters in BF16: 160 MB Optimizer states (only for adapters): 320 MB Activations (gradient checkpointing): 8 GB Total: 44 GB fits in single 48GB GPU! # QLoRA configuration with PEFT from peft import LoraConfig , get_peft_model , p _ e _ _ t _ i g from transformers import BitsAndBytesConfig import torch # 4 - bit quantization config bnb_config = BitsAndB ytesConfig ( load_in_4bit = True , bnb_4bit _q uan _t pe = " nf4 " , bn b_ 4b it _ co mp ut e_ p = torch . bfloat16 , # Compute in BF16 _ 4 _ _ do e _ n = True , # NormalFloat4 - optimal for weights # Quantize the quantization constants ) # LoRA config lora_config = LoraConfig ( =16 , lora_alpha =32 , target_modules =[ " q_proj " , " k_proj " , " v_proj " , " o_proj " , # alpha / = 2 scaling " gate_proj " , " up_proj " , " down_proj " ] , lora_dropout =0.05 , bias = " none " , task_type = " CAUSAL_LM " , ) 77 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems model = p _ e _ _ t _ i g ( model ) model = get_peft_model ( model , lora_config ) model . n _ i l _ a e () # Output : trainable params : 83 ,886 ,080 all params : 70 ,553 ,706 ,496 0.12% # Prepare for QLoRA # Add LoRA adapters

sections.title: 1.9.4 Other PEFT Approaches
sections.content: LoRA dominates modern practice, but it is not the only parameter-efficient method. For completeness, the main alternatives: Table 1.14: PEFT method families. LoRA is the de facto standard for LLM fine-tuning; the others are included for historical context and niche use cases. Method Mechanism Pros / Cons Status LoRA [92] (and variants) Low-rank matrices existing added to weights Adapters [100] Prefix Tuning [101] Prompt Tuning [102] bottleneck inserted beSmall MLPs tween layers Learnable virtual tokens prepended to keys/values at each layer Learnable soft prompt embeddings prepended to input IA3 [103] BitFit [104] Learned vectors that rescale keys, values, and FFN activations Train only bias terms Near-zero Standard Rarely used

sections.title: Historical
sections.content: Mergeable at inference (zero overhead); well-supported; works for all architectures Modular; stackable; adds inference se- (extra latency quential layers) No weight modification; effective for genconeration tasks; sumes context length few Extremely (<0.01%); params weaker than LoRA for complex tasks Even fewer params than LoRA; mergeable; limited capacity params; surprisingly effective for simple tasks; limited expressiveness

sections.title: Why LoRA Won
sections.content: LoRA became the standard because it uniquely combines: (1) zero inference overhead adapters merge into base weights, unlike Adapters or Prefix Tuning which add latency or consume context; (2) composability multiple LoRA adapters can be swapped at serving time for multi-tenant deployments; (3) ecosystem support HuggingFace PEFT, TRL, vLLM, and all major frameworks have first-class LoRA support; (4) proven at scale used in production by Meta, Google, and most open-source fine-tunes on HuggingFace. Unless you have specific constraint that LoRA cannot satisfy, it should be your default choice. 78 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 1.10 Mixture of Experts (MoE)
sections.content: Mixture of Experts models [105, 106] scale model capacity without proportionally scaling compute cost by activating only subset of parameters for each token.

sections.title: 1.10.1 Architecture
sections.content: MoE Layer In MoE transformer, the FFN layer in each block is replaced by parallel expert FFNs plus router that selects which experts to use: MoE(x) = i=1 gi(x) Ei(x), g(x) = TopK(softmax(Wrx)) Ei are expert networks (standard FFN layers) gi(x) are gating weights from the router (only top-K are non-zero) Typically = 2 out of = 864 experts are active per token Total params scale with ; active params scale with K/N of FFN size Figure 1.11: MoE layer with 8 experts and Top-2 routing. Only the two highest-gated experts are computed per token; the rest are skipped entirely.

sections.title: The Load Balancing Problem
sections.content: Without constraints, the router may send most tokens to the same 12 experts (expert collapse). This wastes capacity and creates compute imbalance across GPUs (each expert typically lives on different GPU). Solution: Add an auxiliary load-balancing loss: Lbal = α i=1 fi pi where fi = fraction of tokens routed to expert i, pi = mean router probability for expert i. This encourages uniform expert utilization. 79 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 1.10.3 Noisy Top-K Gating: Making Discrete Routing Trainable
sections.content: The core challenge in MoE is that top-k selection is not differentiable you cant backpropagate through hard pick the top 2 operation. The field has developed two key tricks to solve this: The Routing Differentiability Problem The router computes logits h(x) = Wr for each expert, then selects the top-k. But: The selected experts get gradients through their gate weights (softmax over selected) The selection decision itself (which to pick) has zero gradient Without trick, the router can get stuck: an expert never selected never gets gradient signal never gets selected Approach 1: Noisy Top-K Gating [105]. Add learnable Gaussian noise to the router logits before the top-k selection: h(x) = Wg H(x) = h(x) + ϵ Softplus(Wnoise x), ϵ (0, 1) TopK(v, k)i = if vi is in the top (vi otherwise g(x) = softmax(cid:0)TopK(H(x), k)(cid:1) (clean logits) (noisy logits) (1.11) (sparse gates) Wnoise is learned noise magnitude the model learns how much exploration each expert needs During training, noise occasionally promotes underdog experts into the top-k, giving them gradient signal At inference, noise is removed: use clean logits h(x) for deterministic routing The Softplus ensures noise scale is always positive Approach 2: Gumbel-Softmax Trick (for differentiable discrete sampling). An alternative from the variational inference literature [107]. The Gumbel-Max trick provides exact sampling from categorical distribution: = arg max [log πi + Gi] , Gi Gumbel(0, 1) (1.12) where Gumbel noise is generated as Gi = log( log(Ui)), Ui Uniform(0, 1). For top-k routing: taking the top-k of (log πi + Gi) gives samples without replacement from the categorical distribution defined by π. Since arg max is non-differentiable, the Gumbel-Softmax relaxation replaces it with temperaturecontrolled softmax: ˆgi = exp ((log πi + Gi)/τ ) exp ((log πj + Gj)/τ ) (1.13) τ 0: approaches hard one-hot (exact but non-differentiable) τ : approaches uniform (differentiable but uninformative) In practice, anneal τ from 1.0 down to 0.10.5 during training Straight-through estimator: use hard top-k in the forward pass, but Gumbel-Softmax gradients in the backward pass best of both worlds H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Which Approach Is Used in Practice? Sparsely-Gated MoE [105], Mixtral [106], DeepSeek-V2 [108]: Use Noisy Top-K with Gaussian noise. Simple, effective, well-proven at scale. Switch Transformer [109]: Simplified to Top-1 with no noise (relies on load-balancing loss alone). Research / smaller-scale MoE: Some use Gumbel-Softmax for fully differentiable routing, especially when learning the routing itself is the research objective. Key insight: Both approaches solve the same problem (making discrete selection trainable) via noise injection. Gaussian noise is simpler; Gumbel noise has stronger theoretical guarantees for categorical sampling.
