sections.content: 200500 5060% 1632 Time-to-first-token 100300ms 5002000ms Continuous batching + PagedAttention Zero fragmentation, dynamic block alloc Paged KV eliminates per-seq reservation Prefix caching for repeated system prompts 118 Chapter

sections.title:
Introduction to Reinforcement
Learning
sections.content: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is paradigm where an agent learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an environment, receiving rewards as feedback, and optimizing its policy to maximize cumulative reward over time [158]. Unlike supervised learning (which requires labeled input-output pairs), RL discovers optimal behavior through trial and error. Figure 3.1: Reinforcement Learning overview: an agent interacts with an environment, receiving rewards as feedback and updating its policy through trial and error. Unlike supervised learning which learns from labeled pairs, RL learns what to do by maximizing reward through experience.

sections.title: 3.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP)
sections.content: An MDP is 5-tuple (S, A, P, R, γ): S: State space all possible configurations of the environment A: Action space all actions available to the agent (ss, a): Transition function probability of reaching state from state after taking action R(s, a, s): Reward function immediate scalar feedback for transition γ [0, 1]: Discount factor how much future rewards are valued relative to immediate ones The Markov Property: The future depends only on the current state, not the history: (st+1st, at, st1, at1, . . .) = (st+1st, at) This makes the problem tractable. 119 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Agent-Environment Interaction Loop At each time step t: 1. Agent observes state st 2. Agent selects action at according to policy π(as) 3. Environment transitions to st+1 (st, at) 4. Agent receives reward rt = R(st, at, st+1) 5. Repeat until terminal state or horizon T

sections.title: 3.2 Core Concepts and Definitions
sections.content: Policy π(as): mapping from states to action probabilities. Deterministic: = π(s). Stochastic: π(s). Return (cumulative discounted reward): Gt = k=0 γkrt+k = rt + γrt+1 + γ2rt+2 + Value Function (expected return from state under policy π): π(s) = Eπ [Gt st = s] = Eπ " # γkrt+k st = k=0 Action-Value Function (expected return from state s, taking action a, then following π): Qπ(s, a) = Eπ [Gt st = s, at = a] Advantage Function (how much better action is compared to average): Aπ(s, a) = Qπ(s, a) π(s) Bellman Equations (recursive relationship): π(s) = π(as) Qπ(s, a) = (ss, a) (ss, a) (cid:2)R(s, a, s) + γV π(s)(cid:3) " R(s, a, s) + γ π(as)Qπ(s, a) # Optimal Policy and Bellman Optimality The optimal policy π satisfies: (3.1) (3.2) (3.3) (3.4) (3.5) (3.6) (s) = max s (ss, a) (cid:2)R(s, a, s) + γV (s)(cid:3) Q(s, a) = (ss, a) (cid:20) R(s, a, s) + γ max (cid:21) Q(s, a) (3.7) (3.8) Once is known, the optimal policy is simply: π(s) = arg maxa Q(s, a). 120 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 3.3 Taxonomy of RL Methods
sections.content: Reinforcement learning algorithms can be classified along several axes. Understanding this taxonomy helps select the right approach for given problem. Key Taxonomy Distinctions Model-Free vs Model-Based: Model-Free: Learn policy or value function directly from experience. No knowledge of environment dynamics. Most practical for LLMs (language dynamics are intractable to model). Model-Based: Learn or use model of environment transitions (ss, a). Can plan ahead. More sample-efficient but requires accurate model. Value-Based vs Policy-Based: Value-Based: Learn Q(s, a) or (s), derive policy as arg maxa Q(s, a). Works well for discrete, small action spaces (e.g., Atari). Struggles with continuous/large action spaces. Policy-Based: Directly parameterize and optimize πθ(as). Natural for continuous/highdimensional action spaces. Essential for LLMs (vocabulary = 32K128K actions). Actor-Critic: Combine both policy (actor) proposes actions, value function (critic) evaluates them. PPO for LLMs is actor-critic. On-Policy vs Off-Policy: On-Policy: Learn from data generated by the current policy. Must regenerate data after each update. Examples: REINFORCE, PPO, A2C. More stable but less sample-efficient. Off-Policy: Learn from data generated by any policy (including old versions or other agents). Can reuse past experience. Examples: Q-Learning, DQN, SAC. More sample-efficient but harder to stabilize.

sections.title: 3.4 Temporal Difference (TD) Learning
sections.content: TD learning [159] bootstraps it updates value estimates using other value estimates, without waiting for the full episode to end.

sections.title: 3.4.1 Understanding TD Error: “Surprise” as a Learning Signal
sections.content: TD error measures the discrepancy between an agents current estimate of future reward and newly updated estimate after taking one step. Put simply, it is the difference between what the 121 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems agent thought would happen and what actually happened plus what it expects next. It represents the agents surprise. Intuition: The Driving Analogy Imagine you are driving home and expect the drive to take 30 minutes. The prediction: 30 minutes total. The reality shift: After 10 minutes, you hit unexpected road construction. Your GPS updates, saying you now have 35 minutes left. The TD Error: Total expected time is now 45 minutes (10 elapsed + 35 remaining). The difference between new estimate (45 min) and old estimate (30 min) is +15 minute TD error. You use this surprise to change your route next time. positive TD error means the outcome was better than expected boost this states value. negative TD error means it was worse than expected lower this states value.

sections.title: 3.4.2 The TD Error Formula
sections.content: δt = Rt+1 + γV (St+1) (St) (3.9) Rt+1: The immediate reward received after taking an action. γV (St+1): The estimated discounted value of the next state (what the agent expects to get from the next state onward, scaled by discount factor γ). (St): The original estimate of the current states value. The combined term (Rt+1 + γV (St+1)) is called the TD Target. Therefore: TD Error = TD Target Old Estimate (3.10)

sections.title: 3.4.3 How the Agent Uses TD Error
sections.content: The agent adjusts its value function to drive TD error toward zero: (St) (St) + α δt (3.11) If δt > 0: outcome was better than predicted increase (St) so the agent seeks this state. If δt < 0: outcome was worse than predicted decrease (St) so the agent avoids this state. If δt = 0: prediction was perfect no update needed (convergence). TD vs Monte Carlo Monte Carlo: Wait until episode ends, use actual return Gt. Unbiased but high variance (one full trajectory may be unrepresentative). TD: Update after every step using estimated future value γV (st+1). Biased (depends on accuracy) but much lower variance (one-step updates, doesnt compound noise). TD(λ): Interpolate between TD(0) and Monte Carlo. λ = 0: pure TD. λ = 1: pure MC. This is exactly what GAE does for PPO (with λ = 0.95). TD Target: yt = rt + γV (st+1) the better estimate we move toward. Multi-step TD (n-step returns): G(n) = rt + γrt+1 + + γn1rt+n1 + γnV (st+n) (3.12) H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 3.5 Q-Learning
sections.content: Q-Learning [160] is the foundational off-policy, value-based algorithm. It learns the optimal directly, regardless of the policy being followed. Update rule: Q(st, at) Q(st, at) + α (cid:20) rt + γ max Q(st+1, a) Q(st, at) (cid:21) (3.13) Why Q-Learning is Off-Policy The update uses maxa Q(st+1, a) the value of the best action at the next state, regardless of which action the agent actually took. This means the target is always computed under the optimal policy, even if the behavior policy explores randomly (ϵ-greedy). This is why Q-Learning can learn from replay buffers, demonstrations, or any source of experience. The data doesnt need to come from the current policy. SARSA [161] (on-policy alternative): Uses the action actually taken instead of the max: Q(st, at) Q(st, at) + α [rt + γQ(st+1, at+1) Q(st, at)] (3.14) Deep Q-Networks (DQN) [162]: Replace tabular Q(s, a) with neural network Qθ(s, a). Key innovations: experience replay buffer (off-policy data reuse), target network (stability), ϵ-greedy exploration. DQN Loss Function: The network is trained to minimize the mean squared TD error over mini-batches sampled from the replay buffer: L(θ) = E(s,a,r,s)B "(cid:18) + γ max Qθ(s, a) Qθ(s, a) (cid:19)2# (3.15) where Qθ is the target network frozen copy of Qθ updated only every steps (e.g., = 10,000). This prevents the moving target problem: without it, both the prediction and the target shift simultaneously, causing divergence. Gradient update: Taking the gradient of the loss w.r.t. θ (note: the target is treated as constant no gradient flows through θ): (cid:18) θL = + γ max (cid:19) Qθ(s, a) Qθ(s, a) {z TD error δ } θQθ(s, a) Learning scheme (per training step): θ θ α δ θQθ(s, a) (3.16) (3.17) 1. Act: Select action via ϵ-greedy: with probability ϵ take random action, otherwise = arg maxa Qθ(s, a). Anneal ϵ from 1.0 0.01 over first 1M steps. 2. Store: Save transition (s, a, r, s, d) in replay buffer (capacity 1M). 3. Sample: Draw mini-batch of 32 transitions uniformly from B. 4. Compute target: = + γ(1 d) maxa Qθ(s, a) (zero future value if terminal). 5. Update: Gradient descent on (y Qθ(s, a))2. Clip gradients to [1, 1] (Huber loss variant). 6. Sync target: Every steps, copy θ θ. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 3.5.1 Understanding Replay Buffers
sections.content: A replay buffer [163] (experience replay) is data storage mechanism that saves past experiences so an agent can relearn from them later. Instead of discarding data immediately after an action, the agent stores transitions in memory bank and samples random mini-batches for training. Whats stored: Each transition is tuple: et = (st, at, rt, st+1, dt) (3.18) where dt is boolean flag indicating if the episode ended. Why Replay Buffers Are Essential Breaks data correlation: Consecutive steps are highly correlated. Neural networks generalize poorly on sequential data. Random sampling from buffer makes training samples approximately i.i.d. Prevents catastrophic forgetting: Without buffer, an agent that passes difficult level might forget how to clear it while spending the next 10K steps failing on later level. The buffer ensures it continues to practice old scenarios. Improves sample efficiency: Running environments can be slow. replay buffer allows multiple weight updates from the same transition, extracting more value from every step. import random from collections import deque class ReplayBuffer : def __init__ ( self , capacity ) : self . buffer = deque ( maxlen = capacity ) # Bounded queue def push ( self , state , action , reward , next_state , done ) : self . buffer . append (( state , action , reward , next_state , done ) ) def sample ( self , batch_size ) : # Break correlation by selecting random experiences return random . sample ( self . buffer , batch_size ) def __len__ ( self ) : return len ( self . buffer ) Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) In standard buffer, all experiences have equal sampling probability. But some are much more educational. PER [164] scales sampling probability by TD error magnitude if transition caused massive surprise (high δt), the agent samples it more frequently to correct its model faster. This accelerates learning by 23 on Atari benchmarks. Why Q-Learning Fails for LLMs The action space in language generation is the full vocabulary (A = 32K128K), and the state space is all possible token sequences (infinite). Computing maxa Q(s, a) over 128K actions at every token position is intractable. This is why LLM RL uses policy-based methods (PPO, GRPO) instead. 124 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 3.6 Policy Gradient Methods — REINFORCE
sections.content: Instead of learning value function and deriving policy, directly optimize the policy parameters θ to maximize expected return [165]. Objective: J(θ) = Eτ πθ [R(τ )] = Eπθ Policy Gradient Theorem: hPT t=0 rt θJ(θ) = Eπθ θ log πθ(atst) Gt # " t=0 (3.19) Policy Gradient Theorem Formal Derivation (5 Steps) Step 1: Define the objective. We want to maximize expected return: J(θ) = Eτ πθ " # rt t=0 = τ (τ θ)R(τ ) where (τ θ) = p(s0) QT t=0 πθ(atst) p(st+1st, at) is the trajectory probability. Step 2: Take the gradient. Only πθ terms depend on θ (dynamics doesnt): θJ = θP (τ θ) R(τ ) τ Step 3: Apply the log-derivative trick: θP (τ θ) = (τ θ) θ log (τ θ): θJ = Eτ πθ [θ log (τ θ) R(τ )] Step 4: Expand log (τ θ). The log p(s0) and log p(st+1st, at) terms vanish under θ: θ log (τ θ) = t=0 θ log πθ(atst) Step 5: Combine. Future rewards dont depend on past actions (causality), so each log π pairs only with future return Gt = PT t=t rt: θJ = Eπθ " X t=0 θ log πθ(atst) Gt # Why This Is Beautiful The gradient does not require differentiating through the environment dynamics p(ss, a). The log-derivative trick converts it into an expectation we can estimate by simply running the policy and observing rewards. Replacing Gt with advantage ˆAt = Gt (st) reduces variance without bias (since E[ log π b(s)] = 0 for any state-dependent baseline). REINFORCE Algorithm [165] (Williams, 1992): 1. Sample complete trajectory τ = (s0, a0, r0, s1, a1, r1, . . .) under πθ 2. Compute return Gt = PT k=0 γkrt+k for each time step 3. Update: θ θ + α θ log πθ(atst) Gt REINFORCE Intuition Reward-Weighted Maximum Likelihood θ log πθ(atst) is the direction that increases the probability of action at. Multiplying by Gt means: 125 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems High-reward trajectories: increase probability of all actions taken (positive Gt) Low-reward trajectories: decrease probability of actions taken (negative Gt after baseline) Its supervised learning where the labels are the actions you took, weighted by how good they turned out to be. Variance Reduction with Baseline: θJ(θ) = Eπθ " # θ log πθ(atst) (Gt b(st)) t= (3.20) Any baseline b(st) that doesnt depend on at keeps the gradient unbiased but reduces variance. Best choice: b(st) = π(st). Then Gt (st) Aπ(st, at) = advantage. REINFORCE Limitations High variance: Each gradient uses one trajectory. Thousands of samples needed for stable updates. No bootstrapping: Must wait for full episode (no partial credit). Sample inefficient: Data is used once then discarded (on-policy). No step-size control: Can take catastrophically large policy steps. These limitations motivate the progression: REINFORCE Actor-Critic TRPO PPO.

sections.title: 3.7 Actor-Critic Methods
sections.content: Combine policy gradient (actor) with learned value function (critic) to reduce variance while maintaining the flexibility of policy optimization. Architecture: Actor πθ(as): The policy. Proposes actions. Critic Vϕ(s) or Qϕ(s, a): Evaluates how good state/action is. Provides low-variance baseline. Actor update (using advantage from critic): θJ = θ log πθ(atst) ˆAt , ˆAt = rt + γVϕ(st+1) Vϕ(st) Critic update (minimize TD error): Lcritic = (rt + γVϕ(st+1) Vϕ(st))2i (3.21) (3.22)

sections.title: Evolution to PPO for LLMs
sections.content: 1. REINFORCE [165]: High variance, no bootstrapping impractical for LLMs 2. A2C/A3C [166] (Advantage Actor-Critic): Uses TD-based advantage. Lower variance. But unbounded step sizes. 3. TRPO [167]: Constrains KL divergence between policy updates. Stable but expensive (second-order). 4. PPO [168]: Clips the policy ratio to achieve similar stability as TRPO with first-order optimization only. The standard for LLM RL training. 5. GRPO: Removes the critic entirely. Uses group statistics as baseline. Simpler and effective for verifiable rewards. 126 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 3.8 Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE)
sections.content: Motivation: The Actor-Critic framework needs good estimate of the advantage A(s, a) = Q(s, a) (s) how much better was this action than average? But theres fundamental tension: 1-step TD advantage (rt + γV (st+1) (st)): Low variance (only one random step), but biased if the value function is wrong, the advantage estimate is systematically off. Monte Carlo advantage (Gt (st)): Unbiased (uses actual returns), but high variance the sum of many random rewards fluctuates wildly between episodes. GAE [169] (Schulman et al., 2016) provides smooth interpolation between these extremes via single parameter λ [0, 1]. It takes an exponentially-weighted average of n-step advantage estimates for all n, giving principled way to trade bias for variance. Core idea: Compute the 1-step TD error δt at each timestep, then blend them with exponentially decaying weights (γλ)l recent TD errors get full weight, distant ones are down-weighted: ˆAGAE = (γλ)lδt+l, l= δt = rt + γV (st+1) (st) (3.23) Figure 3.2: GAE data flow: each TD residual δV more future residuals (lower bias, higher variance). t+l is weighted by (γλ)l before summation. Higher λ includes What λ Controls Bias-Variance Tradeoff λ = 0: ˆAt = δt = rt + γV (st+1) (st). Trust value function completely. Low variance, but biased if is inaccurate. λ = 1: ˆAt = high variance. γlrt+l (st). Full Monte Carlo return minus baseline. Unbiased but very λ = 0.95 (standard): Sweet spot. Mostly trusts but corrects with actual returns for distant effects. Works because value head becomes accurate after initial training. For LLMs specifically: γ = 1.0 (no time discounting all tokens matter equally in single-turn), λ = 0.95.

sections.title: 3.8.1 Intuitive Mapping of Bias and Variance in GAE
sections.content: In supervised learning, bias and variance stem from structural model assumptions. In reinforcement learning via GAE, they stem from how much you trust flawed model versus how much you trust chaotic environment: 127 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Bias (Systemic Misalignment): Arises when the estimator relies on the structural assumptions and imperfect predictions of the value network Vθ. If θ is under-trained or lacks capacity, the baseline guesses are systematically wrong. Variance (Sample Jitteriness): Arises when the estimator relies on long, unconstrained environmental trajectories. Stochastic transitions, random seeds, and policy execution noise accumulate over long horizons, causing empirical sample rewards to swing wildly between rollouts.

sections.title: 3.8.2 The Architectural Spectrum: Boundary Analysis
sections.content: Figure 3.3: Bias vs. Variance in GAE: λ controls the trade-off. Small λ (left) yields high bias / low variance via bootstrapping; large λ (right) yields low bias / high variance using full Monte Carlo returns. The optimal choice (λ [0.9, 0.95]) balances stable training with accurate long-horizon credit assignment. The hyperparameter λ serves as slide-rule between two fundamental estimation paradigms. High Bias / Low Variance Limit (λ = 0) ˆAGAE(γ,0) = δV = rt + γVθ(st+1) Vθ(st) (3.24) Behavior: The advantage is heavily dictated by the current state of parameters θ. Intuition: Highly biased because the network is grading its own performance over 1-step window; if Vθ is inaccurate, the gradient step is corrupted. Low variance because it ignores future stochastic events beyond step + 1, leading to smooth, stable parameter updates. Risk: Policy traps in sub-optimal local minima never discovers complex delayed reward sequences. Low Bias / High Variance Limit (λ = 1) When λ = 1, intermediate value terms telescopically cancel, reducing GAE to Monte Carlo return minus baseline: ˆAGAE(γ,1) = γlrt+l Vθ(st) (3.25) l=0 Behavior: Discards bootstrap look-aheads and sums up the literal reality of the entire episode. Intuition: Completely unbiased with respect to true environment dynamics measures actual rewards instead of neural approximations. However, exhibits extreme variance: 128 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems minor perturbations early in an episode can result in completely divergent total returns, causing policy updates to become erratic. Risk: Destructive gradient updates; training explosions.

sections.title: 3.8.3 The Trade-off Matrix
sections.content: By selecting λ [0.95, 0.99], GAE minimizes the total mean squared error of the advantage estimate: Table 3.1: Operational comparison of GAE parameter choices. Configuration Statistical Properties Core Reliance Practical Risk λ = λ [0.95, 0.99] λ = 1 High Bias, Low Variance Balanced (Optimal MSE) Low Bias, High Variance parameters Model (θ) Hybrid blending Empirical ment rollout environPolicy traps in sub-optimal local minima Requires tuning based on environment stochasticity Destructive gradient updates; training explosion

sections.title: 3.8.4 Diagnostics for Tuning λ
sections.content: Monitoring training curves yields direct insight into whether bias or variance dominates: 1. High Variance Indicators: Policy entropy drops precipitously while explained variance of the value function becomes highly negative or erratic policy updates are noisy. Remedy: Lower λ to smooth target updates. 2. High Bias Indicators: Agent achieves early stable training but completely fails to discover complex delayed reward sequences under-estimating long-horizon dependencies due to bootstrapping. Remedy: Raise λ closer to 1.0 to expose policy to real downstream trajectory signals.

sections.title: 3.9 On-Policy vs Off-Policy — Detailed Comparison
sections.content: On-Policy Off-Policy

sections.title:
Data source
After update
sections.content: Current policy πθ only Old data is invalid, must regenerate Any policy (replay buffer) Old data still usable Sample efficiency Low (data used once) Stability More stable (consistent distribution) REINFORCE, PPO, A2C, GRPO PPO, GRPO (generate fresh each step) High (data reused many times) Can diverge (distribution mismatch) Q-Learning, DQN, SAC, DPO DPO (static preference dataset)

sections.title: For LLMs
sections.content: On/Off-Policy for RLHF Methods PPO/GRPO are on-policy: Generate responses with current policy, compute advantages, update, discard data, generate again. This is why generation is 60% of compute you regenerate every step. DPO is off-policy: Train on fixed preference dataset. No generation during training. Much cheaper but suffers from distribution shift (data becomes stale as policy changes). Online DPO is hybrid: Generates fresh data (on-policy generation) but uses DPOs supervised 129 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems loss (off-policy-style optimization). Gets benefits of both. PPOs cleverness: Uses the clip ratio = πnew/πold to squeeze multiple gradient steps from one batch of on-policy data (4 epochs), making it slightly off-policy in controlled way.

sections.title: 3.10 Model-Based vs Model-Free
sections.content: Model-Free Whats learned Planning Sample efficiency Low (must experience everything) No model bias Complex/unknown dynamics PPO, DQN, SAC [170] Accuracy When to use Examples Model-Based Environment model ˆP (ss, a) Policy π and/or value /Q directly No planning, reactive decisions Can simulate future trajectories High (can plan in imagination) Model errors compound Simple dynamics, need efficiency MuZero [171], Dreamer [172], AlphaGo [19] Why LLM RL is Model-Free Language generation dynamics are trivial (append token to sequence deterministic transitions). The model of the environment is not the bottleneck. Whats hard is the reward predicting what humans will prefer. This makes model-based methods unnecessary for LLM RL. The reward model in RLHF could be seen as model in some sense (it predicts human preference), but its used as reward signal, not for planning/simulation. LLM RL is fundamentally model-free policy optimization.

sections.title: 3.11 Reward Shaping
sections.content: Reward shaping [173] is technique where developer modifies or supplements the environments original reward function. Its primary objective is to transform sparse reward scenario where the agent receives feedback only upon final task completion into dense reward scenario with intermediate feedback signals to accelerate convergence.

sections.title: 3.11.1 The Mathematical Framework
sections.content: Let the original reward at time step be Rt(s, a, s). The reshaped reward adds an auxiliary shaping function : t(s, a, s) = Rt(s, a, s) + (s, a, s) (3.26) The Risk of Naive Reshaping: Reward Hacking If (s, a, s) is arbitrarily designed, the agent will find structural loopholes to maximize auxiliary signals while ignoring the global objective. Example: navigation agent rewarded for reaching intermediate landmarks might learn to loop indefinitely around single checkpoint to accumulate infinite rewards without ever reaching the destination. In LLMs: model rewarded for sounding confident might learn to always start with Absolutely! regardless of accuracy.

sections.title: 3.11.2 Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS)
sections.content: To mathematically guarantee that reshaping does not alter the optimal policy, use Potential-Based Reward Shaping. The shaping function is constrained to the difference in scalar potential function Φ across states: (s, a, s) = γ Φ(s) Φ(s) (3.27) 130 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems where Φ : is real-valued potential function evaluating the desirable proximity of state to the goal, and γ is the discount factor. The complete PBRS reward: R(s, a, s) = R(s, a, s) + γ Φ(s) Φ(s) (3.28)

sections.title: 3.11.3 Theoretical Guarantees
sections.content: PBRS Policy Invariance Theorem Policy Invariance: The optimal policy π under the reshaped reward is identical to the optimal policy under the original reward R. The shaping cannot introduce sub-optimal behaviors. Loop Immunity: Any cyclic trajectory starting and ending at the same state results in net potential change of exactly zero (Φ(s) Φ(s) = 0). The agent cannot exploit loops to hack the reward. Convergence Acceleration: While the optimal policy is unchanged, the shaped reward provides denser gradient signals, enabling the agent to converge 550 faster in sparse reward environments.

sections.title: RL Methods for LLMs
sections.content: 132 Chapter

sections.title: RL Foundations for Language Models
sections.content: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) teaches model to imitate demonstrations, but imitation has ceiling: the model can never exceed the quality of its training data. Reinforcement learning breaks this barrier. By generating novel text, receiving reward feedback, and updating toward higher-reward behaviours, an RL-trained model can discover strategies that no human demonstrator wroteproducing outputs that are more helpful, more accurate, and better aligned with human preferences [9]. This is the mechanism behind every frontier model: GPT-4 [23], Claude, Llama-3 [25], and DeepSeek-R1 [15] all apply RL after SFT as the critical step that transforms capable but unsteered model into an aligned assistant.

sections.title: 4.1 Two Paradigms for RL in LLMs
sections.content: RL methods for language models fall into two broad paradigms, each suited to different goals: Paradigm 1: Alignment via Human Preferences (RLHF/DPO). The original motivation for applying RL to LLMs was alignmentmaking models helpful, harmless, and honest. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) [9, 174, 175] trains reward model from pairwise human judgments (which response is better?) and then optimizes the policy to maximize that learned reward. DPO [10] simplifies this by eliminating the reward model entirely, converting preferences directly into supervised loss. Both approaches produce aligned assistants that follow instructions and respect safety constraints. Paradigm 2: Capability Enhancement via Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). More recently, RL has been used not just for alignment but for teaching new capabilitiesparticularly reasoning, mathematics, and code generation. Here the reward comes not from human preferences but from verifiable outcomes: did the model produce the correct answer? Did the code pass all tests? DeepSeek-R1 [15] demonstrated that GRPO with rule-based rewards (format correctness + answer accuracy) can train models to develop sophisticated chain-of-thought reasoning without any human preference data. This paradigmRL from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)is now the dominant approach for building reasoning models and agentic systems. The Shared Foundation Despite their different goals, both paradigms share the same core machinery: policy πθ (the LLM) that generates text autoregressively reward signal r(x, y) (learned from preferences or computed from verification) KL constraint against reference policy to prevent degenerate solutions Policy gradient optimization (PPO or GRPO) to update the model toward higher reward The chapters in this part develop each component in detail. 133 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 4.2 Text Generation as an MDP
sections.content: The key insight that makes RL applicable to language models is recasting autoregressive generation as Markov Decision Process: The LLM-as-Agent Analogy Think of the LLM as an agent writing response one token at time. At each step, it looks at everything written so far (the state), chooses the next word (the action), and the page grows by one token (the transition). When the response is complete, judge scores it (the reward). The goal: learn writing strategy (a policy) that consistently earns high scores. Formally, the MDP for text generation is: State st = (x, y1, . . . , yt1): the prompt concatenated with all tokens generated so far. Action at {1, . . . , V}: choosing the next token from the vocabulary (32K128K options). Transition (st+1st, at): deterministicjust append the chosen token. No environment stochasticity. Reward r: typically given only at the end of generation (sparse). For RLHF: the reward model score. For RLVR: correctness of the final answer. Policy πθ(atst): the LLMs next-token probability distributionexactly what the softmax output already computes. Discount γ = 1.0: episodes are finite (one response), so no discounting needed. This mapping is powerful because the LLM already is policyits softmax output defines πθ(atst) for every state. We dont need to build separate policy network; we just need to adjust the weights θ so the model assigns higher probability to token sequences that earn higher reward.

sections.title: 4.3 The RLHF Pipeline
sections.content: The classic RLHF pipeline [9] consists of four stages: 1. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): Train base model on high-quality demonstrations to produce policy πSFT that can follow instructions. 2. Reward Model Training: Collect human preference comparisons (yw yl for the same prompt) and train reward model Rϕ(x, y) using the Bradley-Terry objective. 3. RL Optimization: Use the reward model as signal to optimize the policy via PPO or GRPO, subject to KL constraint against πSFT. 4. Evaluation and Iteration: Evaluate the aligned model, collect new failure cases, and iterate. For RLVR (reasoning/agentic training), stages 12 are replaced: the SFT model is trained on reasoning traces, and the reward model is replaced by verifier (e.g., checking mathematical correctness). Stage 3 remains the samePPO or GRPO optimization against the reward signal. How LLM RL Differs from Classical RL The LLM setting differs from classical RL in important ways: Deterministic transitions: The next state is just the concatenation of previous tokens no stochastic environment. Sparse reward: Feedback is typically given once at the end of generation (outcome reward) or at key steps (process reward). 134 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Massive action space: 32K128K possible tokens at every step, but exploration is implicit via temperature sampling. KL anchor: LLM RL is constrained to stay close to the SFT policy, preventing reward hacking at the cost of reduced exploration. No value function needed: GRPO eliminates the critic network entirely, using grouprelative normalization of rewards instead. These differences explain why PPO and GRPO dominate over DQN-style approaches for LLMs.

sections.title: 4.4 Roadmap of This Part
sections.content: The chapters ahead build the complete RL-for-LLMs toolkit: 1. PPO (Chapter 5) The clipped surrogate objective, GAE for advantage estimation, the critic network, and the full RLHF training loop. The workhorse behind GPT-4 and Claude. 2. DPO (Chapter 6) Bypassing RL entirely by converting preferences into contrastive supervised loss. Simpler but less flexible than online RL. 3. GRPO (Chapter 7) DeepSeeks critic-free algorithm that uses group-level reward normalization. The method behind DeepSeek-R1 and the dominant choice for reasoning model training. 4. Preference optimization variants (Chapter 8) Online DPO, KTO, Best-of-N, and guidance on method selection. 5. Reward modeling (Chapter 9) Bradley-Terry models, process vs. outcome rewards, rule-based rewards for RLVR, and multi-objective combinations. 6. SFT best practices (Chapter 10) Sequence packing, chat templates, data mixing, and how SFT quality determines the RL ceiling. 7. Systems engineering (Chapter 11) Distributed training at scale: parallelism strategies, generationtraining decoupling, and infrastructure for hundreds of GPUs. 135 Chapter 5 PPO Proximal Policy Optimization

sections.title: 5.1 Motivation and History
sections.content: Problem: Vanilla policy gradient updates have no constraint on step size. single unlucky batch can push the policy into region where it generates garbage garbage gets low rewards next gradient makes things worse unrecoverable collapse. Solution history: 1. TRPO [167] (2015): Constrain KL divergence between old and new policy. Works perfectly but requires expensive second-order optimization (Fisher information matrix, conjugate gradients). 2. PPO (2017) [168]: Achieve similar stability with simple first-order clipped objective. 10 simpler to implement, works almost as well, scales to distributed training trivially.

sections.title: 5.2 The Clipped Objective
sections.content: The core innovation of PPO is clipped surrogate objective that prevents destructively large policy updates while remaining simple to implement. LCLIP(θ) = Et min (cid:16) rt(θ) ˆAt, clip(rt(θ), 1ϵ, 1+ϵ) ˆAt (cid:17)i (5.1) where rt(θ) = πθ(atst) πθold (atst) is the probability ratio. Clipping Intuition The Key Insight The min operator creates pessimistic bound: Good action ( ˆA > 0): We want to increase its probability. The surrogate ˆA grows as increases. But clip caps benefit at = 1 + ϵ. Dont get greedy on one good example. Bad action ( ˆA < 0): We want to decrease its probability. ˆA improves as decreases. But clip caps benefit at = 1 ϵ. Dont forget too aggressively based on one bad example. Net effect: policy changes by at most 20% per update step. Prevents both catastrophic collapse and overconfident specialization.

sections.title: 5.3 Full PPO Loss
sections.content: L = LCLIP c1 (Vθ(st) target {z value loss )2 } +c2 H[πθ(st)] } {z entropy bonus (5.2) Value loss (c1 = 0.1): Trains the critic to predict returns. Also clipped for stability. 136 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Entropy bonus (c2 = 0.01): Prevents premature convergence to deterministic policy. Critical for exploration.

sections.title: 5.4 Derivation of the PPO Gradient and Update Rule
sections.content: This section traces the mathematical path from the RL objective to the PPO update rule, showing why the clipped surrogate works.

sections.title: 5.4.1 Step 1: The RL Objective
sections.content: The goal is to maximize expected cumulative reward under the policy: J(θ) = Eτ πθ " # rt t=

sections.title: 5.4.2 Step 2: Policy Gradient Theorem
sections.content: The gradient of J(θ) with respect to policy parameters: θJ(θ) = Eπθ θ log πθ(atst) ˆAt # " t=0 (5.3) (5.4) where ˆAt is the advantage function (how much better action at was compared to the average action in state st). This replaces the full return with the advantage to reduce variance.

sections.title: 5.4.3 Step 3: Importance Sampling for Off-Policy Data
sections.content: PPO collects data using πθold but updates πθ. To correct for this distribution mismatch, apply importance sampling: θJ(θ) = Eπθold " πθ(atst) πθold(atst) θ log πθ(atst) ˆAt # Define the probability ratio rt(θ) = πθ(atst) (atst) . Using the identity θ log = θf , we get: πθold θJ(θ) = Eπθold θ rt(θ) ˆAt This means maximizing the surrogate objective: LCPI(θ) = Et rt(θ) ˆAt (5.5) (5.6) (5.7)

sections.title: 5.4.4 Step 4: The Problem with Unconstrained Surrogates
sections.content: LCPI is valid objective, but without constraints, single gradient step can push rt(θ) far from 1.0, causing: Importance weights become extreme high variance Policy enters untested regions reward model gives unreliable scores Catastrophic collapse: policy generates garbage, cant recover TRPO solution: Constrain DKL(πθoldπθ) δ. Requires second-order methods (expensive). H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 5.4.5 Step 5: PPO’s Clipped Surrogate (First-Order Approximation)
sections.content: PPO replaces the hard KL constraint with clipped objective that achieves similar behavior using only first-order gradients: LCLIP(θ) = Et min (cid:16) rt(θ) ˆAt, clip(rt(θ), 1ϵ, 1+ϵ) ˆAt (cid:17)i Derivation of the gradient: Let Lt = min(rt ˆAt, rt ˆAt) where rt = clip(rt, 1ϵ, 1+ϵ). θLt = (θrt(θ) ˆAt 0 if rt ˆAt < rt ˆAt (unclipped term is smaller) if rt ˆAt rt ˆAt (clipped term is smaller, gradient = 0) Expanding the conditions: (5.8) (5.9) When ˆAt > 0 and rt < 1 + ϵ: Gradient flows normally policy is encouraged to increase πθ(atst). When ˆAt > 0 and rt 1 + ϵ: Gradient is zero policy has already increased enough, stop pushing. When ˆAt < 0 and rt > 1 ϵ: Gradient flows normally policy is encouraged to decrease πθ(atst). When ˆAt < 0 and rt 1 ϵ: Gradient is zero policy has already decreased enough, stop pushing.

sections.title: 5.4.6 Step 6: The Complete PPO Update Rule
sections.content: Combining the clipped policy loss, value loss, and entropy bonus: θk+1 = θk + α θ LCLIP(θ) c1LVF(θ) + c2H[πθ] where: (cid:16) LVF(θ) = H[πθ] = Vθ(st) target (cid:17)2 (value function regression loss) πθ(ast) log πθ(ast) (entropy of the policy) (5.10) (5.11) (5.12) Summary: Why This Works 1. Policy gradient theorem gives us the direction to improve the policy. 2. Importance sampling lets us reuse data from πθold across multiple epochs. 3. Clipping prevents the importance weights from becoming extreme, keeping updates safe. 4. The min operator ensures we always take the more conservative of (clipped, unclipped) pessimistic lower bound on improvement. 5. Result: Monotonic improvement with probability 1, using only first-order gradients. No Hessians, no conjugate gradients, no line searches.

sections.title: 5.5 Rollout Buffer and Rollouts
sections.content: In PPO, data management relies on specialized, short-term storage system known as Rollout Buffer. Unlike off-policy algorithms (DQN) that store experiences indefinitely in replay buffer, PPO requires an ephemeral structure to satisfy its on-policy mathematical constraints. 138 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 5.5.1 What is a Rollout?
sections.content: A rollout (trajectory) is sequence of interactions generated by the agent running its current policy in the environment: The process: The agent observes state, selects an action, receives reward, and moves to the next state. It repeats for fixed number of steps or until the episode ends. In LLMs/RLHF: rollout consists of taking prompt from dataset and letting the language model generate complete sequence of tokens token-by-token until an end-of-text marker is hit. Each token is one step.

sections.title: 5.5.2 The Rollout Buffer
sections.content: The rollout buffer temporarily stores all data collected during the rollout phase. For every generated token/step, it records: = {(st, at, log πθold(atst), rt, (st))}T t=1 (5.13) st, at, rt: State, action taken, and reward at step t. log πθold(atst): Log-probability of taking that action under the exact policy that generated it (needed for ratio computation). (st): Value functions baseline prediction (needed for GAE advantage computation).

sections.title: 5.5.3 The Rollout Buffer Lifecycle
sections.content: The buffer operates in strict three-phase clockwork cycle: 1. Collect: The active policy interacts with the environment to fill the buffer with fresh trajectories (for 70B model with batch=128, max_tokens=512: up to 65K token-level transitions per rollout). 2. Train: Compute GAE advantages across trajectories. Run epochs (typically 310) of mini-batch gradient descent to update policy weights using the clipped objective. 3. Purge: The entire buffer is completely wiped clean. Because PPO is on-policy, data generated by the old policy cannot be safely reused for the next update cycle the ratio rt(θ) would become stale and the clipping guarantees would break.

sections.title: Rollout Buffer vs Replay Buffer
sections.content: Replay Buffer (DQN, SAC): Off-policy. Stores millions of transitions indefinitely. Random sampling. Data reused across many updates. Rollout Buffer (PPO, GRPO): On-policy. Stores one batch of trajectories. Used for few epochs, then discarded entirely. Fresh data required every cycle. This is why PPO requires continuous generation the buffer is emptied after every update, demanding fresh rollouts. This makes the generation bottleneck (6070% of wall-clock time) particularly painful. vLLM in RLHF Context In RLHF training, vLLM is used for the generation phase (6070% of wall-clock time). The policy model generates rollouts that are then scored by the reward model. Key benefits: Batched generation: Generate 256+ responses in parallel across prompts. Memory efficiency: Fit more concurrent generations higher GPU utilization during the generation bottleneck. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Prefix sharing: When generating = 8 responses per prompt (GRPO), the prompt KV is computed once and shared across all 8 no redundant prefill. Integration: Frameworks like OpenRLHF and TRL use vLLM as the generation backend, separating generation workers (vLLM) from training workers (DeepSpeed/FSDP).

sections.title: 5.6 PPO for RLHF: The Full Loop
sections.content: Concrete PPO Step for 70B Chat Model Setup: Batch of 128 prompts, Llama-3-70B policy, 512 max tokens. Step 1 Generate: Sample 128 responses (temperature=0.7, top-p=0.9). This takes 60% of time. Step 2 Score: Reward model scores each (prompt, response) pair. Range: 0.20.95. Step 3 KL: Compute per-token KL: KLt = log πθ(yty<t) log πref(yty<t). Mean KL across tokens: typically 38. Step 4 Final reward: = rRM 0.05 mean_KL (only at last token). Step 5 GAE: Compute ˆAt for each token position using value head predictions. Whiten advantages (zero mean, unit variance). Step 6 Update: 4 epochs of SGD on mini-batches of 16. Clip ratio ϵ = 0.2. Gradient norm clipping at 1.0. Result: Policy improves by 0.005 win-rate per step. After 10K steps: 510% absolute improvement over SFT. Tokenization Pitfalls in RL for LLMs When computing per-token KL penalties and advantages, remember that tokenization determines what step is. single conceptual action (e.g., outputting 2024) might span 14 tokens depending on the tokenizer. This creates subtle issues: KL accounting: Per-token KL sums to different totals for the same semantic content tokenized differently (e.g., rare words split into more subwords get higher total KL penalty). Credit assignment: GAE assigns advantage per token positionbut semantic decisions often span multiple tokens. The model only truly decides at the first token of word; subsequent subword tokens are largely deterministic. Reward placement: Placing reward only at the final token means all preceding tokens must propagate credit backward through GAElonger responses suffer from more diluted signal. Mitigation: Some systems normalize KL by sequence length, use word-level reward shaping, or apply reward at semantic boundaries rather than the final token.

sections.title: 5.7 Detailed Mechanics: Logits and Policy Updates
sections.content: PPO manages two distinct parameter states in memory, which share the same neural network topology but hold different weight values during optimization: 140 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Figure 5.1: PPO end-to-end: from prompt batch through generation, reward scoring, KL computation, advantage estimation, to clipped policy update. The feedback loop shows the updated policy being used for the next generation step. Core Architecture: Two Networks 1. The Policy Network (πθ): The active, live network parameterized by weights θ. Continuously updated via backpropagation during optimization. 2. The Old Policy Network (πθold): frozen snapshot parameterized by weights θold. Acts as static anchor during single optimization cycle to prevent the policy from shifting too drastically.

sections.title: 5.7.1 Phase 1: Rollout (Data Collection)
sections.content: During data collection, the agent interacts with the environment for steps. At each time-step t: 1. The environment yields the current state/observation st (for LLMs: prompt + tokens generated so far). 2. State st is passed through the current network snapshot (θold). 3. The network outputs raw unnormalized values logits zold vector of size (vocabulary size 32K128K). 4. Probabilities are computed via Softmax: (a st) = Softmax(zold) = exp(zold,a) j=1 exp(zold,j) PV (5.14) 5. An action at (next token) is sampled from (a st), and the transition tuple st, at, rt, st+1 along with log πθold(at st) is stored in the rollout buffer. Why Store Log-Probabilities? Storing log πθold(at st) as scalar during rollout avoids re-running the frozen network during optimization. This saves one full forward pass per mini-batch significant for 70B models.

sections.title: 5.7.2 Phase 2: Optimization Loop (Mini-Batch Updates)
sections.content: Once the rollout buffer is full, PPO runs epochs (typically 310) over mini-batches. For every gradient step, logits are generated for both policies using the stored state st: Old Policy Evaluation (frozen): zold = (st; θold) log πθold(at st) = LogSoftmax(zold)[at] (5.15) Implementation shortcut: reuse the stored scalar from rollout instead of re-computing. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Live Policy Evaluation (updating): znew = (st; θ) log πθ(at st) = LogSoftmax(znew)[at] (5.16) Because θ updates after every mini-batch gradient step, znew changes continuously throughout the optimization loop, whereas zold remains perfectly static.

sections.title: 5.7.3 From Logits to Probability Ratio
sections.content: The core PPO ratio measures how much more or less likely an action is under the new policy vs the old: rt(θ) = πθ(at st) πθold(at st) (5.17) To avoid catastrophic numerical underflow/overflow from dividing raw probabilities, the calculation is performed in log-space: log πθ(at st) = LogSoftmax(znew)[at] log πθold(at st) = LogSoftmax(zold)[at] The ratio is recovered via exponentiation of the difference: rt(θ) = exp(log πθ(at st) log πθold(at st)) This ratio is injected into the PPO clipping objective: LCLIP(θ) = ˆEt min (cid:16) rt(θ) ˆAt, clip(rt(θ), 1ϵ, 1+ϵ) ˆAt (cid:17)i (5.18) (5.19) (5.20) (5.21)

sections.title: How Clipping Works
sections.content: If ˆAt > 0 (good action): ratio is clipped at 1 + ϵ cannot over-exploit good actions. If ˆAt < 0 (bad action): ratio is clipped at 1 ϵ cannot over-penalize bad actions. The min() ensures we always take the more conservative estimate. Result: monotonic improvement within trust region no catastrophic collapses.

sections.title: 5.7.4 The PPO Weight Lifecycle
sections.content: Table 5.1: Evolution of θ and θold across PPO training phases.

sections.title: Phase
sections.content: Live θ Old θold Ratio rt(θ) 1. Rollout Start 2. Batch Step 1 3. Batch Step 4. Clipping Active 5. Optimization End Highly optimized 6. Next Cycle Active copy Same active copy Frozen Computes gradients Modifying (θ = θold) Frozen Frozen Bounded by ϵ Discarded Receives fresh θ θ θold Always 1.0 (by identity) 1.0 (initial step) Deviates from 1.0 (e.g., 1.06, 0.94) Trapped at bound (1 ϵ) N/A Resets back to 1.

sections.title: 5.7.5 Continuous Action Spaces Extension
sections.content: For continuous action spaces (not typical for LLMs, but important for robotics RL), the network outputs distribution parameters instead of discrete logits: Predicted mean vector µ 142 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Predicted standard deviation vector σ Log-probabilities are computed via the Gaussian log-PDF: log π(at st) = (cid:19)2 1 2 (cid:18) at µ σ log(σ) 1 log(2π) (5.22) The ratio rt(θ) = exp(log πθ log πθold) is then computed identically and fed into the same clipping objective.

sections.title: 5.8 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The HuggingFace TRL library [176] provides production-ready implementations of all major RL methods for LLMs. from trl import PPOConfig , PPOTrainer , o e r s M h u a from transformers import AutoTokenizer from peft import LoraConfig # Model setup model = o e r s M h u a . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " , torch_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , device_map = " auto " , peft_config = LoraConfig ( =64 , lora_alpha =16 , target_modules =[ " q_proj " ," v_proj " , " k_proj " ," o_proj " ]) ) tokenizer = AutoTokenizer . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " ) # PPO config with all critical hyperparameters ppo_config = PPOConfig ( learning_rate =1.5 -6 , batch_size =128 , mini_batch_size =16 , ppo_epochs =4 , gamma =1.0 , lam =0.95 , cliprange =0.2 , cliprange_value =0.2 , vf_coef =0.1 , init_kl_coef =0.05 , target_kl =6.0 , whiten_rewards = True , d t _ u a n _ p =4 , max_grad_norm =1.0 , # Low LR for stability # Prompts per step # Gradient accumulation unit # Epochs per batch ( reuse data ) # No discounting ( single turn ) # GAE lambda # PPO epsilon # Value function clipping # Value loss coefficient # Initial KL penalty # Adaptive KL target # Normalize advantages ) ppo_trainer = PPOTrainer ( config = ppo_config , model = model , tokenizer = tokenizer , dataset = prompt_dataset , data_collator = collator ) # Training loop for batch in ppo_trainer . dataloader : # 1. Generate responses query_tensors = batch [ " input_ids " ] response_tensors = ppo_trainer . generate ( query_tensors , max_new_tokens =512 , temperature =0.7 , top_p =0.9 , do_sample = True ) # 2. Score with reward model texts = [ tokenizer . decode (r , skip_special_tokens = True ) for in response_tensors ] rewards = [ torch . tensor ( reward_model . score (q , ) ) for , in zip ( batch [ " query " ] , texts ) ] # 3. PPO update ( handles KL , GAE , clipping internally ) 143 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems stats = ppo_trainer . step ( query_tensors , response_tensors , rewards ) # Monitor : stats [" ppo / mean_scores "] , stats [" ppo / policy / approx_kl "]

sections.title: 5.9 Critical Hyperparameters
sections.content: Parameter Typical cliprange init_kl_coef 0.2 0.010.1 target_kl 4 ppo_epochs 4 learning_rate batch_size temperature 15 106 64256 0.71.0 Effect of Getting It Wrong Too low: no learning. Too high: instability. Too low: reward hacking. Too high: stuck at SFT. Adaptive controller target. Lower = conservative. Too many: overfits to batch. Too few: wastes gen compute. Too high: catastrophic forgetting. Larger = smoother gradients, more gen compute. Lower: less exploration. Higher: noisier advantages. 144 Chapter 6 DPO Direct Preference Optimization

sections.title: 6.1 Motivation
sections.content: PPO requires 4 models in memory (policy, reference, reward model, value head), complex RL infrastructure, and is notoriously unstable. DPO [10] asks: can we skip the RL and learn directly from preferences? Key insight: The optimal policy under the RLHF objective (reward maximization + KL penalty) has closed-form solution. We can derive supervised loss that implicitly optimizes the same objective.

sections.title: 6.2 Mathematical Derivation
sections.content: Step 1: RLHF objective: maxπ Ex,yπ[r(x, y)] βDKL[ππref] Z(x) πref(yx) exp Step 2: The optimal solution is: π(yx) = 1 Step 3: Rearrange to express reward in terms of policy: r(x, y) = β log π(yx) Step 4: Substitute into Bradley-Terry preference model (yw yl) = σ(r(yw) r(yl)). The Z(x) πref(yx) + β log Z(x) (cid:16) r(x,y) β (cid:17) cancels! LDPO(θ) = E(x,yw,yl) (cid:20) log σ (cid:18) β log πθ(ywx) πref(ywx) β log (cid:19)(cid:21) πθ(ylx) πref(ylx) (6.1)

sections.title: What DPO Actually Does
sections.content: Define the implicit reward as ˆr(x, y) = β log πθ(yx) πref(yx) . DPO minimizes the cross-entropy loss where the label is: chosen should have higher implicit reward than rejected. The margin is controlled by β: Large β: need large margin policy moves aggressively risk forgetting Small β: small margin suffices policy stays close to reference conservative The reference model acts as regularizer: the policy must justify any deviation from it by showing preference alignment.

sections.title: 6.3 Gradient Analysis
sections.content: The DPO gradient decomposes as: θL = β σ(ˆrw + ˆrl) } {z weight: higher when model is wrong [θ log πθ(ywx) θ log πθ(ylx)] (6.2) H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Interpretation: The gradient increases probability of chosen and decreases rejected. The weight is largest when the model currently prefers the wrong answer it focuses learning on confusing pairs. Concrete DPO Example Prompt: Explain quantum entanglement to 10-year-old. Chosen (yw): Imagine you have two magic coins. When you flip one and its heads, the other one instantly becomes tails, no matter how far apart they are! log πθ(ywx) = 15.3, log πref(ywx) = 16.1 Rejected (yl): Quantum entanglement is phenomenon where two particles become correlated such that the quantum state of one particle cannot be described independently. log πθ(ylx) = 12.8, log πref(ylx) = 12.5 Implicit rewards: ˆrw = 0.1 ((15.3) (16.1)) = 0.08, ˆrl = 0.1 ((12.8) (12.5)) = 0.03 Loss input: σ(0.08 (0.03)) = σ(0.11) = 0.527 Loss: log(0.527) = 0.64 The model barely prefers the chosen. Gradient will push hard. After training: chosen probability increases, rejected decreases, until margin stabilizes around 1/(2β).

sections.title: 6.4 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The following shows minimal working example using HuggingFace TRL. from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM , AutoTokenizer from peft import LoraConfig from datasets import load_dataset model = ut del orC usalLM . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " , torch_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , attn_im plement at io = " fl ash_at tentio n_2 " ) tokenizer = AutoTokenizer . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " ) # Dataset format : {" prompt ": str , " chosen ": str , " rejected ": str } dataset = load_dataset ( " argilla / ultrafeedback - binarized - preferences " ) lora_config = LoraConfig ( =64 , lora_alpha =16 , lora_dropout =0.05 , target_modules =[ " q_proj " ," k_proj " ," v_proj " ," o_proj " ," gate_proj " ," up_proj " ," down_proj " ]) dpo_config = DPOConfig ( # KL regularization strength # Very low LR for stability # Standard DPO loss # Max sequence length # Truncation for prompts # Effective batch = 16 # DPO overfits fast - 1 epoch ! output_dir = " ./ dpo_output " , beta =0.1 , learning_rate =5 -7 , loss_type = " sigmoid " , max_length =2048 , max_ prompt_leng th =1024 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =2 , d t _ u a n _ p =8 , gr ad ie nt _ ch ec kp oi n = True , bf16 = True , num_train_epochs =1 , warmup_ratio =0.1 , logging_steps =10 , eval_strategy = " steps " , eval_steps =200 , save_strategy = " steps " , save_steps =500 , ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , 146 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems # With LoRA , ref = base model ( no copy needed !) ref_model = None , args = dpo_config , train_dataset = dataset [ " train " ] , eval_dataset = dataset [ " test " ] , tokenizer = tokenizer , peft_config = lora_config , ) trainer . train () # Key metrics to monitor : train / rewards / chosen , train / rewards / rejected , train / rewards / margins

sections.title: 6.5 How DPO Works: Full Mechanics
sections.content: This section provides the complete computational details of DPO what happens at the token level during training.

sections.title: 6.5.1 Sequence-Level Log-Probabilities
sections.content: The key quantity in DPO is the log-probability of an entire sequence = (y1, y2, . . . , yT ) given prompt x. This is computed as the sum of per-token log-probabilities: log πθ(yx) = t=1 log πθ(yt x, y<t) (6.3) Each term log πθ(ytx, y<t) is the log-softmax output at position for the actual token yt in the sequence. This is identical to the cross-entropy loss used in standard language modeling but here we sum rather than average. Critical detail: The gradient flows through every token position in both yw and yl. There is no masking of intermediate tokens every token contributes to the sequence-level log-probability.

sections.title: 6.5.2 The DPO Loss Decomposed
sections.content: Starting from the loss: LDPO(θ) = E(x,yw,yl)D[log σ(β hθ(x, yw, yl))] where the implicit reward margin hθ is: hθ(x, yw, yl) = log πθ(ywx) πref(ywx) } log πθ(ylx) πref(ylx) } {z chosen reward proxy {z rejected reward proxy Expanding into token-level terms: hθ = yw t=1 log πθ(yt wx, y<t ) log πref(yt wx, y<t ) yl t= log πθ(yt x, y<t ) log πref(yt x, y<t ) (6.4) (6.5) (6.6)

sections.title: 6.5.3 Forward Pass: Step by Step
sections.content: For one training example (x, yw, yl): 1. Concatenate: Form two sequences: [x; yw] and [x; yl]. Pad to equal length within the batch. 2. Forward pass (policy πθ): Run both sequences through the model. Collect logits at every response position. 147 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems 3. Extract log-probs: At each position in the response, take log softmax(logitst)[yt] the log-probability of the actual token. 4. Sum over tokens: logp_chosen = log πθ(yt wx, y<t ) logp_rejected = tresponse positions tresponse positions log πθ(yt x, y<t ) 5. Subtract reference (pre-computed or from second forward pass): ratio_w = logp_chosen ref_logp_chosen ratio_l = logp_rejected ref_logp_rejected 6. Compute loss: = log σ(β (ratio_w ratio_l)) 7. Backward pass: Gradients flow back through steps 5 4 3 2 to update θ. (6.7) (6.8) (6.9) (6.10)

sections.title: 6.5.4 Token-Level Gradient Analysis
sections.content: Does every token get gradient? Yes. The gradient with respect to the logits at position in the chosen sequence is: logits(w) = σ(β hθ) } {z scaling factor β log πθ(yt logits(w) w) (6.11) Key insight: The scaling factor σ(β hθ) is shared across all tokens in both sequences. It acts as an adaptive learning rate: When hθ is small (model cant distinguish chosen from rejected): scaling 0.5 strong gradient, learn aggressively. When hθ is large (model already prefers chosen): scaling 0 negligible gradient, dont over-fit. Effect on chosen tokens: Probability is increased (log-prob pushed up). Effect on rejected tokens: Probability is decreased (log-prob pushed down). Relative to reference: Only the difference from πref matters. If the model already assigns high probability to the chosen response (matching the reference), theres little gradient.

sections.title: 6.5.5 Per-Token vs. Sequence-Level: Length Normalization
sections.content: A subtle issue: longer sequences naturally have lower log-probabilities (more terms summed, each 0). If yw yl, the loss can be biased toward preferring shorter responses. Solutions: Length-normalized DPO: Replace log πθ(yx) with 1 t log πθ(yt). Used in some implementations (SimPO adopts this). Standard DPO: Uses raw sum (no normalization). This implicitly penalizes verbosity the model must assign high probability to every token in the chosen response. Practical impact: On benchmarks, length-normalized DPO reduces length gaming but can hurt instruction-following quality. Standard (unnormalized) is more common in production.

sections.title: 6.5.6 Label Masking: What Gets Gradients
sections.content: 148 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Which Tokens Receive Gradient in DPO Prompt tokens (x): NO gradient. The loss is computed only over response positions. Prompt tokens provide context but their logits dont contribute to log π(yx). Chosen response tokens (yw): ALL tokens get gradient. Each yt contributes to the sum. Gradient pushes their probabilities up. Rejected response tokens (yl): ALL tokens get gradient. Each yt contributes to the sum. Gradient pushes their probabilities down. Padding tokens: NO gradient. Masked out with attention mask.

sections.title: 6.5.7 Pseudocode: DPO Training Step
sections.content: DPO Forward + Backward (PyTorch-style) def dpo_loss ( model , ref_model , batch , beta =0.1) : " " " One DPO training step . " " " # batch contains : input_ids_chosen , input_ids_rejected , # labels_chosen , labels_rejected ( prompt masked to -100) # 1. Forward pass : get per - token log - probs logps_chosen = get _ seq uence_lo gp ro ( model , batch [ " chosen " ]) logps_rejected = t_s quence_logprob ( model , batch [ " rejected " ]) # 2. Reference log - probs ( pre - computed or computed here ) with torch . no_grad () : ref_logps_chosen = ge _sequence_logprob ( ref_model , batch [ " chosen " ]) ref_lo gp s_re jected = t_sequence_logprob ( ref_model , batch [ " rejected " ]) # 3. Compute implicit reward margins chosen_rewards = beta * ( logps_chosen - ref_logps_chosen ) rejected_rewards = beta * ( logps_rejected - ref_logps_rejected ) # 4. DPO loss = - log ( sigmoid ( chosen_reward - rejected_reward ) ) loss = -F . logsigmoid ( chosen_rewards - rejected_rewards ) . mean () return loss def et _s eq enc _lo prob ( model , sequences ) : " " " Sum of log - probs over response tokens only . " " " outputs = model ( sequences [ " input_ids " ] , attention_mask = sequences [ " mask " ]) logits = outputs . logits [: , : -1 , :] # Shift for next - token prediction # Gather log - prob of actual tokens labels = sequences [ " labels " ][: , 1:] log_probs = . log_softmax ( logits , dim = -1) token_logps = log_probs . gather ( -1 , labels . unsqueeze ( -1) ) . squeeze ( -1) # Shifted labels # Mask : only sum over response tokens ( labels != -100) mask = ( labels != -100) . float () return ( token_logps * mask ) . sum ( dim = -1) # Shape : [ batch_size ]

sections.title: DPO Implementation Pitfalls
sections.content: Forgetting to mask the prompt: If prompt tokens are included in the log-prob sum, the model optimizes for prompt likelihood (useless) and the effective β is wrong. Using mean instead of sum: 1 log π vs. t log π these give different implicit length penalties. Must be consistent between πθ and πref. 149 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Stale reference model: If πref is too far from πθ (e.g., base model vs. fine-tuned), the KL term dominates and gradients vanish. Solution: use the SFT checkpoint (not base) as reference. β too large: Magnifies log-prob differences sigmoid saturates zero gradients. Start with β = 0.1, tune in [0.05, 0.5]. β too small: Theoretically allows more freedom from reference (weaker KL constraint), but the gradient β σ(βh) becomes vanishingly small loss landscape is flat extremely slow convergence. The model has permission to move far but receives almost no signal telling it where to move.

sections.title: 6.6 DPO Variants and When Each Fails
sections.content: When DPO Fails 1. Distribution shift: Preference data from old model. Current policy generates different text loss is optimizing on irrelevant examples. 2. No exploration: Cant discover behaviors not in dataset. Stuck in local optimum. 3. Reference collapse: If reference is too strong, policy cant move. If too weak, no regularization. 4. Data quality: Noisy labels poison training. Unlike PPO which averages over many samples, DPO memorizes individual pairs. 5. Preference data diversity: Ensure chosen/rejected pairs cover the full spectrum of quality differences (not just good-vs-terrible). Pairs that differ in approach, not just quality, teach richer policy distinctions. 6.7 β Selection Guide β

sections.title: When to Use
sections.content: Only if data is extremely clean and you need large distributional shift Good data, want noticeable improvement over SFT Default starting point. Good balance of quality vs stability Noisy data, or model already close to desired behavior Safety fine-tuning where you must not break capabilities

sections.title: 6.8 DPO Batch Size Configuration and Scaling
sections.content: Unlike standard SFT which operates on single-sequence token predictions, DPO leverages pairwise loss comparing preferred sequence against dispreferred sequence. This fundamentally alters memory utilization and optimization stability.

sections.title: 6.8.1 Global Batch Size Target
sections.content: Empirical evidence across DPO implementations establishes an optimal global batch size range: Bglobal [32, 128] (6.12) Bglobal < 32: Severe gradient noise in implicit reward estimation policy oscillates destructively between alignment goals (helpfulness vs. safety). H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Bglobal > 128: Diminishing returns on convergence velocity; high communication overhead across distributed compute.

sections.title: 6.8.2 Mathematical Decomposition
sections.content: Because DPO loads two model copies simultaneously (active policy πθ + frozen reference πref), per-sequence memory is doubled. The global batch size is decomposed as: Bglobal = Bmicro NGPUs Kaccum (6.13) Bmicro: Per-device micro-batch size (preference pairs per forward pass). NGPUs: Number of parallel data-processing devices. Kaccum: Gradient accumulation steps before weight update. The pairing multiplier: single DPO data instance contains prompt (x), chosen (yw), and rejected (yl). The actual tensor load per micro-batch: Tsequences = 2 Bmicro (6.14) For models >7B parameters on 80GB GPUs with context lengths 40968192 tokens, the physical limit is rigidly constrained to Bmicro [1, 2].

sections.title: 6.8.3 Distributed Scaling Configurations
sections.content: Table 6.1: Distributed scaling profiles for DPO training (Bglobal = 64 target).

sections.content: 8-GPU Node

Bglobal
Bmicro

Kaccum
Throughput
sections.content: 64 2 (4 sequences) 1 32 steps Sequential/slow 64 2 (4 sequences) 8 4 steps High parallel throughput

sections.title: 6.8.4 VRAM Optimization: Pre-computing Reference Log-Probabilities
sections.content: The DPO loss: LDPO(θ) = E(x,yw,yl) (cid:18) (cid:20) log σ β log πθ(ywx) πref(ywx) β log (cid:19)(cid:21) πθ(ylx) πref(ylx) (6.15) Because πref is completely static throughout training, its outputs can be pre-computed:

sections.title: Reference Model Eviction Strategy
sections.content: 1. Execute forward pass over dataset using only πref before training begins. 2. Cache the scalars log πref(ywx) and log πref(ylx) to disk. 3. Evict πref completely from GPU memory. Result: Available GPU memory doubles can increase Bmicro from 12 to 48, maximizing hardware utilization and training throughput. Implementation: In TRL, set precompute_ref_log_probs=True in DPOConfig. For 70B models, this saves 140GB of GPU memory across the cluster. 151 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 6.9 DPO Extensions and Variants
sections.content: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) reformulates RLHF as supervised learning problem by deriving closed-form mapping between the reward function and the optimal policy. The standard DPO loss is: LDPO(θ) = E(q,yw,yl) (cid:20) log σ (cid:18) β log πθ(ywq) πref(ywq) β log πθ(ylq) πref(ylq) (cid:19)(cid:21) , where yw is the preferred (winning) response, yl is the dispreferred (losing) response, and β controls the strength of the KL penalty. The following subsections cover the most important extensions and variants. 6.9.1 f-DPO Generalised f-Divergence DPO Beyond Reverse KL Standard DPO uses reverse KL divergence as the regulariser between policy and reference. Reverse KL is mode-seeking: it prefers to concentrate probability mass on few high-reward responses. Forward KL is mode-covering: fit spreads probability mass to cover all plausible responses. DPO [177] generalises to any f-divergence, allowing practitioners to trade off these behaviours. The f-DPO loss replaces the log-ratio with the derivative of the f-divergence generator: (cid:20) Lf -DPO = (cid:19) (cid:18) πθ(ywq) πref(ywq) (cid:18) πθ(ylq) πref(ylq) (cid:19)(cid:21) , where is the derivative of the f-divergence generator function. f-Divergence Options in TRL reverse_kl: (u) = log u. Standard DPO. Mode-seeking. forward_kl: (u) = 1/u. Mode-covering. Better diversity. js_divergence: (u) = log(2u/(u + 1)). Balanced mode-seeking/covering. alpha_divergence: (u) = uα1. Interpolates between forward and reverse KL. f-DPO in TRL from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer # Jensen - Shannon divergence ( balanced ) config = DPOConfig ( f_d iverge nce_ty pe = " js_divergence " , beta =0.1 , ) # Alpha divergence ( alpha =0: forward KL , alpha =1: reverse KL ) config_alpha = DPOConfig ( f_diverge nce_ty pe = " alpha_divergence " , _a ph _ iv e _ oe =0.5 , beta =0.1 , # alpha parameter ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , ) 152 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems When to Use f-DPO Use JS divergence when you want balance between diversity and quality. Use forward KL for creative tasks where diversity is paramount. Use reverse KL (standard DPO) for tasks with single correct answer. Use alpha divergence to continuously interpolate and tune the trade-off.

sections.title: 6.9.2 Robust DPO
sections.content: Noisy Labels in Preference Data Human preference annotations are noisy. Annotators disagree, make mistakes, and sometimes flip the preferred/dispreferred labels. Standard DPO treats all labels as ground truth, which can cause the model to overfit to noise. Robust DPO [178] analytically debiases the loss under known noise model. Assume each label is flipped with probability ϵ (the noise rate). The debiased loss is: Lrobust = (1 ϵ) LDPO(yw, yl) ϵ LDPO(yl, yw) 1 2ϵ , where LDPO(yw, yl) is the standard DPO loss treating yw as preferred, and LDPO(yl, yw) is the loss with labels flipped. This correction removes the bias introduced by label noise. Intuition for Robust DPO The formula is linear combination that subtracts out the contribution of flipped labels. When ϵ = 0, it reduces to standard DPO. When ϵ = 0.5, the denominator goes to zero the labels are pure noise and no learning is possible. In practice, ϵ [0.05, 0.2] covers most real annotation noise levels.

sections.title: Robust DPO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer # corresponds to epsilon = 0.1 config = DPOConfig ( loss_type = " robust " , label_smoothing =0.1 , beta =0.1 , ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: 6.9.3 TR-DPO – Trust Region DPO
sections.content: Stale Reference Model Problem Standard DPO uses fixed reference model πref throughout training. As the policy πθ improves, the KL penalty β log(πθ/πref) grows, eventually dominating the loss and preventing further improvement. TR-DPO [179] periodically updates the reference model to track the current policy. TR-DPO updates the reference model using an exponential moving average (EMA): where α (0, 1) is the mixup coefficient. This is applied every Tsync gradient steps. ref α π(t) π(t+1) θ + (1 α) π(t) ref, H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems TR-DPO in TRL from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer # standard DPO loss # enable TR - DPO # alpha : how much of current policy to mix in # T_sync : sync every 512 steps config = DPOConfig ( loss_type = " sigmoid " , sync_ref_model = True , _ el_ xup _ ph =0.6 , _ ode l_ sy nc _ ste =512 , beta =0.1 , ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , ) When to Use TR-DPO Long training runs where the policy drifts far from the initial reference. When you observe the DPO loss plateauing early due to KL penalty domination. Iterative DPO pipelines where new preference data is collected from the current policy. Set α close to 1 for fast reference updates; close to 0 for slow updates.

sections.title: 6.9.4 EXO – Exact Optimisation
sections.content: DPOs KL Direction Problem DPO is derived by solving for the optimal policy under reverse KL constraint. However, the resulting loss actually optimises forward KL objective in the reward space, which is the wrong direction. EXO [180] corrects this by using reverse KL probability matching, which is the theoretically correct objective for alignment. EXO minimises the reverse KL between the model distribution and the target (reward-optimal) distribution: LEXO = Eyπθ (cid:20) log πθ(yq) p(yq) (cid:21) , where p(yq) πref(yq) exp(r(y, q)/β) is the optimal policy. In practice, EXO approximates this using the available preference pairs: (cid:20) LEXO log σ (cid:18) β log πref(ywq) πθ(ywq) β log πref(ylq) πθ(ylq) (cid:19)(cid:21) . Note the swapped roles of πθ and πref compared to DPO.

sections.title: EXO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer config = DPOConfig ( loss_type = " exo_pair " , beta =0.1 , ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = config , H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: 6.9.5 NCA – Noise Contrastive Alignment
sections.content: Likelihood Collapse in DPO known failure mode of DPO is likelihood collapse: the model learns to decrease the probability of the losing response but also decreases the probability of the winning response (since the loss only cares about the difference). NCA [181] adds an absolute likelihood term to prevent this. NCA reframes alignment as noise-contrastive estimation. The loss has three terms: LNCA = log σ(rw) 1 2 log σ(rw) 1 2 log σ(rl), where ry = β log(πθ(yq)/πref(yq)) is the implicit reward. The first term encourages high reward for yw; the second and third terms penalise high reward for both yw and yl (preventing collapse). NCA in TRL from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer config = DPOConfig ( loss_type = " nca_pair " , beta =0.01 , # small beta : absolute likelihood term dominates ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: When to Use NCA
sections.content: When you observe the winning response probability decreasing during DPO training. For tasks where absolute response quality matters, not just relative ranking. Use small β (e.g., 0.01) to give the absolute likelihood term more weight.

sections.title: Hinge Loss as a Simpler Alternative
sections.content: The log-sigmoid loss in DPO is smooth but can be slow to converge when the margin is large. SLiC-HF [182] uses hinge loss, which is zero when the margin exceeds threshold and linear otherwise. This is simpler, faster, and surprisingly competitive. The SLiC-HF loss is: LSLiC = max (cid:18) 0, δ β log πθ(ywq) πref(ywq) + β log πθ(ylq) πref(ylq) (cid:19) , where δ is the margin threshold. When the model already assigns margin of δ between winning and losing responses, the loss is zero. SLiC-HF in TRL from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer config = DPOConfig ( 155 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems loss_type = " hinge " , beta =0.1 , ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: 6.9.7 Iterative RPO – Reasoning Preference Optimisation
sections.content: DPO Forgets How to Generate Standard DPO trains the model to discriminate between winning and losing responses. But for reasoning tasks, the model also needs to generate correct reasoning traces. model that can discriminate but not generate is useless at inference time. RPO adds an NLL (negative log-likelihood) term on the winning response to ensure the model learns to generate it. The RPO loss combines DPO and SFT: where LNLL(yw) = log πθ(ywq) is the standard language modelling loss on the winning response. LRPO = λ1LDPO(yw, yl) + λ2LNLL(yw),

sections.title: Iterative RPO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer # Standard DPO loss # NLL regularisation weight ( RPO ) config = DPOConfig ( loss_type = " sigmoid " , rpo_alpha =1.0 , beta =0.1 , ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: When to Use RPO
sections.content: Reasoning tasks (math, code, logic) where the model must generate step-by-step solutions. When DPO training causes the model to lose fluency or generation quality. Iterative pipelines: generate rollouts, label them, train with RPO, repeat. The NLL term acts as regulariser, preventing the policy from collapsing.

sections.title: 6.9.8 SimPO – Simple Preference Optimisation
sections.content: Reference-Free Preference Learning DPO requires reference model to compute the implicit reward. This doubles memory usage and adds complexity. SimPO [183] eliminates the reference model by using the average log-probability of the response as an implicit reward, with length normalisation term to prevent the model from preferring short responses. SimPO defines the implicit reward as: 156 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems rSimPO(yq) = β log πθ(yq), and the loss as: (cid:20) LSimPO = log σ (cid:18) β yw log πθ(ywq) log πθ(ylq) γ (cid:19)(cid:21) , β yl where γ > 0 is target reward margin that ensures the winning response has strictly higher reward than the losing response by at least γ. SimPO vs DPO vs ORPO DPO: uses reference model; ratio-based implicit reward. ORPO: reference-free; adds odds-ratio term to SFT loss. SimPO: reference-free; length-normalised log-prob reward + margin. SimPO is simpler than DPO (no reference model) and more principled than ORPO. The length normalisation in SimPO is critical: without it, the model prefers long responses.
