SimPO in TRL from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer config = DPOConfig ( loss_type = " simpo " , simpo_gamma =0.5 , beta =2.5 , # No ref_model needed ! # target reward margin gamma # length normalisation coefficient ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = None , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , # SimPO is reference - free ) 157 Chapter 7 GRPO Group Relative Policy Optimization Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [14] is reinforcement learning algorithm designed specifically for language models that eliminates the need for separate value network (critic). Introduced by DeepSeek as part of their DeepSeekMath work and later scaled to DeepSeek-R1 [15], GRPO has rapidly become the dominant RL method for LLM trainingadopted by most open-source alignment frameworks (TRL, OpenRLHF, veRL) as the default algorithm. The core idea is deceptively simple: instead of training neural network to predict expected reward (the critic in PPO), GRPO estimates it empirically by generating multiple responses to the same prompt and using the groups reward statistics as baseline. This removes an entire model from memory, halves the engineering complexity, andsurprisinglyoften outperforms PPO because empirical baselines are more accurate than poorly-trained value function. GRPO is particularly effective for: Reasoning tasks with verifiable rewards (math, code) where binary correctness provides clean signal. Large models (70B+) where the memory savings from removing the critic are critical. Multi-turn and agentic settings where value estimation across tool calls is intractable. This chapter covers GRPOs motivation, algorithm, key variants (Dr. GRPO, DAPO, 2-GRPO, GDPO), and practical implementation with TRL.

sections.title: 7.1 Motivation
sections.content: PPOs value model (critic) has three major problems for language: 1. Memory: The value head shares the policy backbone (140GB for 70B). Doubles memory if separate. 2. Accuracy: Predicting expected reward for partial sequence is extremely hard. The value function is often wrong wrong advantages wrong gradient direction. 3. Training: Value head needs many samples to converge. During early RL, it gives noisy predictions that destabilize policy learning. GRPOs key insight [14]: Instead of learning (s), estimate it empirically from group of samples. Generate responses to the same prompt, compute their rewards, and use the group statistics as the baseline. 158 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 7.2 Algorithm
sections.content: 1. For each prompt x, sample completions: {y1, . . . , yG} πθ(x) 2. Score each: ri = R(x, yi) 3. Normalize within group: ˆAi = riµG σG where µG = 1 rj, σG = std({rj}) 4. Apply PPO-style clipped update using these advantages ˆAi = ri µG σG , = min (cid:16) rt(θ) ˆAi, clip(rt(θ), 1ϵ) ˆAi (cid:17)i βDKL[πθπref] (7.1) Why Group Normalization Works The group mean approximates (s): If you sample enough responses to the same prompt, their average reward is Monte Carlo estimate of the expected reward = value function. Above mean = good move: ˆAi > 0 means this response is better than average for this prompt. Reinforce it. Below mean = bad move: ˆAi < 0 means worse than average. Suppress it. Normalization: Dividing by σG ensures advantages are scale-invariant across prompts with different reward ranges. DeepSeek-R1 breakthrough [15]: Pure GRPO with binary correctness rewards (r = 1 if answer correct, = 0 otherwise) trained on math/code spontaneously developed chain-of-thought reasoning, self-verification, and error correction without any explicit instruction to do so. Figure 7.1: GRPO in action: G=5 responses are sampled for single math prompt. Three are correct (r=1), two are wrong (r=0). The group mean µG=0.6 acts as the baseline; correct responses receive positive advantage (reinforced), wrong ones receive negative advantage (suppressed).

sections.title: 7.3 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The following shows minimal working example using HuggingFace TRL. from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM , AutoTokenizer model = ut del orC usalLM . from_pretrained ( " Qwen / Qwen2 .5 -7 - Instruct " , torch_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , attn_im plement at io = " fl ash_at tentio n_2 " ) tokenizer = AutoTokenizer . from_pretrained ( " Qwen / Qwen2 .5 -7 - Instruct " ) grpo_config = GRPOConfig ( output_dir = " ./ grpo_output " , num_generations =8 , temperature =1.0 , max _com ple on_ gt =2048 , beta =0.04 , learning_rate =1 -6 , # = group size # High temp for diversity within group # Max response length # KL penalty coefficient 159 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems # Prompts per device ( x8 gens = 16 responses ) _ i _ i _ c _ e =2 , d t _ u a n _ p =8 , num_train_epochs =2 , bf16 = True , d nt _ ch ec kp oi n = True , max_grad_norm =0.5 , logging_steps =10 , # vLLM generation for speed ( critical for GRPO due to 8 generation ) use_vllm = True , m _ _ o _ l t =0.7 , ) # Reward function : binary correctness for math def reward_fn ( completions , prompts , ** kwargs ) : " " " Return list of floats : 1.0 if correct , 0.0 if wrong . " " " rewards = [] for completion , prompt in zip ( completions , prompts ) : answer = extract_answer ( completion ) expected = get_ground_truth ( prompt ) rewards . append (1.0 if answer == expected else 0.0) return rewards # Can combine multiple reward functions ! def format_reward_fn ( completions , ** kwargs ) : " " " Bonus for using proper LaTeX formatting . " " " return [0.5 if " boxed { " in else 0.0 for in completions ] trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , args = grpo_config , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn , format_reward_fn ] , train_dataset = math_dataset , tokenizer = tokenizer , ) trainer . train () # Multi - objective !

sections.title: When to Use
sections.content: 2 Very noisy (coin flip) Low

sections.title: Moderate
sections.content: 8 Good (standard)

sections.title: Extreme
sections.content: Never recommended too noisy for stable learning Quick experiments, easy tasks (pass rate > 50%) Default. Good balance for most tasks Hard tasks (pass rate < 20%), need many attempts to get positives Only if you have massive compute and very hard task Critical: Group Must Contain Both Successes and Failures If all responses are correct (ri = 1 i): all advantages = 0, no learning signal! If all wrong: same problem. The prompts difficulty must match models capability. Goldilocks rule: Filter prompts to 2080% pass rate for current model. Re-filter every 500 steps as model improves. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 7.5.1 Diversity in GRPO Groups
sections.content: Mode Collapse in RL Training Without diversity pressure, RL-trained LLMs collapse to narrow set of high-reward responses: The model learns one template answer for each question type Entropy drops rapidly; the model becomes deterministic Reward hacking becomes easier (narrow outputs are easier to exploit) Generalization suffers: the model memorizes reward patterns, not reasoning The KL penalty βDKL[πθπref] is the primary diversity mechanism, but its not sufficient alone. GRPO Group Diversity GRPO generates responses per prompt and uses within-group ranking. Diversity within the group is critical: High temperature (τ = 0.81.0): Ensures varied responses for meaningful comparison Large (816): More samples = more likely to include both good and bad approaches DAPOs No Repeat penalty: Rejects duplicate responses within group to force exploration If all responses are identical: advantage is zero, no learning signal If responses are too diverse (random): reward signal is noisy, slow learning Sweet spot: Temperature that gives distinct approaches while staying on-topic. Table 7.1: Diversity-promoting methods for RL training.

sections.title:
KL penalty
Rejection sampling
sections.content: Best-of-N

sections.title: How It Promotes Diversity
sections.content: Add αH(πθ) to the reward. Directly penalizes low-entropy (deterministic) policies. βDKL[πθπref] prevents collapse toward single mode. Generate many candidates, keep top-k by reward. Naturally selects for diverse high-quality responses. At inference: generate responses, score all, return the best. Diversity comes from sampling. DPO with diverse pairs Train on pairs where chosen/rejected differ in approach, not just Multi-reward quality. Use multiple reward models (safety, helpfulness, code quality). Prevents collapsing to one dimension. Diversity vs. Quality Tradeoff More diversity is not always better: Too much diversity (high entropy) = random, unhelpful responses Too little diversity (low entropy) = repetitive, reward-hacked responses Monitor: Track response entropy, unique n-gram ratio, and reward distribution width during training. If all three are dropping simultaneously, you have collapse problem. 161 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Verbalized Sampling for RL Data Collection Post-training alignment (RLHF, DPO) often reduces output diversity due to typicality bias: human annotators systematically prefer familiar, typical text over novel alternatives. This mode collapse is data-level phenomenon, not purely algorithmic. Verbalized Sampling (VS) [111] is training-free prompting strategy that circumvents this collapse by asking the model to explicitly verbalize probability distribution over multiple responses in single generation. Verbalized Sampling Core Idea Instead of sampling single response (which collapses to the mode), prompt the model to output multiple candidate responses along with their probabilities: Generate 5 jokes about coffee and their corresponding probabilities. The model produces list like: 1. Joke (probability: 0.35) 2. Joke (probability: 0.25) 3. Joke (probability: 0.20) 4. Joke (probability: 0.12) 5. Joke (probability: 0.08) Then sample from this verbalized distribution. Because the model explicitly represents the full distribution (not just the argmax), lower-probability but creative/diverse responses become accessible. # Verbalized Sampling : prompt model to output distribution def verbalized_sample ( model , tokenizer , task , =5) : prompt = ( " { task } " " Generate { } different responses and assign probability " " to each ( probabilities should sum to 1.0) . " " Format : [ response ] ( probability : . XX ) " ) output = model . generate ( tokenizer ( prompt , return_tensors = " pt " ) . input_ids , max_new_tokens =1024 , temperature =0.7 , do_sample = True , ) # Parse responses and probabilities from output responses , probs = s _ b z _ t u n ( tokenizer . decode ( output [0]) ) # Sample from the verbalized distribution import random chosen = random . choices ( responses , weights = probs , =1) [0] return chosen

sections.title: Why Verbalized Sampling Works
sections.content: Bypasses mode collapse: Standard sampling from aligned models heavily concentrates on one or two safe responses. VS forces the model to articulate alternatives it knows but wouldnt normally surface. Diversity is semantic: Unlike temperature scaling (lexical noise), VS produces genuinely different approachesthe model reasons about distinct options. Scales with capability: More capable models produce better-calibrated verbalized 162 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems distributionsthey benefit more from VS (1.62.1 diversity gain in creative writing). Training-free: No fine-tuning or modified decoding; works with any instruction-following model at inference time. For GRPO: Use VS to generate the response candidates per promptensures the group contains semantically diverse approaches rather than surface-level variations. Before diving into the extensions, let us briefly recap the base GRPO algorithm established in the previous sections. The core mechanismsampling group of completions, normalizing their rewards, and applying clipped policy gradientis elegant in its simplicity. However, practitioners quickly discovered specific failure modes: pretraining bias diluting gradients (Dr. GRPO), symmetric clipping limiting exploration (DAPO), wasteful large group sizes (2-GRPO), and reward-scale imbalance in multi-objective settings (GDPO). The following sections address each of these in turn. GRPO Baseline Recap Given prompt q, sample completions {o1, . . . , oG} from the current policy πθ. Compute rewards {r1, . . . , rG} and normalise: ˆAi = ri µr σr + ϵ , µr = 1 i=1 ri, σr = t 1 (ri µr)2. i=1 The clipped surrogate loss (per token) is: LGRPO =

sections.title:
1
G
sections.content: G i=1 1 oi oi t=1 (cid:16) min ρi,t ˆAi, clip(ρi,t, 1ϵ, 1+ϵ) ˆAi (cid:17) , where ρi,t = πθ(oi,tq, oi,<t) / πold(oi,tq, oi,<t).

sections.title: 7.5.2 DAPO – Dynamic Adaptive Policy Optimization
sections.content: Why DAPO? Base GRPO uses symmetric clipping: the policy is equally constrained whether it wants to increase or decrease the probability of token. But exploration and exploitation have different risk profiles. Increasing the probability of good token is generally safe; suppressing token that happened to appear in bad completion can be catastrophically wrong if the token itself is neutral. DAPO [184] introduces five targeted fixes that together substantially improve training stability and final performance. Component 1 Asymmetric Clipping (Clip-Higher) Standard PPO/GRPO clips the importance ratio symmetrically at [1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ]. DAPO replaces this with an asymmetric band: clipDAPO(ρ, A) = (clip(ρ, 1 ϵ, 1 + ϵhigh) clip(ρ, 1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ) if > 0 if 0 where ϵhigh > ϵ (typical values: ϵ = 0.2, ϵhigh = 0.28). When the advantage is positive the policy is allowed to move further toward the good token; when the advantage is negative the usual conservative clipping applies to avoid over-suppression. Component 2 Token-Level Loss Aggregation Base GRPO divides the loss by the number of sequences G. DAPO divides by the total number of tokens across all sequences: 163 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Ltoken = 1 i=1 oi PG oi i= t=1 min(cid:0)ρi,t ˆAi, clipDAPO(ρi,t, ˆAi) ˆAi (cid:1). This prevents long completions from dominating the gradient signal simply because they contain more tokens. Component 3 Overlong Filtering When completion is truncated (no EOS token within the maximum length budget), it provides misleading signal: the model is penalised for tokens that were generated correctly but happened to appear before the truncation boundary. DAPO masks these completions entirely: mi = 1[EOS oi], Lfiltered = PG i=1 mi PG t( ) . i=1 mioi Component 4 Soft Overlong Punishment Rather than hard mask, softer variant applies length penalty that grows smoothly as completions approach the maximum length Lmax: ri ri λ max (cid:18) 0, oi Lcache Lmax Lcache (cid:19) , where Lcache is safe length threshold. Component 5 Dynamic Sampling DAPO re-samples prompts whose entire group of completions receives the same reward (all correct or all incorrect), because such groups contribute zero gradient after normalisation. This keeps the effective batch size stable throughout training.

sections.title: DAPO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( # Clip - Higher # Asymmetric clipping epsilon =0.2 , epsilon_high =0.28 , # Token - level loss loss_type = " dapo " , # Overlong filtering k _ n e _ p i = True , # Generation budget max _co ple on_ gt =1024 , num_generations =8 , # Note : DAPO loss internally handles zero - variance group filtering # enables token - level aggregation ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , ) trainer . train ()

sections.title: When to Use DAPO
sections.content: Long-form reasoning tasks where completions frequently hit the length limit. 164 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Any setting where you observe reward variance collapsing mid-training. When base GRPO shows instability (loss spikes, entropy collapse). Recommended as drop-in improvement over base GRPO for most tasks.

sections.title: 7.5.3 GSPO – Group Sequence Policy Optimization
sections.content: The Off-Policy Problem GRPO clips importance ratios per token. But sequence of 500 tokens can have product of per-token ratios that is astronomically large or small, even if each individual ratio is within [1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ]. When training for multiple gradient steps on the same batch (off-policy), this mismatch grows rapidly and the clipping bound becomes meaningless at the sequence level. GSPO [185] defines sequence-level importance weight as the geometric mean of per-token ratios, which equals the oi-th root of the full sequence probability ratio: si(θ) = (cid:18) πθ(oi q) πold(oi q) (cid:19)1/oi = exp 1 oi oi t=1 log πθ(oi,tq, oi,<t) πold(oi,tq, oi,<t) . This is the length-normalised sequence probability ratio. The GSPO loss clips this single scalar per sequence: LGSPO =

sections.title:
1
G
sections.content: G i=1 (cid:16) min si(θ) ˆAi, clip(si(θ), 1ϵ, 1+ϵ) ˆAi (cid:17) .

sections.title: GSPO vs GRPO Clipping
sections.content: GRPO: clips each of the oi per-token ratios independently. sequence can have all ratios within bounds yet have product ratio of 1050. GSPO: clips the geometric mean once per sequence. Guarantees the sequence-level policy shift is bounded. GSPO is theoretically correct for off-policy IS; GRPO is an approximation.

sections.title: GSPO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( # Sequence - level importance sampling o n _ p li _ e = " sequence " , # Off - policy : reuse each batch for multiple gradient steps step s_pe r_ ge ne ati =4 , num_generations =8 , epsilon =0.2 , # GSPO mode ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , ) When to Use GSPO GSPO is most beneficial when steps_per_generation > 1 (off-policy training). For purely on-policy training (steps_per_generation = 1) the difference from GRPO is negligible. Off-policy 165 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems training can dramatically reduce generation cost (the most expensive step), making GSPO + off-policy strong efficiency choice.

sections.title: 7.5.4 Dr. GRPO – Debiased Reward GRPO
sections.content: The Pretraining Bias Problem Standard GRPO normalises advantages within group, but the pretraining distribution introduces systematic bias: tokens that are common in pretraining data receive large gradients even when they carry no task-relevant information. Dr. GRPO [186] identifies and corrects this bias, focusing gradient signal on informative tokens. Dr. GRPO modifies the per-token gradient weight to account for the tokens marginal contribution to the reward signal. Tokens that the model already assigns high probability to (regardless of the reward) are down-weighted: wi,t = ˆAi (cid:0)1 πref(oi,tq, oi,<t)(cid:1), where πref is the reference (pretrained) model. This is form of token efficiency: the gradient is concentrated on tokens where the policy genuinely needs to change. Dr. GRPO in TRL from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( loss_type = " dr_grpo " , num_generations =8 , beta =0.04 , # KL penalty coefficient ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , # required for token weighting ) When to Use Dr. GRPO When training on tasks with large vocabulary mismatch between pretraining and RL. When you observe that common filler tokens dominate the gradient. Pairs well with reference model that is close to the initial policy. 7.5.5 2-GRPO Minimal Two-Rollout GRPO It Takes Two Insight The It Takes Two paper [187] demonstrates empirically and theoretically that GRPO with = 2 (just two completions per prompt) matches or exceeds GRPO with = 16 on most reasoning benchmarks. This is surprising why would fewer samples be sufficient? The key insight is that GRPOs effectiveness does not primarily come from accurate advantage estimation (which requires large G). Instead, it comes from an implicit contrastive objective that is structurally similar to DPO: L2-GRPO E(o+,o)πθ log σ β log " πθ(o+q) πold(o+q) β log πθ(oq) πold(oq) !# , 166 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems where o+ is the higher-reward completion and the lower-reward one. With = 2, this contrastive structure is explicit. With = 16, the same signal is present but diluted by redundant pairs. Compute Savings from 2-GRPO = 2 vs = 16: 8 less generation compute. Generation is typically the bottleneck (6080% of wall-clock time). Total training speedup: approximately 46 end-to-end. No accuracy loss on GSM8K, MATH, and code benchmarks. 2-GRPO in TRL from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( num_generations =2 , loss_type = " grpo " , epsilon =0.2 , # With =2 , batch size must be at least 2 * nu m_ pr om pt s_ pe r_ st ep _ i _ i _ c _ e =2 , # The key change -- just two rollouts # Standard GRPO loss is fine ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , ) Caveats of 2-GRPO With = 2, advantage normalisation is over only two values, so the normalised advantages are always {+1, 1} (or {0, 0} if rewards are equal). This means the gradient magnitude is fixed regardless of the reward gap. For tasks where the magnitude of the reward difference matters (e.g., partial credit), larger may still be beneficial.

sections.title: The Brittleness of Hard Clipping
sections.content: PPO-style clipping creates discontinuous gradient: the gradient is zero outside the clip band and non-zero inside. This cliff edge can cause instability near the boundary and makes the trust region sensitive to the choice of ϵ. SAPO [188] replaces the hard clip with smooth, temperature-controlled gate function. SAPO replaces the min(ρA, clip(ρ, ) A) objective with smooth surrogate: LSAPO(ρ, A) = σ σ (cid:19) (cid:19) (cid:18) ρ 1 τ+ (cid:18) 1 ρ τ ρ if > 0 ρ if where σ is the sigmoid function and τ+, τ are asymmetric temperature parameters. higher temperature produces softer gate (more exploration); lower temperature approaches hard clipping.

sections.title: SAPO Temperature Intuition
sections.content: τ+ = 1.0: moderate gate for positive advantages (allow exploration). τ = 1.05: slightly softer gate for negative advantages (avoid over-suppression). 167 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems As τ 0: recovers hard PPO clipping. As τ : recovers unclipped policy gradient. SAPO in TRL from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer # tau_ + for positive advantages # tau_ - for negative advantages config = GRPOConfig ( loss_type = " sapo " , o _ tem pe ra tu e_p =1.0 , o _ tem pe ra tu e_n =1.05 , num_generations =8 , ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: The Silent vLLM Probability Mismatch
sections.content: When using vLLM for fast generation, the log-probabilities returned by vLLM differ from those computed during the training forward pass [189]. This is not bug it arises from different CUDA kernels, different floating-point precision, and different attention implementations (e.g., FlashAttention vs PagedAttention). The mismatch silently breaks the on-policy assumption: the old policy probabilities used to compute importance ratios are wrong, leading to biased gradient estimates. Truncated Importance Sampling (TIS) TIS corrects the bias by multiplying the gradient by truncated correction factor: wTIS(oi) = min (cid:18) C, πtrain(oiq) πvllm(oiq) (cid:19) , where πtrain is the probability from the training forward pass and πvllm is the probability reported by vLLM. The truncation at prevents extreme corrections from destabilising training. Masked Importance Sampling (MIS) MIS takes harder approach: it zeros out the gradient for any sequence where the correction ratio exceeds threshold C: wMIS(oi) = 1 (cid:20) πtrain(oiq) πvllm(oiq) (cid:21) . This is more conservative but avoids the risk of large (even truncated) correction weights. Sequence-Level vs Token-Level IS Both TIS and MIS can be applied at the token level or the sequence level: Sequence-level: compute the ratio as the geometric mean over all tokens (as in GSPO). Theoretically correct but higher variance. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Token-level: compute separate ratio for each token. Biased (the product of per-token corrections is not the sequence correction) but lower variance. TIS and MIS in TRL from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer # Truncated IS correction for vLLM probability mismatch config_tis = GRPOConfig ( use_vllm = True , m _ o n _ p g _ r i = True , m _ o n _ p g _ e = " sequence_truncate " , m _ o n _ p g _ =5.0 , # TIS # threshold ) # Masked IS correction config_mis = GRPOConfig ( use_vllm = True , m _ o n _ p g _ r i = True , m _ o n _ p g _ e = " sequence_mask " , m _ o n _ p g _ =3.0 , # MIS ) When to Use TIS/MIS Always consider enabling when using vLLM for generation. TIS is preferred when the mismatch is small (same model, different precision). MIS is preferred when the mismatch is large or unpredictable. Sequence-level IS is theoretically preferred; token-level is practical compromise.

sections.title: Principled Reward Reshaping
sections.content: Most GRPO variants modify the clipping mechanism heuristically. VESPO derives principled reward-reshaping kernel from variational inference framework, treating policy optimisation as approximate posterior inference. VESPO [190] derives resulting kernel that is smooth, asymmetric, and naturally handles staleness in asynchronous or off-policy training. VESPO derives weighting function (τ ) for each trajectory τ from the variational objective. The final gradient weight takes the form: g(τ ) = (τ )k exp(cid:0)λ(1 (τ ))(cid:1), where (τ ) = πθ(τ )/πold(τ ) is the sequence-level importance weight, controls the sharpness of the weighting, and λ controls the exponential decay for stale (low-weight) trajectories. This kernel: Is smooth everywhere (no discontinuous gradient at clip boundaries). Naturally down-weights stale trajectories (W 1) via the exponential term. Is asymmetric: high-weight trajectories (W > 1) are treated differently from low-weight ones.

sections.title: VESPO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( loss_type = " vespo " , 169 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems # sharpness exponent ( positive advantages ) # staleness decay ( positive advantages ) # off - policy ; VESPO handles staleness vespo_k_pos =2.0 , vespo_lambda_pos =3.0 , num_generations =8 , p _pe r_ ge ne ati =2 , ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: 7.5.9 DPPO – Direct Policy Divergence Policy Optimization
sections.content: The Problem with Ratio Clipping PPOs ratio clipping is proxy for constraining the KL divergence between old and new policy. But the proxy is imperfect: clipping over-penalises low-probability tokens (where small absolute change in probability corresponds to large ratio change) and under-penalises high-probability tokens (where large absolute change corresponds to small ratio change). DPPO [191] replaces ratio clipping with direct divergence estimates. DPPO computes the trust region constraint directly using either Total Variation (TV) or KL divergence between the old and new policy distributions: LDPPO = Eh ˆA πθ(oq) 1[D(πθπold) δ] , where is the chosen divergence measure. In practice, DPPO approximates this with token-level binary or top-k masks: binary_tv: mask tokens where πθ πold > δ. binary_kl: mask tokens where πθ log(πθ/πold) > δ. topk_tv: keep only the top-k tokens by TV contribution. topk_kl: keep only the top-k tokens by KL contribution. DPPO Conceptual Implementation DPPO is not yet available as built-in TRL trainer. custom implementation would use GRPOTrainer with modified loss that clips based on distributional divergence (TV or KL) rather than the standard probability ratio: # Pseudocode : DPPO requires custom trainer subclass from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( num_generations =8 , beta =0.04 , ) # Override the loss computation to use distributional clipping : # clip when TV ( pi_new pi_old ) > delta , rather than when # pi_new / pi_old exceeds [1 - eps , 1+ eps ] DPPO is Research-Stage DPPO is recent research contribution and is not yet integrated into mainstream RL libraries. It is most useful when you observe that standard ratio clipping is failing (e.g., on tasks with highly skewed token probability distributions). 170 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 7.5.10 ScaleRL and CISPO
sections.content: Scaling Laws for RL The ScaleRL paper [192] conducts systematic study of what makes RL training for LLMs scale effectively. The key finding is that two modifications batch-level reward scaling and DAPO-style token-level loss together unlock strong performance at scale, while neither alone is sufficient. CISPO (Clipped IS Policy Optimization) is the resulting algorithm. Batch-Level Reward Scaling Standard GRPO normalises rewards within group of completions for single prompt. CISPO normalises rewards across the entire batch: ˆAi = ri µbatch σbatch + ϵ , where µbatch and σbatch are computed over all rewards in the current training batch. This provides more stable baseline and prevents any single prompt from dominating the gradient. CISPO Loss CISPO combines batch-level scaling with DAPOs token-level loss aggregation and asymmetric clipping: LCISPO = 1 i,t mi,t oi i=1 t= mi,t min(cid:0)ρi,t ˆAi, clipDAPO(ρi,t, ˆAi) ˆAi (cid:1), where mi,t is the overlong-filtering mask.

sections.title: CISPO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer # batch - level reward normalisation # epsilon_max for CISPO ( ScaleRL paper ) config = GRPOConfig ( loss_type = " cispo " , scale_rewards = " batch " , k _ n e _ p i = True , epsilon =0.2 , epsilon_high =5.0 , num_generations =8 , ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ reward_fn ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: ScaleRL Key Findings
sections.content: 1. Batch-level reward scaling alone: modest improvement. 2. Token-level loss alone: modest improvement. 3. Both together: synergistic significantly better than either alone. 4. Larger batch sizes benefit more from batch-level scaling. 5. CISPO is the recommended default for large-scale RL training. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 7.5.11 GDPO – Group Reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization
sections.content: The Multi-Reward Collapse Problem In multi-objective RL (e.g., optimising for both correctness and format), standard GRPO normalises the combined reward. If one reward has much higher variance than another, it dominates the normalised advantage, effectively ignoring the other reward. This is advantage collapse: the lowvariance reward contributes near-zero gradient. GDPO [193] normalises each reward independently before aggregating. The core mechanism normalises each reward independently before aggregating: ˆA(i) = r(i) µn σn + ϵ , ˆA(i) = n=1 wn ˆA(i) , where r(i) is the n-th reward for completion i, µn and σn are the mean and standard deviation of reward within the group, and wn are user-specified weights. GDPO vs Standard Multi-Reward GRPO Standard: ˆA(i) = wnr(i) µcombined σcombined . High-variance rewards dominate. GDPO: normalise each reward separately, then combine. Each reward contributes proportionally to its weight wn. GDPO is essential when rewards have very different scales or variances.

sections.title: GDPO in TRL
sections.content: from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( t _ e v _ r t = " normalize_then_sum " , reward_weights =[1.0 , 0.5] , num_generations =8 , # weights for [ correctness , format ] ) def correctness_rew ar ( completions , ** kwargs ) : return [1.0 if is_correct ( ) else 0.0 for in completions ] def format_reward ( completions , ** kwargs ) : return [0.1 if has_good_format ( ) else 0.0 for in completions ] trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ correctness_reward , format_reward ] , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: 7.5.12 GOPO – Group Ordinal Policy Optimization
sections.content: GOPO [194] starts from simple observation: reward models are trained with pairwise comparisons (is better than B?), so only the rank order of their outputs is trustworthythe raw numeric scores carry no inherent meaning. Yet GRPO feeds those raw magnitudes directly into the advantage calculation. For tasks with non-verifiable rewardssummarization, open-ended chat, instruction followingthis mismatch introduces noise, because gap of 0.6 reward points might reflect genuine quality in one region of the output space and mean nothing in another. Key Insight: Discard reward magnitudes entirely. Use only the ordinal ranking of rewards within group. Algorithm: Given group of responses {o1, . . . , oN } with rewards {r1, . . . , rN }: 172 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems 1. Rank responses by reward: assign rank rank(oi) {1, . . . , } (1 = worst, = best). 2. Replace raw advantages with rank-based scores: ˆAGOPO = (cid:19) (cid:18) rank(oi) (7.2) where is monotonic transformation (e.g., linear mapping to [1, 1] or quantile normalization). 3. Apply PPO-style clipped objective using rank-based advantages. Comparison with GRPO: Aspect Advantage signal GRPO ˆAi = (ri µ)/σ (uses magnitudes) GOPO ˆAi = (ranki/N ) (uses ordinal rank only) Sensitivity to reward scale High miscalibrated RM Best for scores distort advantages Verifiable well-calibrated) rewards (binary, None invariant to monotonic reward transformations Non-verifiable rewards (RM-based, noisy magnitudes) Empirical gains (over GRPO on non-verifiable tasks): Reward curves (both training and held-out) sit above GRPO throughout optimization Win-rates judged by separate LLM evaluator improve at most training checkpoints Convergence is markedly fastermatching GRPOs final quality with fewer gradient steps The advantage grows as the reward model becomes noisier or more poorly calibrated When to Use GOPO vs. GRPO Use GRPO: When rewards are verifiable and exact (math correctness, code tests pass/fail, binary signals). Magnitudes carry meaningful information. Use GOPO: When rewards come from learned reward model on subjective tasks (helpfulness, style, safety). The RMs relative ordering is trustworthy but its absolute scores are arbitrary. 173 Chapter

sections.title: Preference Optimization Variants
sections.content: This chapter covers the family of methods that extend or replace DPO with different objectives, data assumptions, or architectural trade-offs. Each addresses specific limitation of standard offline DPO: distribution shift (Online DPO), the need for paired data (KTO), overfitting to noisy labels (IPO), reference model memory cost (ORPO), or training complexity (Best-of-N).

sections.title: 8.1.1 Motivation
sections.content: Standard DPOs primary limitation: the preference data was generated by different model (often an older checkpoint or even different model family). As training progresses, the policy generates text that looks nothing like the training pairs the loss is optimizing on an irrelevant distribution. Online DPO solution [195]: Generate fresh preference pairs from the current policy at every step, judge them with reward model, then apply the DPO loss.

sections.title: 8.1.2 Algorithm
sections.content: 1. Generate responses per prompt from current πθ 2. Score all responses with reward model rϕ 3. Create pairs: highest-scoring = chosen, lowest-scoring = rejected 4. Apply DPO loss on these fresh pairs 5. Repeat (new generation every step) Online DPO = Best of Both Worlds From DPO: simple supervised loss, no value function, no GAE, stable optimization From PPO: on-policy data, self-improvement beyond dataset, no distribution shift Key difference from GRPO: uses DPO loss (pair-based) instead of PPO loss (per-sample advantage) Trade-off : Needs reward model (DPO doesnt), but no value head (PPO does). Middle ground complexity.

sections.title: 8.1.3 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The following shows minimal working example using HuggingFace TRL. 174 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems from trl import OnlineDPOConfig , OnlineDPOTrainer from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM , o e r u e s i i model = ut ode For ausalLM . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " , torch_dtype = torch . bfloat16 ) reward_model = o e r u e s i i . from_pretrained ( " RLHFlow / ArmoRM - Llama3 -8 - v0 .1 " , torch_dtype = torch . bfloat16 ) onl ine_dpo_conf ig = OnlineDPOConfig ( # DPO beta ( same meaning as standard DPO ) # responses per prompt output_dir = " ./ online_dpo_output " , learning_rate =5 -7 , beta =0.1 , num_generations =4 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =4 , d t _ u a n _ p =4 , max_new_tokens =512 , temperature =0.7 , bf16 = True , num_train_epochs =1 , logging_steps =10 , ) trainer = OnlineDPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_model = reward_model , args = online_dpo_config , train_dataset = prompt_dataset , tokenizer = tokenizer , ) trainer . train ()

sections.title: Offline DPO Static pairs
sections.content: 2 (policy + reference) Online DPO Fresh from πθ

sections.title: PPO
sections.content: Fresh from πθ 3 (policy + reference + reward model) 4 (policy + reference + reward model + value head)

sections.title: Best For
sections.content: limited Quick alignment, compute When DPO plateaus, need exploration PPO clip Max quality, complex reasoning

sections.title: 8.2.1 Motivation
sections.content: DPO requires paired preferences: for the same prompt, you need both good and bad response. In practice, most feedback is unpaired: users give thumbs up/down on individual responses, with no matched pair. KTOs insight [11]: Use prospect theory (from behavioral economics). Humans feel losses more strongly than gains. thumbs down should produce stronger gradient than thumbs up.

sections.title: 8.2.2 Loss Function
sections.content: LKTO = Eyw [λw(1 v(x, yw))] + Eyl [λl v(x, yl)] (8.1) where v(x, y) = σ (cid:16) β log πθ(yx) πref(yx) zref (cid:17) , and zref is the expected KL divergence (a running baseline). 175 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems KTO Intuition via Prospect Theory Desirable responses (yw): The model gets utility from increasing their probability. But with diminishing returns once its already quite likely, dont push harder. Undesirable responses (yl): Loss aversion means the penalty for generating bad text is weighted more strongly than the reward for good text. Default: λl = 1.0, λw = 1.0, but you can set λl > λw. Key advantage: Each training example is independent! No need to find matched pairs. Can use thumbs-up/down data directly. KTO Data Format Unlike DPO which needs: {"prompt": ..., "chosen": ..., "rejected": ...} KTO only needs: {"prompt": ..., "completion": ..., "label": true/false} This means you can use: Thumbs up/down from production traffic Upvotes/downvotes from forums Human ratings binarized (45 stars = good, 12 = bad) Any per-response quality signal

sections.title: 8.2.3 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The following shows minimal working example using HuggingFace TRL. from trl import KTOConfig , KTOTrainer # Dataset format : {" prompt ": str , " completion ": str , " label ": bool } # label = True for desirable , label = False for undesirable kto_dataset = [ { " prompt " : " What 2+2? " , " completion " : " The answer is 4. " , " label " : True } , { " prompt " : " What 2+2? " , " completion " : " It might be 5. " , " label " : False } , ] kto_config = KTOConfig ( output_dir = " ./ kto_output " , beta =0.1 , desirable_weight =1.0 , undesirable_we ig ht =1.0 , aversion ) learning_rate =5 -7 , max_length =2048 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =4 , d t _ u a n _ p =4 , num_train_epochs =1 , bf16 = True , # Weight for good examples # Weight for bad examples ( increase for loss ) trainer = KTOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = kto_config , train_dataset = kto_dataset , tokenizer = tokenizer , # Or None with LoRA ) trainer . train ()

sections.title: 8.2.4 When to Choose KTO
sections.content: You have binary feedback but not matched pairs 176 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Production thumbs-up/down data at scale One class dominates (e.g., 90% good, 10% bad) KTO handles imbalance better Rapid iteration with noisy labels (more robust than DPO to noise) 8.3 IPO Identity Preference Optimization

sections.title: 8.3.1 Motivation
sections.content: DPO has degenerate solution: it can achieve zero loss by making the margin between chosen and rejected infinitely large. In practice, this means DPO overfits pushing chosen probability to 1 and rejected to 0, memorizing training data. IPOs fix [12]: Instead of log-sigmoid (which saturates), use squared loss that targets specific margin. The loss is minimized at finite gap, not at infinity.

sections.title: 8.3.2 Loss Function
sections.content: "(cid:18) LIPO = log πθ(ywx) πref(ywx) log πθ(ylx) πref(ylx) 1 2β (cid:19)2# (8.2) IPO vs DPO: Regularization Through Target Margin DPO: σ(margin) 1 optimally. Margin . No natural stopping point. IPO: Margin 1 Result: IPO is more robust to noisy labels (a mislabeled pair gets bounded influence), and generalizes better because it doesnt memorize. 2β optimally. Squared loss penalizes both too-small and too-large margins.

sections.title: 8.3.3 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The following shows minimal working example using HuggingFace TRL. from trl import DPOConfig , DPOTrainer # IPO is implemented as DPO loss_type variant in TRL ipo_config = DPOConfig ( output_dir = " ./ ipo_output " , beta =0.1 , loss_type = " ipo " , learning_rate =5 -7 , max_length =2048 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =4 , d t _ u a n _ p =8 , bf16 = True , num_train_epochs =1 , # The key difference ! ) trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = None , args = ipo_config , train_dataset = pref_dataset , tokenizer = tokenizer , peft_config = lora_config , ) trainer . train ()

sections.title: 8.3.4 When to Choose IPO over DPO
sections.content: Noisy preference data (crowdsourced, AI-judged with errors) Observing DPO overfitting (train loss 0 but eval degrades) Want more conservative, robust alignment 177 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Multiple epochs needed (DPO degrades after epoch 1; IPO is more stable)

sections.title: 8.4.1 Motivation
sections.content: All methods so far need reference model either as separate copy (doubles memory) or implicitly via LoRA. ORPO [13] eliminates the reference entirely by combining SFT and preference alignment in single loss. Key insight: Use the odds ratio of generating chosen vs rejected as the preference signal. The SFT component naturally prevents collapse (no need for KL regularization).

sections.title: 8.4.2 Loss Function
sections.content: LORPO = LSFT(yw) } {z standard NLL on chosen λ where oddsθ(yx) = Pθ(yx) 1Pθ(yx) . ORPO: SFT + Alignment in One Shot (cid:18) log log σ oddsθ(ywx) oddsθ(ylx) {z preference alignment via odds ratio } (cid:19) (8.3) SFT term: Trains the model to generate the chosen response well (standard language modeling). Odds ratio term: Additionally pushes the model to prefer chosen over rejected. The odds ratio is natural contrast that doesnt require reference model. Why no reference needed?: The SFT loss already anchors the model to reasonable text. It serves the same role as KL-to-reference in other methods. One model, one forward pass, one loss. 50% less memory!

sections.title: 8.4.3 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The following shows minimal working example using HuggingFace TRL. from trl import ORPOConfig , ORPOTrainer orpo_config = ORPOConfig ( # Odds ratio weight ( lambda ) output_dir = " ./ orpo_output " , beta =0.1 , learning_rate =5 -7 , max_length =2048 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =2 , d t _ u a n _ p =8 , bf16 = True , num_train_epochs =1 , gr ad ie nt _ ch ec kp oi n = True , ) trainer = ORPOTrainer ( model = model , args = orpo_config , train_dataset = pref_dataset , tokenizer = tokenizer , peft_config = lora_config , ) trainer . train ()

sections.title: 8.4.4 When to Choose ORPO
sections.content: # No ref_model needed ! # Same format as DPO : prompt / chosen / rejected Memory-constrained: cant afford reference model copy (saves 70140GB for 70B) 178 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Starting from base model (not SFTd yet) ORPO does SFT simultaneously Want simplest possible pipeline: one model, one loss, one training run Good preference data available from the start ORPO Limitations Less studied than DPO/PPO fewer proven recipes at 70B+ scale The SFT component means it needs high-quality chosen responses (not just relative preference) Harder to debug: two loss components can conflict See Also: SimPO SimPO [183] is another reference-free preference method that uses length-normalized logprobability as an implicit reward, eliminating the reference model entirely. It is covered in Section 6.9.8 alongside other DPO extensions due to its shared reference-free philosophy.

sections.title: 8.5.1 Motivation
sections.content: Sometimes the simplest approach wins. Best-of-N [196] requires no training at all during the RL phase just generate multiple candidates and pick the best one.

sections.title: 8.5.2 Algorithm
sections.content: 1. For each prompt, generate responses from the policy (typically = 464) 2. Score all responses with reward model 3. Select the highest-scoring response 4. (Optional) Use selected responses as SFT data for the next iteration Best-of-N response : = arg max yiπθ(x) rϕ(x, yi) (8.4) Why Best-of-N is Legitimate RL Method At inference time: Best-of-N improves output quality without changing model weights. With = 64, win-rate improves 1020% over greedy sometimes matching or exceeding PPO. As training method (Rejection Sampling Fine-Tuning / RFT): 1. Generate many responses, select best ones 2. SFT on the selected responses 3. Repeat (iterative refinement) This is how many production models are trained: simpler than PPO, almost as effective, completely stable. Theoretical connection [197]: Best-of-N implements an implicit KL-constrained policy: πBoN(yx) πθ(yx)11/N r(x, y)1/N . 179 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 8.5.3 TRL Implementation
sections.content: The following shows minimal working example using HuggingFace TRL. from transformers import pipeline import numpy as np # Inference - time Best - of - ( manual implementation ) gen_pipeline = pipeline ( " text - generation " , model = model , tokenizer = tokenizer ) def best_of_n ( prompt , =16 , temperature =0.8) : " " " Generate candidates and return the highest - reward one . " " " candidates = gen_pipeline ( prompt , m_r tur _se uences =n , temperature = temperature , do_sample = True , max_new_tokens =512 , ) scores = [ reward_model . score ( prompt , [ " generated_text " ]) for in candidates ] return candidates [ np . argmax ( scores ) ][ " generated_text " ] best_response = best_of_n ( prompt , =16) # Training : Rejection Sampling Fine - Tuning ( RFT ) from trl import SFTConfig , SFTTrainer # Step 1: Generate and filter all_responses = [] for prompt in prompts : candidates = [ generate ( prompt , temp =0.9) for _ in range (16) ] scores = [ reward_model . score ( prompt , ) for in candidates ] best_idx = np . argmax ( scores ) if scores [ best_idx ] > threshold : # Quality gate all_responses . append ({ " prompt " : prompt , " completion " : candidates [ best_idx ]}) # Step 2: SFT on best responses sft_config = SFTConfig ( output_dir = " ./ rft_output " , learning_rate =2 -5 , num_train_epochs =2 , max_seq_length =2048) trainer = SFTTrainer ( model = model , args = sft_config , train_dataset = all_responses , tokenizer = tokenizer ) trainer . train () # Step 3: Repeat from Step 1 with updated model ( iterative RFT )

sections.title: Notes
sections.content: N 1 4 Baseline +58% win-rate 16 +1015% win-rate +1520% win-rate 64 256 +1822% win-rate 1 4 16 64 256 Standard sampling Minimum useful. Good cost/quality ratio Strong. Often matches PPO quality Diminishing returns start Only for critical applications Best-of-N as Baseline Always compare your RL method against Best-of-N with the same compute budget. If PPO with 64 GPU-hours doesnt beat Best-of-N with 64 GPU-hours of generation, your PPO has bug.

sections.title: 8.6 Summary: Choosing an Alignment Method
sections.content: We have now surveyed the full landscape of preference optimization and RL-based alignment methods. This section consolidates the key trade-offs into single reference to help practitioners select the right approach for their constraints. 180 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 8.1: Cross-method comparison of alignment approaches. Method Models Data Compute Stability Best For 4 Online (gen) PPO 2 (no critic) Online (gen) GRPO Offline pairs DPO 2 Online (gen) Online DPO 3 Unpaired binary 2 KTO Offline pairs 2 IPO Offline pairs 1 ORPO Online (gen) 1+RM Best-of-N Very high High Low Medium Low Low Very low Medium Max quality, complex reasoning Math/code (verifiable rewards) Style/safety, limited compute Low Medium High Medium-High DPO without distribution shift High Very high High Perfect Production feedback, thumbs up/down Noisy labels, anti-overfitting Memory-limited, SFT+align combined Strong baseline, data generation Figure 8.1: Approximate quality vs. compute frontier. Methods above the SFT ceiling line improve beyond what supervised fine-tuning alone achieves. Position is illustrative and model-dependent. Decision Tree: Which Method to Use? 1. Do you have verifiable rewards? (math/code) GRPO 2. Do you need max quality on complex tasks? PPO 3. Do you have paired preferences? DPO (or IPO if noisy) 4. Only unpaired binary feedback? KTO 5. Memory-limited, starting from base model? ORPO 6. DPO plateauing, want on-policy? Online DPO 7. Need strong baseline quickly? Best-of-N / RFT 181 Chapter

sections.title: Reward Model Training
sections.content: Reward models are the bridge between human preferences and the RL training signal. well-trained reward model is essential for successful RLHF; poorly trained one leads to reward hacking and misaligned behaviour. This section covers the theoretical foundations, practical training techniques, and architectural choices for reward models.

sections.title: 9.1 Bradley-Terry Model – Full Derivation
sections.content: The Bradley-Terry model [198] is the standard probabilistic framework for pairwise preference learning. Given two responses y1 and y2 to prompt q, the model assumes: (y1 y2 q) = σ(r(y1, q) r(y2, q)) = er(y1,q) er(y1,q) + er(y2,q) , where : is the scalar reward function and σ is the sigmoid function.

sections.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
sections.content: Given dataset = {(q(k), y(k) , y(k) )}N k=1 of preference pairs, the MLE objective is: LBT(ϕ) =

sections.title:
1
N
sections.content: N k=1 log σ(cid:0)rϕ(y(k) , q(k)) rϕ(y(k) , q(k))(cid:1), where rϕ is neural network parameterised by ϕ. This is binary cross-entropy loss where the positive class is the preferred response. Bradley-Terry Assumptions 1. Preferences are transitive: if y1 y2 and y2 y3, then y1 y3. 2. Preferences are determined by scalar reward (no multi-dimensional preferences). 3. The preference probability depends only on the difference in rewards. 4. Preferences are independent across pairs (no annotator effects). These assumptions are often violated in practice, motivating extensions like Plackett-Luce models for ranking and multi-dimensional reward models.

sections.title: Margin Loss Extension
sections.content: A common extension adds margin to ensure minimum gap between winning and losing rewards: Lmargin =

sections.title:
1
N
sections.content: N k=1 log σ(cid:0)rϕ(y(k) , q(k)) rϕ(y(k) , q(k)) m(cid:1). 182 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 9.2 Reward Model Architectures
sections.content: Classification Head on LLM The standard reward model architecture takes pretrained LLM and replaces the language modelling head (which maps hidden states to vocabulary logits) with scalar regression head (which maps the final hidden state to single reward value). The architecture is: 1. Backbone: pretrained LLM (e.g., Llama, Mistral) that encodes the prompt-response pair into sequence of hidden states. 2. Pooling: extract the hidden state at the last token position (for decoder-only models) or the [CLS] token (for encoder models). 3. Regression head: linear layer Rd1 that maps the pooled hidden state to scalar reward. Reward Model Training in TRL from trl import RewardConfig , RewardTrainer from transformers import o e r u e s i i # Load model with scalar head ( num_labels =1) model = o e r u e s i i . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " , num_labels =1 , ) config = RewardConfig ( output_dir = " reward_model " , _ i _ i _ c _ e =4 , d t _ u a n _ p =4 , learning_rate =1 -5 , num_train_epochs =1 , # Margin loss t _ a _ f i =0.01 , ) trainer = RewardTrainer ( model = model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , ) trainer . train () # must have chosen / rejected columns

sections.title: Reward Centering
sections.content: Raw reward model outputs can have arbitrary scale and offset. Centering the rewards (subtracting the mean) stabilises RL training: rcentered(y, q) = rϕ(y, q) Eyπθ [rϕ(y, q)]. In TRL, this is implemented via the center_rewards_coefficient parameter, which adds regularisation term to the reward model loss that penalises non-zero mean rewards. 183 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Length Bias Correction Reward models are known to exhibit length bias: they tend to assign higher rewards to longer responses, regardless of quality. This can be corrected by: 1. Length normalisation: divide the reward by the response length. 2. Length-controlled training: include length as feature and train the model to be lengthinvariant. 3. Calibration: post-hoc regression to remove the length effect. Margin Losses Adding margin to the Bradley-Terry loss ensures the reward model assigns meaningfully different scores to preferred and dispreferred responses: Lmargin = max(cid:0)0, (rw rl)(cid:1).

sections.title: PRM
sections.content: Final answer only (prompt, answer, correct?) Low

sections.title:
Easier to hack
Simple tasks
Low
sections.content: Each reasoning step (prompt, steps, step labels) High Dense Harder to hack Multi-step reasoning High (score each step) When to Use PRMs Process Reward Models are most valuable when: The task requires multi-step reasoning (math, code, logic). The final answer is binary (correct/incorrect) but intermediate steps vary in quality. You want to use the reward model for search (e.g., beam search with step scores). You have access to step-level annotations (or can generate them automatically). For simple tasks (sentiment, toxicity, factuality), ORMs are sufficient and much cheaper. PBRS in RLHF for LLMs Original reward: Binary correctness (1 if final answer is right, 0 otherwise) extremely sparse for multi-step reasoning. Potential function: Φ(s) = partial credit from verifier (e.g., fraction of intermediate reasoning steps that are logically valid). Shaped reward: Agent gets incremental signal for each valid reasoning step while preserving the guarantee that the optimal policy still maximizes final-answer correctness. Practical implementations: Process reward models (PRMs) that score each step in chain-of-thought Intermediate compilation checks in code generation 184 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Partial match scores for multi-part answers This is direct application of Potential-Based Reward Shaping (PBRS) [173] to the LLM setting the theoretical guarantee that shaped rewards preserve the optimal policy makes PRMs principled approach to dense reward in reasoning tasks. Automatic PRM Annotation Step-level annotations can be generated automatically using: 1. Monte Carlo rollouts: for each intermediate step, sample multiple completions and use the fraction that reach the correct answer as the step reward. 2. LLM-as-judge: use strong LLM to evaluate each step. 3. Formal verification: for math/code, use verifier to check each step.

sections.title: 9.5 Rule-Based Rewards for RLVR
sections.content: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) uses deterministic, rule-based reward functions instead of learned reward models. This substantially reduces reward hacking (though models can still exploit format tricks, edge cases, or test memorization) and is the approach used in DeepSeek-R1 [15]. Rule-Based Reward Functions in TRL import re def format_reward ( completions , ** kwargs ) : " " " Reward for using < think >... </ think > < answer >... </ answer > format . " " " rewards = [] pattern = " < think >.*? </ think > * < answer >.*? </ answer > " for completion in completions : text = completion [0][ " content " ] rewards . append (1.0 if re . fullmatch ( pattern , text , re . DOTALL ) else 0.0) return rewards def correctness_rew ar ( completions , ground_truth , ** kwargs ) : " " " Reward for correct final answer . " " " rewards = [] for completion , gt in zip ( completions , ground_truth ) : text = completion [0][ " content " ] match = re . search ( " < answer >(.*?) </ answer > " , text , re . DOTALL ) if match : answer = match . group (1) . strip () rewards . append (1.0 if answer == gt else 0.0) else : rewards . append (0.0) return rewards def ode_ ex ec uti _ rew ard ( completions , test_cases , ** kwargs ) : " " " Reward for code that passes test cases . " " " import subprocess , tempfile , os rewards = [] for completion , tests in zip ( completions , test_cases ) : code = completion [0][ " content " ] passed = 0 for test in tests : with tempfile . NamedTemp oraryFil ( mode = " " , suffix = " . py " , delete = False ) as : . write ( code + " " + test ) fname = . name 185 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems try : result = subprocess . run ( [ " python " , fname ] , capture_output = True , timeout =5 , text = True ) passed += int ( result . returncode == 0) except Exception : pass finally : os . unlink ( fname ) rewards . append ( passed / len ( tests ) ) return rewards Rule-Based Reward Pitfalls Format gaming: models learn to produce the correct format without correct content. Always combine format and correctness rewards. Test case leakage: if test cases are in the training data, the model memorises them. Timeout exploitation: models may generate code that times out (avoiding failure). Use strict timeouts and penalise timeouts explicitly. Reward sparsity: binary rewards (0/1) can be too sparse for complex tasks. Consider partial credit or intermediate rewards.

sections.title: 9.6 Multi-Objective Rewards – Combination Strategies
sections.content: When training with multiple reward signals, the combination strategy significantly affects the final policy. Multi-Reward Combination Strategies 1. Weighted sum: = wnrn. Simple but sensitive to scale. 2. Normalise then sum (GDPO): normalise each reward to zero mean and unit variance within the group, then sum with weights. Scale-invariant. 3. Lexicographic: optimise rewards in priority order; only consider lower-priority rewards when higher-priority ones are tied. 4. Constrained: maximise primary reward subject to constraints on secondary rewards. 5. Pareto: maintain Pareto front of policies and select based on preference. Multi-Reward Training in TRL from trl import GRPOConfig , GRPOTrainer config = GRPOConfig ( # GDPO : normalise each reward independently t _ e v _ r t = " normalize_then_sum " , reward_weights =[1.0 , 0.3 , 0.1] , num_generations =8 , # correctness , format , length ) trainer = GRPOTrainer ( model = model , reward_funcs =[ correctness_reward , format_reward , length_penalty_reward , ] , 186 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems args = config , train_dataset = dataset , )

sections.title: 9.7 Listwise Rank-Based Rewards
sections.content: While the Bradley-Terry model handles pairwise preferences (yw yl), many practical scenarios involve ranking multiple responses simultaneously. Listwise reward models learn from complete orderings, providing richer training signal and enabling better calibration. Motivation: Beyond Pairwise Why Listwise? Richer signal: ranking of responses contains (cid:0)K 2 (cid:1) implicit pairwise comparisons, but also captures relative margins (how much better rank 1 is vs. rank 3). Better calibration: Pairwise BT models only learn differences in reward; listwise models learn absolute reward scale. Natural fit for GRPO: GRPO generates responses per prompt and ranks them listwise rewards align directly with this workflow. Annotator efficiency: Ranking 5 responses is faster than labeling all 10 possible pairs independently. Plackett-Luce Model The Plackett-Luce (PL) model [199] is the standard extension of Bradley-Terry to full rankings. Given responses y1, . . . , yK with ranking π (where π(1) is the best): Plackett-Luce Likelihood (π q) = i= erϕ(yπ(i),q) j=i erϕ(yπ(j),q) PK Intuition: Sequentially select the best remaining item. At each step, the probability of selecting item π(i) is softmax over the remaining items. Loss function: LPL(ϕ) = 1 X K1 rϕ(yπ(i), q) log (q,π)D i=1 erϕ(yπ(j),q) j=i Plackett-Luce Reduces to Bradley-Terry For = 2, the PL model gives: (y1 y2) = Bradley-Terry model. PL is strict generalization. er(y1) er(y1)+er(y2) = σ(r(y1) r(y2)) exactly the ListMLE and Rank-Based Losses

sections.title: Listwise Loss Functions
sections.content: ListMLE [200]: Directly maximizes the PL likelihood of the ground-truth ranking. Simple and effective. ListNet [201]: Minimizes KL divergence between the models top-1 probability distribution 187 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems and the ground-truth: LListNet = i=1 Ptrue(yi is best) log Pmodel(yi is best) where Pmodel(yi is best) = erϕ(yi) erϕ(yj ) . LambdaRank [202]: Weights pairwise gradients by the change in ranking metric (e.g., NDCG). Useful when ranking quality matters more at the top. RankNet [203]: Pairwise cross-entropy summed over all pairs equivalent to BT on all (cid:0)K 2 (cid:1) pairs extracted from the ranking. Listwise Rewards for GRPO and Rejection Sampling Integration with GRPO GRPO naturally produces ranked groups: for each prompt, responses are scored and ranked. listwise reward model can be trained directly on these rankings: 1. Generate: Sample = 8 responses per prompt from the policy. 2. Rank: Use an existing reward model (or human annotators) to produce full ranking π. 3. Train listwise RM: Optimize the PL loss on (q, π) tuples. 4. Use in GRPO: The listwise RM assigns scalar rewards r(yi, q) to each response; GRPO computes advantages as ˆAi = (ri µ)/σ. Advantage over pairwise: The listwise RM sees all responses simultaneously, learning that rank-1 should have much higher reward than rank-N (not just slightly better than one other response).

sections.title: Listwise Training Challenges
sections.content: Annotation cost: Full rankings are expensive. Partial rankings (top-3 out of 8) reduce cost with minimal quality loss. Ties: Real rankings often have ties. Use the Plackett-Luce extension for ties: assign equal probability mass to tied items. Position bias: Annotators tend to prefer items shown first. Randomize presentation order and train debiasing. List length: Training on = 48 is typical. Longer lists (K > 16) add noise without much benefit. Consistency: Rankings from different annotators may disagree. Use inter-annotator agreement (κ > 0.6) as quality filter. Plackett-Luce Training Code import torch import torch . nn . functional as def plackett_luce_l os ( rewards , rankings ) : " " " 188 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Args : rewards : ( batch , ) - predicted scalar rewards for responses rankings : ( batch , ) - ground - truth ranking indices (0 = best ) Returns : scalar loss " " " batch_size , = rewards . shape # Sort rewards by ground - truth ranking order sorted_rewards = torch . gather ( rewards , 1 , rankings ) # ( batch , ) # PL log - likelihood : sum over positions loss = 0.0 for in range ( - 1) : # Log - softmax over remaining items ( position to ) remaining = sorted_rewards [: , :] log_probs = remaining [: , 0] - torch . logsumexp ( remaining , dim =1) loss -= log_probs . mean () # ( batch , - ) return loss / ( - 1) # Example : 8 responses per prompt , ranked by annotator rewards = reward_model ( responses ) # ( batch , 8) rankings = torch . argsort ( human_scores , descending = True ) loss = la ck et t_luce _l oss ( rewards , rankings ) loss . backward () # best first 189 Chapter

sections.title: SFT Best Practices and Techniques
sections.content: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is the foundation of the RLHF pipeline. The quality of the SFT model determines the ceiling of what RL can achieve: RL can refine and improve behaviour, but it cannot reliably introduce behaviour that is entirely absent from the SFT model. This section covers the key techniques for effective SFT.

sections.title: The Padding Problem
sections.content: Standard SFT batches pad all sequences to the length of the longest sequence in the batch. For datasets with high length variance (e.g., mix of short instructions and long documents), this wastes 5080% of compute on padding tokens. Sequence packing eliminates this waste. Sequence packing concatenates multiple short examples into single sequence of length max_seq_length, separated by EOS tokens. The attention mask ensures that tokens from different examples do not attend to each other: 1. Sort examples by length (optional, improves packing efficiency). 2. Greedily pack examples into bins of size max_seq_length. 3. Use block-diagonal attention mask to prevent cross-example attention. 4. Compute loss only on non-padding tokens.

sections.title: Packing Efficiency
sections.content: Typical packing efficiency: 8595% (vs 2050% with padding). Speedup: 24 for datasets with high length variance. Memory: similar to padding (same total tokens per batch). Caveat: requires careful attention masking to avoid cross-contamination.

sections.title: Sequence Packing in TRL
sections.content: from trl import SFTConfig , SFTTrainer config = SFTConfig ( max_seq_length =4096 , packing = True , output_dir = " sft_model " , _ i _ i _ c _ e =4 , d t _ u a n _ p =4 , learning_rate =2 -5 , num_train_epochs =3 , # enable sequence packing 190 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems ) trainer = SFTTrainer ( model = model , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , # da tase t_ te xt _field =" text " , ) trainer . train () # or use formatting_func

sections.title: 10.2 Chat Templates and Formatting
sections.content: Why Chat Templates Matter Language models are trained on raw text, but instruction-following models need to distinguish between system prompts, user messages, and assistant responses. Chat templates encode this structure into the token sequence. Using the wrong template (or no template) at inference time causes significant performance degradation. ChatML Format ChatML is the most widely used chat template: # ChatML format template = " " " < im_start > system { system_message } < im_end > < im_start > user { user_message } < im_end > < im_start > assistant { assistant_message } < im_end > " " "

sections.title: Llama Format
sections.content: Llama 3 uses different template with special tokens: # Llama 3 format template = " " " < begin_of_text > < start_header_id > system < end_header_id > { system_message } < eot_id > < start_header_id > user < end_header_id > { user_message } < eot_id > < start_header_id > assistant < end_header_id > { assistant_message } < eot_id > " " "

sections.title: Applying Chat Templates in TRL
sections.content: from transformers import AutoTokenizer from trl import SFTConfig , SFTTrainer tokenizer = AutoTokenizer . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " ) def formatting_func ( example ) : " " " Apply chat template to dataset example . " " " messages = [ { " role " : " system " , " content " : " You are helpful assistant . " } , { " role " : " user " , " content " : example [ " instruction " ]} , { " role " : " assistant " , " content " : example [ " response " ]} , ] return tokenizer . ppl _chat_template ( messages , tokenize = False , add _ gen at io _p rom = False , ) 191 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems config = SFTConfig ( max_seq_length =2048 , output_dir = " sft_model " , ) trainer = SFTTrainer ( model = model , tokenizer = tokenizer , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , formatting_func = formatting_func , )

sections.title: 10.3 Completion-Only Masking
sections.content: Why Mask the Prompt? In instruction fine-tuning, the model should learn to generate the assistants response, not to predict the users question or the system prompt. Computing loss on the prompt tokens wastes gradient signal and can cause the model to memorise prompts rather than learning to respond to them. Completion-only masking sets the loss to zero for all non-assistant tokens. Completion-Only Masking in TRL from trl import SFTConfig , SFTTrainer , a l r C l o l from transformers import AutoTokenizer tokenizer = AutoTokenizer . from_pretrained ( " meta - llama / Llama -3.1 -8 - Instruct " ) # Define the response template ( tokens after which loss is computed ) resp onse_templa te = " < start_header_id > assistant < end_header_id > " collator = a l r C l o l ( response_ templa te = response_template , tokenizer = tokenizer , ) config = SFTConfig ( max_seq_length =2048 , output_dir = " sft_model " , ) trainer = SFTTrainer ( model = model , tokenizer = tokenizer , args = config , train_dataset = dataset , data_collator = collator , formatting_func = formatting_func , # completion - only masking )

sections.title: Completion Masking Pitfalls
sections.content: The response template must exactly match the tokenised form. Off-by-one errors in tokenisation can cause the mask to be applied incorrectly. For very short responses, masking the prompt may leave too few tokens for meaningful gradient signal. Consider minimum response length threshold. Multi-turn conversations require masking all non-assistant turns, not just the first. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 10.4 Data Mixing Strategies for Multi-Task SFT
sections.content: The Multi-Task Challenge Training on multiple tasks simultaneously can improve generalisation but also causes task interference: gradients from different tasks conflict, degrading performance on individual tasks. Data mixing strategies control the relative contribution of each task to the training signal. Proportional Mixing Sample from each dataset proportionally to its size: pk = Nk j=1 Nj PK , where Nk is the number of examples in dataset k. This is the default in most frameworks and works well when datasets are of similar quality. Temperature Mixing Apply temperature to smooth the proportions: pk 1/T . = 1: proportional mixing. : uniform mixing. < 1: over-samples large datasets. > 1: over-samples small datasets. Quality-Weighted Mixing Weight datasets by estimated quality (e.g., perplexity under reference model, human quality ratings): where qk is the quality score for dataset k.

sections.title: Data Mixing in TRL
sections.content: pk Nk qk, from datasets import concatenate_datasets , interleave_datasets # Proportional mixing ( default ) mixed_dataset = co cat enate_datase ts ([ dataset_math , dataset_code , dataset_general , ]) . shuffle ( seed =42) # Temperature mixing ( =2: over - sample small datasets ) mixed_dataset = ter leave_datasets ( [ dataset_math , dataset_code , dataset_general ] , probabilities =[0.4 , 0.4 , 0.2] , seed =42 , # manually set after temperature scaling ) config = SFTConfig ( output_dir = " sft_model " ) trainer = SFTTrainer ( model = model , args = config , train_dataset = mixed_dataset , ) H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: Tax
sections.content: As LLMs transition through sequential training phases pre-training continued pre-training SFT RLHF/DPO performance degradation frequently manifests on standard benchmarks. Two fundamentally distinct phenomena drive these regressions, and confusing them leads to wrong mitigation strategies.

sections.title: 10.5.1 Catastrophic Forgetting (Structural Erasure)
sections.content: Catastrophic Forgetting Catastrophic forgetting is an unintentional optimization failure: when network optimized on distribution DA is subsequently trained on disjoint distribution DB, the weight updates required for DB physically overwrite the parameter structures encoding DA: θt+1 = θt ηθLB(θt) = LA(θt+1) LA(θt) (10.1) The knowledge is destroyed the weights encoding Task no longer exist. This is irreversible without retraining. Symptoms: Complete breakdown on tasks not in fine-tuning data (e.g., model forgets how to do math after SFT on chat data) Loss of language diversity model only generates in the narrow style of fine-tuning distribution Reduced factual accuracy on knowledge not reinforced during fine-tuning Degraded multilingual ability after English-only SFT Mechanistic cause Fisher Information perspective: The Fisher Information Matrix of Task identifies which parameters are important for DA: = ExDA θ log πθ(x) θ log πθ(x)T (10.2) Parameters with high Fisher eigenvalues are critical for Task A. Unconstrained gradient descent on Task ignores these eigenvalues entirely θ points along LB regardless of whether it destroys high-Fisher directions for LA.

sections.title: 10.5.2 Alignment Tax (Behavioral Constraint)
sections.content: The alignment tax is deliberate, expected trade-off : the models raw capability (unconstrained generation, maximal reasoning bandwidth) decreases because the policy is constrained to produce safe, well-formatted, preference-aligned outputs. Mechanism: During DPO/PPO, the policy πθ is penalized for deviating from the reference πref via KL divergence: rimplicit(x, y) = β log πθ(yx) πref(yx) (10.3) This leash constrains the models output distribution it cannot explore high-variance reasoning paths that deviate too far from the reference. The knowledge is not erased; its suppressed. The model still knows the answer but its distribution is flattened toward safe, generic responses. Symptoms: Over-refusal (I cant help with that for benign queries) Stylistic stiffness hedge words, excessive caveats, verbose safety disclaimers 194 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Lower scores on raw capability benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval) while improving on preference benchmarks (MT-Bench, AlpacaEval) Reduced ability to produce complex, high-entropy outputs (creative writing, novel algorithms)

sections.title: 10.5.3 Comparative Taxonomy
sections.content: Table 10.1: Catastrophic Forgetting vs. Alignment Tax complete comparison. Dimension Intentionality Parameter state Information Dominant phase Primary symptom Reversibility

sections.title: Detection
sections.content: Catastrophic Forgetting Alignment Tax (optimization Unintentional artifact) Prior knowledge physically overwritten Destroyed: weights no longer encode the capability Sequential SFT, domain continued pre-training Complete breakdown of baseline capabilities Irreversible without retraining from checkpoint Perplexity on pre-training eval set spikes Expected trade-off (incurred deliberately for safety/helpfulness) Latent distributions constrained/truncated Suppressed: knowledge exists but is harder to trigger Preference optimization (PPO, DPO, KTO, RLHF) Over-refusal, stylistic stiffness, lower raw benchmark scores Partially reversible: adjust β, system prompt, or fine-tune Perplexity stable but win-rate on capability benchmarks drops Smaller models pay larger alignment tax

sections.title: 10.5.4 Mitigation Strategies
sections.content: For Catastrophic Forgetting: 1. Data replay: Mix 510% of pre-training data into SFT dataset. Ensures gradient updates dont completely neglect pre-training distribution. 2. Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) [204]: Add regularization Ω(θ) = λ 2 Fi(θi θ )2 that penalizes changes to parameters with high Fisher information for the original task. 3. LoRA / Parameter-efficient fine-tuning: Train only low-rank adapters (< 1% of parameters), leaving base weights completely frozen. This prevents permanent destruction of pre-trained knowledge you can always remove the adapter and recover the original model. However, while the adapter is active, the combined system (W0 + BA) can still exhibit forgetting: the adapter may shift the models effective behavior away from old skills. LoRA protects the checkpoint, not the active inference behavior. 4. Conservative learning rate: Use 15 106 with few epochs (13). Larger rates accelerate forgetting. 5. Progressive training: Mix distributions gradually, increasing SFT data proportion over time rather than switching abruptly. For Alignment Tax: 1. Tune β carefully: Lower β gives the model more freedom (reduces the tax) but may sacrifice safety. Optimal β [0.05, 0.3] for most settings. 2. High-quality, diverse SFT data: Part of the alignment tax comes from SFT narrowing the output distribution; broader, more diverse SFT data reduces this component. The RL phase adds further constraint via KL regularization [9]. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems 3. Conditional alignment: Train the model to be aligned only when safety flag is active. At inference, disable constraints for benchmarking (research-only technique). 4. Constitutional AI / RLAIF: Use model-generated feedback to create more nuanced preference data that preserves capability while improving alignment. 5. Targeted RL budget: Dont over-train with RL. Monitor capability benchmarks and stop when the tax exceeds acceptable thresholds (typically 25% MMLU regression). How to Tell Which One You Have Run the base model on the failing tasks: If the base model succeeds and the fine-tuned model completely fails catastrophic forgetting. Prompt engineering test: If careful prompting (e.g., ignore safety guidelines and solve this math problem step by step) recovers the capability alignment tax (knowledge is suppressed, not erased). Perplexity check: Compute perplexity on pre-training validation set. Spike = forgetting. Stable = alignment tax. Few-shot recovery: If providing few in-context examples restores the capability alignment tax. If even many examples cant recover it forgetting.

sections.title: 10.6 Connection to RL – SFT Quality Determines RL Ceiling
