sections.content: The SFT-RL Relationship The SFT model is the starting point for RL training. RL can: Amplify behaviours that are present but weak in the SFT model. Suppress behaviours that are present but undesirable. Refine the style and format of responses. RL cannot: Introduce capabilities that are entirely absent from the SFT model. Recover from severe catastrophic forgetting in the SFT stage. Compensate for reward model that is systematically biased. The Exploration-Exploitation Tradeoff in SFT For RL to work, the SFT model must occasionally produce correct responses (so the reward signal is non-zero). If the SFT model never produces correct response to given prompt, RL cannot learn to produce correct responses there is no positive signal to amplify. This is why SFT quality is the ceiling for RL performance. Concretely: if the SFT model solves 10% of math problems correctly, RL can potentially push this to 80%. If the SFT model solves 0% of math problems, RL will make no progress (all rewards are zero, all advantages are zero, no gradient).

sections.title: Practical Implications
sections.content: 1. SFT data quality: use high-quality, diverse data. small amount of high-quality data is better than large amount of low-quality data. 2. SFT data coverage: ensure the SFT data covers the tasks you want to improve with RL. If task is not in the SFT data, RL will struggle. 196 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems 3. SFT training duration: do not over-train the SFT model. Over-training reduces diversity and makes RL exploration harder. 4. Warm-up: consider short SFT warm-up on task-specific data before RL, even if the base model is already instruction-tuned. Checking SFT Quality Before RL import numpy as np from tqdm import tqdm def es ti mate_pass_a t_ ( model , tokenizer , dataset , =8 , n_samples =100) : " " " Estimate pass@k for the SFT model . If pass@1 < 5% , RL will likely fail . If pass@k < 20% , RL will struggle . " " " pass_at_1_scores = [] pass_at_k_scores = [] for example in tqdm ( dataset . select ( range ( n_samples ) ) ) : prompt = example [ " prompt " ] ground_truth = example [ " answer " ] # Sample completions inputs = tokenizer ( prompt , return_tensors = " pt " ) . to ( model . device ) outputs = model . generate ( ** inputs , max_new_tokens =512 , do_sample = True , temperature =0.8 , um _r et ur n_ se uen es =k , ) correct = 0 for output in outputs : response = tokenizer . decode ( output , skip_s pe cial_tokens = True ) if ground_truth in response : correct += 1 # pass@1 : fraction of samples that are correct ( estimated success rate ) pass_at_1_scores . append ( correct / ) # pass@k : at least one of samples is correct pass_at_k_scores . append ( correct >= 1) print ( " Pass@1 ( estimated ) : { np . mean ( pass_at_1_scores ) :.2%} " ) print ( " Pass@ { }: { np . mean ( pass_at_k_scores ) :.2%} " ) print ( " RL viability : { Good if np . mean ( pass_at_1_scores ) > 0.05 else Poor } " ) es timate_p ass_at _k ( sft_model , tokenizer , eval_dataset )

sections.title: SFT Best Practices Summary
sections.content: 1. Use sequence packing to maximise GPU utilisation. 2. Apply completion-only masking to focus gradient on assistant responses. 3. Use the correct chat template for your model family. 4. Mix data proportionally with temperature scaling (T 2) for multi-task SFT. 5. Use LoRA to prevent catastrophic forgetting. 6. Evaluate pass@k before starting RL to ensure the SFT model is viable starting point. 197 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems 7. Do not over-train: 13 epochs is usually sufficient for instruction fine-tuning. 8. Monitor diversity metrics (entropy, n-gram diversity) to detect mode collapse. 198 Chapter System Architecture & Infrastructure at Scale Training LLMs with reinforcement learning from human feedback is as much systems engineering challenge as it is an algorithmic one. Unlike standard supervised fine-tuningwhich involves single model, single forward-backward pass, and well-understood scalingRLHF requires multiple models (policy, reference, reward model, value head) to be loaded simultaneously, coordinated through complex rollout-scoring-training loop, and distributed across dozens to hundreds of GPUs. This chapter covers the systems-level details that make large-scale RLHF training possible: memory budgeting, parallelism strategies (Data, Tensor, Pipeline, Sequence, and their combinations), the generation bottleneck, decoupled architectures, weight synchronization, fault tolerance, and production monitoring.

sections.title: 11.1 The 4-Model Memory Challenge
sections.content: Figure 11.1: 70B PPO memory budget: the four models required for RLHF and their memory footprints. Total: 14701560GB. Minimum 1920 A100-80GB (naive). With ZeRO-3: fits in 8 nodes. Memory Budget Reality Check 70B BF16 Policy weights (BF16)

sections.content: 80 GB/GPU = 1920 A100s minimum (without any parallelism overhead). 199 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 11.2 Parallelism Strategies in Detail
sections.content: Training large language models requires distributing computation across many GPUs. There are fundamentally different axes along which to parallelize, each with distinct trade-offs. This section provides detailed coverage of each strategy with mathematical formulations, diagrams, and practical guidance. Figure 11.2: Overview of the four parallelism strategies. Production systems typically combine 23 of these simultaneously.

sections.title: 11.2.1 Data Parallelism (DP) and Distributed Data Parallelism (DDP)
sections.content: Data Parallelism is the simplest and most common form of distributed training [205]. Each GPU holds complete copy of the model, processes different mini-batch, and synchronizes gradients. Vanilla DP (PyTorch DataParallel). single-process approach where one master GPU scatters input, gathers outputs, and broadcasts gradients. Limited by GIL and PCIe bandwidth to the master GPU. Distributed Data Parallelism (DDP, DistributedDataParallel). Multi-process: each GPU runs its own process. Gradients are synchronized via ring-AllReduce [206] in the background while backward computation continues. Figure 11.3: DDP: each GPU holds full model replica and processes different batch. Gradients are averaged via ring AllReduce, overlapped with backward computation. Key properties of DDP: Memory: Each GPU stores full model + optimizer + gradients. For 70B BF16: 560 GB/GPU impossible without memory optimizations. Communication: One AllReduce of gradient tensor per step. Size = model parameters 2 bytes (BF16). Ring AllReduce cost: 2 1 bytes transferred per GPU. Scaling: Near-linear up to 64 GPUs. Beyond that, communication starts to dominate. Gradient bucketing: DDP groups parameters into buckets (default 25 MB) and starts AllReduce as soon as buckets gradients are readyoverlapping communication with backward computation. 200 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems import torch . distributed as dist from torch . nn . parallel import Di tr bu te Da aP ar ll as DDP dist . ini t_ pr oc ess_gr ou ( backend = " nccl " ) model = model . to ( local_rank ) model = DDP ( model , device_ids =[ local_rank ] , # NCCL for GPU communication ra nt _ _ k t_ ie = True , static_graph = True ) # Memory optimization # Enable comm optimizations DP vs DDP Always Use DDP PyTorchs legacy DataParallel (DP) should never be used for LLM training: Single-process, limited by Python GIL All gradients funnel through GPU 0 (bottleneck) 23 slower than DDP even on single node Cannot scale beyond one machine DDP is the minimum parallelism strategy. For LLMs >7B, FSDP/ZeRO is preferred.

sections.title: 11.2.2 Tensor Parallelism (TP)
sections.content: Tensor Parallelism (Megatron-LM style [207]) splits individual weight matrices across GPUs. Each GPU computes partial result, and an AllReduce combines them. Column-Parallel Linear Layer. The weight matrix Rdh is split column-wise across GPUs: = [W0 W1 WT 1], Wi Rdh/T (11.1) Each GPU computes Yi = XWi independently (no communication). The output is split along the hidden dimension. Row-Parallel Linear Layer. The weight matrix is split row-wise: = [W0; W1; . . . ; WT 1] where Wi Rd/T h. Input must also be split. Each GPU computes partial sum, then an AllReduce produces the final output. Figure 11.4: Column-parallel linear layer (TP=2). The weight is split column-wise; each GPU computes XWi independently. The MLP pairs this with row-parallel layer to avoid redundant AllReduce. 201 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Transformer Block with TP. In Transformer layer, Megatron-LM applies TP as follows: 1. MLP: Column-parallel for the first linear (h 4h), row-parallel for the second (4h h). One AllReduce after the row-parallel layer. 2. Attention: Q, K, projections are column-parallel (split heads across GPUs). Output projection is row-parallel. One AllReduce after output projection. 3. Total: 2 AllReduce per transformer layer (one for attention, one for MLP). Figure 11.5: Tensor Parallel communication pattern in one Transformer block. Two AllReduce operations (marked in red) are required per layerone after attention, one after MLP. Why TP is Restricted to Intra-Node Each transformer layer requires 2 AllReduce operations (marked as and above). For 70B model with 80 layers, thats 160 AllReduce operations per forward pass (320 including backward). At NVLink speeds (600 GB/s), each AllReduce takes <0.5 ms. But over InfiniBand (50 GB/s), the same operation takes 4 ms, making the total overhead 160 4 = 640 mslonger than the computation itself. 202 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Rule: TP degree GPUs per node (typically TP 8). Use DP/FSDP for inter-node scaling. TP Degree Selection TP=1: No tensor parallelism. Model fits on one GPU (typically 13B with BF16). TP=2: Minimal split. Good for 1334B inference on 2 GPUs. Low overhead (<5%). TP=4: Standard for 3470B inference. Overhead 812%. TP=8: Full node. Required for 70B+ training. Overhead 1218%. TP>8: Cross-node TP. Rarely usedonly for 200B+ models where PP alone is insufficient. Overhead 3050%. Important: Number of attention heads must be divisible by TP degree. For LLaMA-70B (64 heads), valid TP = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64.

sections.title: 11.2.3 Sequence Parallelism (SP)
sections.content: Sequence Parallelism [208] addresses memory bottleneck that Tensor Parallelism alone cannot solve: the activation memory in LayerNorm and Dropout layers. The Problem. With TP, weight memory is split across GPUs. But LayerNorm and Dropout operate on the full hidden dimension and are replicated on every GPU. Their activations (needed for backward pass) consume memory proportional to dthe same on every GPU, unreduced by TP. The Solution. Split the sequence dimension for operations that dont require cross-GPU communication (LayerNorm, Dropout, residual connections). Each GPU processes s/T slice of the sequence for these operations, then gathers the full sequence only where needed (attention, linear layers). Figure 11.6: Sequence Parallelism reduces activation memory for LayerNorm/Dropout by splitting along the sequence dimension. Communication (AllGather/ReduceScatter) replaces the AllReduce used in standard TPsame total bytes transferred, but memory is saved. SP Communication is Free Standard TP uses AllReduce after each sub-layer, which is equivalent to ReduceScatter + AllGather. SP simply reorders these primitives: TP without SP: AllReduce (= ReduceScatter + AllGather) same data on all GPUs LayerNorm on full tensor (wasteful). TP with SP: ReduceScatter each GPU has 1/T of sequence LayerNorm on partial tensor AllGather before next TP layer. 203 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems The total communication volume is identical! SP is purely memory optimization with zero additional communication cost. It should always be enabled when using TP. Memory savings from SP (70B model, TP=8, batch=4, seq=2048): Activation savings = (T 1)bsdnlayers2 bytes = 7420488192802 59 GB/GPU (11.2)

sections.title: 11.2.4 Pipeline Parallelism (PP)
sections.content: Pipeline Parallelism splits the model vertically by layers, assigning consecutive groups of layers to different devices (stages). Activations flow forward through stages; gradients flow backward. The Bubble Problem. Naive pipeline execution creates bubblesidle time while stage waits for input from the previous stage or gradients from the next: Figure 11.7: Pipeline bubble comparison. Left: naive pipeline with one micro-batch has 75% idle time. Right: GPipe with = 4 micro-batches reduces bubbles significantly. With , bubble fraction approaches zero. Bubble Fraction Formula. For pipeline stages and micro-batches per step: Bubble fraction = 1 + 1 (when ) (11.3) To keep bubble overhead <10%, you need 10 (P 1). For PP=4: at least 30 micro-batches. Table 11.1: Pipeline scheduling strategies

sections.title: GPipe
sections.content: 1F1B Interleaved 1F1B

sections.title: Characteristics
sections.content: M activations activations activations allthen all-forward steady-state memory Simple; backward [209] Interleaved; bounded [210] Virtual stages (V ); further reduces bubble [211] Splits backward into and phases [212] Zero-Bubble (ZB-H1) 0 activations Pipeline Schedules. 1F1B: The Production Standard The 1F1B (one-forward-one-backward) schedule [210] is used in most production systems (Megatron-LM [211], DeepSpeed [213]): Warmup: Forward passes fill the pipeline (P-1 micro-batches). Steady state: Alternate one forward and one backward per time slot. This bounds peak activation memory to micro-batches (vs for GPipe). Cooldown: Remaining backward passes drain the pipeline. Memory advantage: GPipe must store activations for all micro-batches simultaneously. 1F1B only stores sets of activations at steady statecritical when = 32 but = 4. 204 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Communication in PP. Unlike TP (AllReduce), PP only requires point-to-point communication of activations between adjacent stages: Data per transfer = bmicro 2 bytes (BF16) (11.4) For micro-batch=4, seq=2048, d=8192: 4 2048 8192 2 = 128 MB per transfer. At InfiniBand 50 GB/s: 2.6 ms per transfersmall relative to compute per stage. Load Balancing. Not all layers have equal compute: Embedding layer: Very cheap (lookup table). Transformer blocks: Uniform compute. Final LM head: Moderate (large matrix multiply for vocabulary projection). Assign more transformer layers to middle stages and fewer to the first/last stages to balance compute.

sections.title: 11.2.5 Fully Sharded Data Parallelism (FSDP / ZeRO-3)
sections.content: FSDP [214] (PyTorch) and ZeRO-3 [213] (DeepSpeed) address the memory duplication inherent in DDP: instead of every GPU holding full copy of parameters, gradients, and optimizer states, each GPU owns only 1/N slice and reconstructs the full tensor on-the-fly when needed. Figure 11.8: FSDP shards all model state across GPUs. Each GPU owns 1/N of parameters, optimizer states, and gradients. Full parameters are reconstructed on-demand via AllGather before each layers computation. FSDP execution flow per layer: 1. Forward: AllGather parameters compute discard non-owned shards. 2. Backward: AllGather parameters (again) compute gradients ReduceScatter gradients (each GPU gets its gradient shard) discard non-owned parameter shards. 3. Optimizer step: Each GPU updates only its owned shard using its gradient shard and optimizer states. from functools import partial from torch . distributed . fsdp import ll ar Da ar el as FSDP from torch . distributed . fsdp import ShardingStrategy , MixedPrecision , BackwardPrefetch from torch . distributed . fsdp . wrap import n r _ o _ p _ i from transformers . models . llama . modeling_llama import LlamaDecoderLayer H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 11.2: Memory comparison: DDP vs FSDP/ZeRO stages (70B model, 8 GPUs). Baseline: BF16 params (140 GB) + BF16 grads (140 GB) + FP32 master+m+v (840 GB) = 1120 GB per GPU. Strategy Sharded Memory/GPU Communication DDP (no sharding) Nothing ZeRO-1 ZeRO-2 Optimizer states Optimizer + gradients Everything ZeRO-3 / FSDP 1120 GB 385 GB 368 GB 140 GB AllReduce (gradients only) AllReduce (gradients) AllReduce (gradients) AllGather + ReduceScatter (per layer) # Wrap model with FSDP auto_wrap = partial ( transformer_auto_wrap_policy , ra ns rme _ lay r_ cl ={ LlamaDecoderLayer }) mp_policy = MixedPrecision ( param_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , reduce_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , buffer_dtype = torch . bfloat16 , ) # ZeRO -3 model = FSDP ( model , shar ding_strate gy = ShardingStrategy . FULL_SHARD , mixed_precision = mp_policy , auto_wrap_policy = auto_wrap , use_orig_params = True , limi t_all_gathe rs = True , time ) forward_prefetch = True , layer back ward_prefet ch = BackwardPrefetch . BACKWARD_PRE , # Wrap each transformer layer # Required for torch . compile compatibility # Bound peak memory (1 AllGather in flight at # Prefetch next layer params during current # Prefetch during backward ) FSDP Communication Volume FSDP communicates 3 more data than DDP per step: DDP: 1 AllReduce of gradients = 2M bytes total across ring (where = model size in bytes). FSDP: 2 AllGather (forward + backward) + 1 ReduceScatter = 3M bytes. This is the memorycommunication trade-off. FSDP is worthwhile when: (a) model doesnt fit in GPU memory with DDP, or (b) communication is well-overlapped with compute (modern frameworks achieve 7090% overlap). 11.2. 3D Parallelism: Combining Strategies Production systems at scale (70B+) combine TP, PP, and DP/FSDP simultaneously: Production Recipe: 70B on 64 A100-80GB (8 nodes) Intra-node (NVLink 600GB/s): TP=8 for generation, FSDP within node for training. Inter-node (InfiniBand 400Gb/s): FSDP across nodes (8-way data parallel). Result: Each GPU holds 70GB. Policy weights gathered per-layer during forward/backward. Pipeline Parallel: Only if model exceeds 100B+ and wont fit with TP+ZeRO. Adds complexity (bubble overhead 1020%) and scheduling headaches. 206 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Figure 11.9: 3D parallelism layout for 16 GPUs: TP=4 (within each box, using NVLink), PP=2 (orange arrows, stages), DP=2 (red arrows, gradient sync). Each dimension exploits different level of the communication hierarchy. Decision flowchart: 1. Does the model fit on 1 GPU? Use DDP. 2. Does it fit on 1 node with FSDP? Use FSDP (ZeRO-3). 3. Does it fit on 1 node with TP+FSDP? Use TP (intra-node) + FSDP (inter-node). 4. Still doesnt fit? Add PP across nodes. This is the last resort. Table 11.3: Parallelism strategy comparison summary Strategy Splits Communication Scaling Limit Overhead When to Use DP/DDP FSDP TP SP PP AllReduce (grads) Batch Params+Opt+Grad AllGather+RS Weight matrices Activations (seq) Layers (stages) AllReduce (2/layer) Reuses TP comms Point-to-point 64 GPUs 100s of GPUs 8 GPUs (1 node) Same as TP 16 stages Model fits on 1 GPU Default for >13B Large model inference+train 510% 1020% 1218% 0% extra Always with TP 1530% 100B+ models only

sections.title: 11.3 The Generation Bottleneck: Quantitative Analysis
sections.content: Roofline Analysis: Why Generation is Memory-Bound A100 specs: 312 TFLOPS (BF16 tensor cores), 2 TB/s HBM bandwidth. Roofline crossover: 312T/2T = 156 FLOP/byte. Operations below 156 FLOP/byte are memory-bound. Autoregressive generation: For each token, read all weights (140GB for 70B) and do 2 70B = 140G FLOPs per token (at batch=1). Arithmetic intensity: 140G FLOP/140GB = 1 FLOP/byte. Thats 156 below the roofline! Utilization: 1/156 = 0.6% of peak FLOPS utilized. The GPU is 99.4% idle, waiting for memory reads. Token rate: 2TB/s/140GB = 14.3 tokens/second (single stream, batch=1). For 512 tokens: 512/14.3 = 35.8 seconds per response (batch=1, TP=1). Batching helps: Batch=64 with TP=4 reads weights once, generates 64 tokens in parallel. Arithmetic intensity: 64 1 = 64 FLOP/byte. Better, but still below roofline! Table 11.4: Generation throughput for 70B model (512 tokens, various configurations) Config Batch Time/batch Tok/s/GPU Notes TP=1, batch=1 TP=4, batch=1 TP=4, batch=32 TP=4, batch=128, vLLM 128 128 TP=4, batch=128, INT 1 1 32 36s 9s 15s 45s 25s 14 57 1092 1456 2621 Baseline, worst case Linear TP scaling for gen Near-optimal batching Continuous batching 2 bandwidth savings 207 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Optimization stack (cumulative speedup): 1. vLLM + PagedAttention [157] (24): Eliminates KV cache fragmentation, enables larger batches 2. Continuous batching [215] (1.52): Dont wait for longest sequence; start new ones as others finish 3. Speculative decoding [143] (23): Small draft model proposes 5 tokens, large model verifies in one forward pass. Accept 34 on average. 4. INT8/FP8 weights for gen (2): Halve bandwidth needs. Quality loss is minimal since were sampling (not computing exact logits for training) 5. CUDA graphs (1.11.3): Eliminate kernel launch overhead for fixed-shape operations 6. Prefix caching (1.5 for shared-prefix prompts): Dont recompute system prompt KV cache # Production vLLM generation setup from vllm import LLM , SamplingParams engine = LLM ( model = " ./ polic y_checkpoint " , tens or_p ar al le _si =4 , gp u_ me mo y_ ut il iz o =0.92 , ma x_ nu m_ at ch ed _t n =16384 , max_num_seqs =256 , dtype = " bfloat16 " , ena ble_ pre x_c in = True , spec ulative_mod el = " ./ draft_1B " , nu m_ sp ec la ti ve _t n =5 , block_size =16 , swap_space =4 , ) # Generate responses for RLHF batch sampling_params = SamplingParams ( # TP =4 per instance # Leave headroom for KV cache # Max tokens in flight # Max concurrent sequences # Cache system prompt KV # Speculative decoding # PagedAttention block size # GB swap space for preemption temperature =0.7 , top_p =0.9 , max_tokens =512 , logprobs =1 , # Need log - probs for PPO ratio calculation ) outputs = engine . generate ( prompts , sampling_params ) # Extract : responses , log_probs for each token ( needed for PPO / GRPO )

sections.title: 11.4 Decoupled Architecture: Production Design
sections.content: Production RLHF systems such as DeepSpeed-Chat [216] and OpenRLHF [217] use decoupled architecture that separates generation, scoring, and training into independently scalable clusters. Why Decouple? Generation is memory-bandwidth bound (need fast HBM, waste compute). Training is compute-bound (need tensor cores, waste bandwidth during backprop). Same hardware cant optimize both: If you put everything together, you either waste compute during generation or waste bandwidth during training. Decoupling lets each cluster use optimal hardware/config. Practical benefits: Scale generation and training independently 208 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Figure 11.10: Decoupled RLHF architecture. Each cluster optimized for its workload. Scored rollouts accumulate in the experience buffer before being consumed by training. Generation cluster is stateless trivial fault tolerance Can overlap gen(step + 1) with training(step ) 3040% speedup Different quantization: INT8 for generation (bandwidth), BF16 for training (precision)

sections.title: 11.5 Weight Synchronization Strategies
sections.content: Strategy Staleness Bandwidth Quality Impact Synchronous (every step) Periodic (every 50 steps) Delta compression (INT8) Async streaming 0 steps 25 avg 25 avg 510 steps 140 GB/step Perfect but too slow 2.8 GB/step amortized <2% quality loss <3% quality loss 0.4 GB/step 14 GB/step (background) <1% quality loss Why Staleness is OK for PPO/GRPO PPOs clipped objective was designed for off-policy data! The clip [1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ] bounds the impact of stale data. With 1050 steps of staleness: Policy changes 0.11% per step (with proper LR) Over 50 steps: 5% policy drift PPO clip handles up to 20% drift by design Empirically: quality loss <2% for 50-step staleness Bandwidth math: 70B BF16 = 140GB. InfiniBand 400Gb/s = 50GB/s full sync in 2.8s. With delta compression: <0.5s. Async = free (runs in background).

sections.title: What Gets Sharded
sections.content: Memory/GPU (70B, 8 GPUs) None (Data Parallel) Nothing (full replica) Optimizer states only ZeRO-1 Optimizer states + Gradients ZeRO-2 Optimizer + Gradients + PaZeRO-3 (FSDP) rameters 560GB per GPU (impossible) 175GB 105GB 70GB (fits in A100-80GB!) Additional techniques: Gradient checkpointing [218]: Dont store all activations; recompute during backward pass. Saves 60% activation memory, costs 33% extra compute. Selective: only checkpoint attention layers (memory-heavy), keep FFN activations (compute-heavy to recompute). H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Mixed precision [219]: Forward in BF16 (2 bytes/param), optimizer states in FP32 (4 bytes each for m,v). Master weights in FP32 for accumulation. CPU offloading (ZeRO-Infinity [220]): Move optimizer states to CPU RAM. 50% memory savings but 23 slower (PCIe 64GB/s bottleneck). Activation offloading: Move activations to CPU during forward, bring back for backward. Only when memory is truly critical. Flash Attention [7, 82]: O(n) memory instead of O(n2) for attention. 24 faster + massive memory savings for long sequences.

sections.title: 11.6.1 Flash Attention’s Impact on RLHF
sections.content: Why Flash Attention Matters for RLHF RLHF involves generating long sequences (rollouts) and then training on them. Without Flash Attention: 4K-token sequence with 32 heads requires 4 GB just for attention matrices This severely limits batch size during PPO/GRPO training Gradient checkpointing of attention activations is expensive With Flash Attention: Attention memory is O(n) dominated by Q, K, V, tensors Longer rollouts (8K32K tokens) become feasible with the same GPU memory Backward pass recomputes attention tiles from Q, K, (no stored n2 matrix) This is the key enabler for long-context RLHF (e.g., reasoning models)

sections.title: Flash Attention and Gradient Checkpointing
sections.content: Flash Attentions backward pass recomputes the attention tiles on-the-fly from Q, K, (which are stored). This means Flash Attention already implements form of activation recomputation for the O(n2) attention matrix. You do not need to additionally checkpoint the attention layer doing so would recompute Q, K, unnecessarily. # DeepSpeed ZeRO -3 configuration for 70 RLHF training ds_config = { " bf16 " : { " enabled " : True } , " zero_ optimi zation " : { " stage " : 3 , " overlap_comm " : True , compute # Overlap communication with " con igu us_ die ts " : True , " reduce_scatter " : True , " redu ce _bucket_ si ze " : 5 e7 , " pre etc _bu t_s ze " : 5 e7 , " a _ s e _ e l " : 1 e5 , " offload_opt imizer " : { " device " : " cpu " , " pin_memory " : True } , " sub_group_size " : 1 e9 , # Better memory layout # More efficient than allreduce # 50 params per bucket # Prefetch next bucket # Keep small params on all GPUs # Reduce fragmentation # CPU offload } , " d t _ u a n _ p " : 4 , " gradi ent_cl ipping " : 1.0 , " i _ r _ c _ e _ _ " : 2 , " wa ll_clo k_b eak own " : True , } 210 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 11.7 Fault Tolerance at Scale
sections.content: Hardware Failure Reality Individual GPU MTBF: 10,000 hours. 512-GPU cluster MTBF: 10000/512 20 hours. But with software/network: 48 hours realistically. Multi-day training run: Will see 515 failures. Without fault tolerance, one failure kills everything. Production fault tolerance stack: 1. Detection: NCCL timeout (60s), GPU heartbeat (10s), NVML health monitoring, ECC error counting. 2. Checkpointing: Async every 50100 steps. Non-blocking (background thread). Save: model weights, optimizer states (Adam m/v), scheduler state, RNG states, KL coefficient, replay buffer. Keep last 3 checkpoints. Time: 30s for 70B (parallel write to NVMe). 3. Recovery: (a) Generation cluster = stateless, just restart and load latest weights. (b) Training cluster: load checkpoint, rebuild NCCL process group excluding failed node, redistribute FSDP shards, resume from last checkpoint. 4. Elastic training: Torch Elastic / Kubernetes auto-scaling. Replace failed node within minutes. Training continues with 1 GPUs temporarily. 5. Prevention: GPU health pre-screening (run GEMM stress test before starting). Hot spares on standby. Redundant network paths (dual-rail InfiniBand).

sections.title: 11.9 Monitoring and Observability
sections.content: Key Metrics to Track During RLHF Training Quality metrics (log every 10 steps): Mean reward (should increase then plateau) KL divergence from reference (should stay 310) Response length distribution (watch for length hacking) Entropy (should decrease slowly, not collapse) System metrics (log every step): GPU utilization (target: >80% during training, >60% during gen) Memory watermark per GPU (catch OOM before it happens) Generation throughput (tokens/sec, should be stable) Gradient norm (spikes = instability incoming) NCCL communication time (detect network degradation)

sections.title: 11.10 Network Topology and Communication Patterns
sections.content: Efficient distributed training requires understanding the hierarchical communication fabric that connects GPUs. Modern clusters use two-tier architecture: ultra-fast intra-node links and slower but scalable inter-node networks.

sections.title: 11.10.1 Intra-Node: NVLink and NVSwitch
sections.content: Table 11.5: NVLink generations and their impact on LLM training

sections.title: Generation BW per link

sections.content: A100 (DGX A100) H100 (DGX H100) B200 (DGX B200) Within single node (typically 8 GPUs), NVSwitch provides full-bisection bandwidth between all GPU pairs. This means any GPU can communicate with any other at full NVLink speed simultaneouslycritical for Tensor Parallelism where every layer requires an AllReduce across all 8 GPUs.

sections.title: NVSwitch vs PCIe Topology
sections.content: With NVSwitch (DGX/HGX): All 8 GPUs connected all-to-all at 6001800 GB/s. AllReduce for TP takes 0.2ms per layer. Without NVSwitch (PCIe-only servers): GPUs communicate through CPU PCIe root complex at 3264 GB/s. TP across 8 GPUs becomes 1030 slower. Never use TP>2 on PCIe-only systems.

sections.title: 11.10.2 Inter-Node: InfiniBand and RoCE
sections.content: For FSDP/ZeRO-3 AllGather and ReduceScatter operations across nodes, the inter-node network dominates. 212 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 11.6: Inter-node networking options for LLM training clusters Technology Bandwidth Latency Notes InfiniBand NDR InfiniBand NDR (dual-rail) RoCE v2 Ethernet (TCP) 400 Gb/s (50 GB/s) 800 Gb/s (100 GB/s) 100400 Gb/s 100400 Gb/s 12 µs 12 µs 25 µs 1050 µs Not suitable for >16 GPU training Gold standard, RDMA, lossless Used in H100 clusters Cheaper, needs PFC/ECN tuning

sections.title: 11.10.3 Communication Primitives and Their Costs
sections.content: Understanding when each collective is used helps diagnose bottlenecks: Table 11.7: NCCL collective operations in distributed LLM training Collective Data Moved Used By When AllReduce 2 1 TP, DP AllGather 1 ReduceScatter 1 Broadcast Send/Recv FSDP forward FSDP backward PP PP Sum gradients or activations across GPUs Reconstruct full parameter tensor before matmul Distribute gradient backprop Send activations to next pipeline stage Point-to-point between adjacent stages shards after where is the message size (bytes) and is the number of participants. 213 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Communication-Computation Overlap Modern frameworks (FSDP, DeepSpeed) aggressively overlap communication with computation: Forward pass: While layer computes, AllGather prefetches parameters for layer + 1. After layer finishes, its parameters are immediately discarded (free-after-forward). Backward pass: While layer computes gradients, ReduceScatter sends layer + 1s gradients. This overlap hides 7090% of communication latency when properly tuned. Tuning knobs: prefetch_factor (how many layers ahead to prefetch), reduce_bucket_size (granularity of gradient reduction), backward_prefetch (pre vs post backward prefetch strategy).

sections.title: 11.10.4 Network Topology Design
sections.content: Production clusters use fat-tree or rail-optimized topologies: Fat-tree: Full bisection bandwidth at every level. Any node can communicate with any other at full speed. Expensive (many switches) but maximally flexible. Rail-optimized: GPU on every node connects to the same leaf switch (rail i). AllReduce within rail is cheap; cross-rail traffic is expensive. Used by Metas RSC and Googles TPU pods. 3D torus / Dragonfly: Used in HPC clusters (Frontier, Aurora). Topology-aware job placement is critical. Job Placement Matters On 512-GPU cluster, random node assignment can cause 23 slowdown due to network congestion. Always request contiguous node blocks. Production schedulers (Slurm, Kubernetes) should enforce locality: all nodes in training job should be on the same leaf switch or within one hop of each other.

sections.title: 11.11.1 Measuring Training Efficiency: MFU
sections.content: Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) [221] is the standard metric for training efficiency: MFU = Observed throughput (tokens/sec) FLOPs per token Peak hardware FLOPS For transformer with parameters, sequence length, and batch size: FLOPs per token 6P + 12 nlayers dmodel (11.5) (11.6) The factor of 6 comes from: 2 (multiply-add) 3 (forward + backward, where backward 2 forward). The second term accounts for attentions O(s2) cost. Why MFU Decreases with Scale Larger models require more parallelism, which introduces: 1. Communication overhead: AllGather/ReduceScatter for FSDP (1015% at 64 GPUs) 2. Pipeline bubbles: PP introduces idle time at start/end of micro-batches (1525% with PP=4) 3. Memory for auxiliary models: Reference/RM take GPU memory that could hold larger batches 4. Load imbalance: Not all layers have equal compute (embeddings vs transformer blocks) 214 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 11.8: MFU benchmarks across scales and hardware Model Hardware MFU Tokens/sec/GPU Configuration LLaMA-7B 8A100 LLaMA-13B 16A100 LLaMA-70B 64A100 57% 52% 45% GPT-4 (est.) PaLM-540B 10,000+ H100 6144 TPUv4 4050% 46% 3,200 1,750 FlashAttn, FlashAttn, FSDP, BF16 FSDP, BF16 FSDP+TP=8, FlashAttn 3D parallelism DP+TP+PP Rule of thumb: Target MFU > 40% for training. If below 30%, diagnose with profiling.

sections.title: 11.11.2 Compute-Optimal Batch Sizing
sections.content: The effective batch size interacts with hardware utilization in non-obvious ways: Effective batch size = micro_batch grad_accum DP degree (11.7) Too small: GPU underutilized (low arithmetic intensity), communication dominates. Too large: Diminishing learning per token (critical batch size exceeded), wastes compute. Sweet spot: The critical batch size Bcrit where gradient noise equals gradient signal. For LLMs, Bcrit 14M tokens [222]. For RLHF specifically, the batch contains rollouts (not just tokens): RLHF batch = Nprompts Kgenerations Lavg response length (11.8) Typical production values: = 128 prompts, = 14 generations, = 256512 tokens 32K256K tokens per step.

sections.title: 11.11.3 Profiling and Bottleneck Diagnosis
sections.content: Key profiling tools and what they reveal:

sections.title: Best For
sections.content: torch.profiler NVIDIA Nsight Systems nccl_debug=INFO torch.cuda.memory_stats Allocation patterns DeepSpeed Flops Profiler py-spy / scalene Kernel timing, memory Full GPU timeline Collective sizes/times Per-layer FLOPs CPU profiling Finding slow ops, memory leaks Visualizing overlap, gaps between kernels Diagnosing communication bottlenecks Finding fragmentation, peak usage Identifying load imbalance Data loading, tokenization bottlenecks Diagnosing Low MFU: Checklist 1. GPU utilization < 80%? Data loading bottleneck (check CPU, I/O). 2. Large gaps between kernels? Python overhead, synchronization points. Use CUDA graphs. 3. Communication > 20% of step time? Reduce TP degree, increase batch size, check network health. 4. Memory at 99%? Cannot increase batch. Try gradient checkpointing, offloading. 5. OOM during generation? KV cache too large. Reduce max_seq_len or batch size for 215 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems gen.

sections.title: 11.12 Cost Analysis and Cloud Deployment
sections.content: Understanding the economics of RLHF training is essential for planning.

sections.title: 11.12.2 RLHF Training Cost Estimation
sections.content: Cost = Nsteps Tstep 3600 NGPUs CGPU/hr (11.9) Cost Example: 70B Model RLHF (10K steps)

sections.title: Steps
sections.content: 10,000 Time per step (decoupled) Total training time GPUs (generation + training) Cost per GPU-hour (spot) Total cost Breakdown by phase: 45 seconds 10000 45/3600 = 125 hours 64 A100-80GB $1.20 125 64 $1.20 = $9,600 Generation cluster (32 GPUs): $4,800 (60% of time) Training cluster (32 GPUs): $4,800 (could overlap $3,400 effective) Scoring (shared with gen GPUs): included above With overlap: Effective cost $7,500 for full RLHF alignment of 70B model.

sections.title: 11.12.3 Cost Optimization Strategies
sections.content: Spot/preemptible instances: 5070% savings. Requires robust checkpointing (save every 5 minutes). Right-sizing: Dont use H100 for generation (memory-bound); A100 achieves similar tokens/$ for inference. Quantized inference: INT8/FP8 for generation and scoring halves GPU count for those clusters. Progressive training: Start with 8B proxy model for reward engineering/debugging ($200), then scale to 70B. LoRA for reference-free: Eliminates reference model entirely (50% memory reduction). 216 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Shorter sequences first: Curriculum from 2565121024 token generations saves 40% compute.

sections.title: 11.13 Distributed Checkpointing
sections.content: At scale, naive checkpointing becomes bottleneck. 70B model with optimizer state requires saving 840 GB per checkpoint (FP32 master weights + Adam + v).

sections.title: 11.13.1 Checkpointing Strategies
sections.content: Table 11.10: Checkpointing approaches for large-scale RLHF Strategy Save Time (70B) Storage/ckpt Characteristics Synchronous (all ranks) Async (background copy) <1s (non-blocking) Incremental (delta) Sharded (FSDP native) 3060s (blocking) <1s 510s 420 GB 420 GB 520 GB 420 GB sharded Simple, stalls training Overlaps with next step Only save changed params Each rank saves its shard

sections.title: 11.13.2 Production Checkpointing with torch.distributed.checkpoint
sections.content: import torch . distributed . checkpoint as dcp from torch . distributed . checkpoint . state_dict import get_state_dict , StateDictOptions # Save : each rank writes its shard in parallel state_dict = { " model " : get_state_dict ( model , options = StateDictOptions ( full_state_dict = False ) ) } dcp . save ( state_dict = state_dict , storage_writer = dcp . FileSystemWriter ( " / mnt / checkpoints / step_5000 " ) , planner = dcp . De fa ultSavePlanner () , # Handles FSDP sharding automatically ) # Async save : non - blocking , runs in background thread future = dcp . async_save ( state_dict = state_dict , storage_writer = dcp . FileSystemWriter ( " / mnt / checkpoints / step_5000 " ) , ) # Training continues immediately ; future . result () blocks only if needed

sections.title: Checkpoint Hygiene for RLHF
sections.content: RLHF checkpoints must capture more than standard pre-training: Policy model weights + optimizer states (standard) KL coefficient (β) and its schedule state Replay buffer contents (for off-policy corrections) RNG states for all GPUs (reproducibility) Prompt iterator position (avoid re-processing prompts) Reward model version tag (for auditability) Wandb/metrics run ID (for continuous logging) 217 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 11.14 Hardware Selection Guide
sections.content: Choosing the right hardware depends on model size, budget, and training phase. Table 11.11: Hardware recommendations by model scale and training phase Model Size Training Phase Recommended Configuration 7B 713B 1334B 70B 70B >100B SFT + RLHF SFT + RLHF SFT + RLHF RLHF (full) RLHF (LoRA) RLHF 12 A100 48 A100 816 A100/H100 3264 A100/H100 816 A100/H100 128+ H100 Single node, no parallelism needed FSDP, optional TP=2 for gen FSDP + TP=4 for gen Decoupled, FSDP + TP=8 No ref model, LoRA adapters 3D parallelism (TP+PP+DP) H100 vs A100: When is the Upgrade Worth It? H100 provides: 1.6 peak FLOPS (989 vs 624 TFLOPS for BF16 with sparsity; 495 vs 312 without sparsity) 2 memory bandwidth (3.35 vs 2.0 TB/s) FP8 support (additional 2 for inference) NVLink 4.0 (900 vs 600 GB/s) For training: 1.82.2 faster end-to-end (FP8 support and higher bandwidth amplify the raw FLOPS advantage). For generation: 1.7 faster (bandwidth-bound, so 2 BW 1.7 throughput with overhead). Cost-performance: At 1.5 the price, H100 is almost always better value for training. For inference-only (generation clusters), A100 at spot pricing can be more cost-effective.

sections.title: 11.15 Optimizer Configuration for RL Training
sections.content: RL training (PPO, GRPO, DPO) imposes unique demands on the optimizer compared to pretraining or SFT. The loss landscape is non-stationary (the policy changes what data is generated), gradients are noisier (reward signal variance), and training is more prone to catastrophic forgetting or reward hacking. This section consolidates RL-specific optimizer guidance, using AdamW [80] as the default optimizer.

sections.title: 11.15.1 Why RL Requires Different Optimizer Settings
sections.content: RL vs. SFT Optimization Key Differences Non-stationary data distribution: unlike SFT where the dataset is fixed, RL generates new rollouts each iterationthe data distribution shifts with the policy. High gradient variance: reward signals are sparse and noisy; gradients have much higher variance than cross-entropy on curated data. Smaller updates required: the policy must stay close to the reference model (KL constraint), so learning rates are 10100 smaller than SFT. No weight decay: regularization comes from the KL penalty, not weight decay. Adding WD on top can fight the KL constraint. Shorter warmup: RL starts from converged SFT checkpointthe optimizer state needs minimal warmup. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 11.15.2 Recommended Hyperparameters by RL Method
sections.content: Table 11.12: Optimizer settings for RL training phases. All use β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.95, ϵ = 108, max_grad_norm=1.0, BF16. Method Optimizer LR WD Warmup Schedule AdamW DPO PPO (policy) AdamW AdamW PPO (critic) AdamW GRPO 5e-7 1e-6 1e-6 1e0.0 0.0 0.0 0.0 50 steps 20 steps 20 steps 20 steps Constant or Linear Constant Constant Constant Why Constant Schedule for RL? Cosine and linear-decay schedules assume fixed training horizon and monotonically decreasing loss. RL training has neither: reward may plateau, spike, or oscillate unpredictably. constant LR (after brief warmup) keeps the optimizer responsive throughout training. If you must decay, use very gentle linear schedule with high minimum LR ratio ( 0.5).

sections.title: 11.15.3 Beta-2 = 0.95 for RL: Faster Adaptation
sections.content: The default Adam β2 = 0.999 gives very long memory for the second moment (1000-step effective window). In RL training, the loss landscape changes rapidly as the policy evolvesthe gradient variance from 1000 steps ago is irrelevant. Using β2 = 0.95 shortens the window to 20 steps, making the adaptive learning rate respond quickly to changing gradient statistics. When beta2 = 0.95 Hurts For very small batch sizes (e.g., batch=1 in online RL), β2 = 0.95 can make the second moment estimates too noisy. In this regime, use β2 = 0.99 as compromise, or increase the effective batch size via gradient accumulation.

sections.title: 11.15.4 Mixed Precision for RL: FP32 Master Weights Are Critical
sections.content: RL training is particularly sensitive to numerical precision: Gradients are noisiersmall updates must accumulate accurately over many steps Learning rates are very small (106107), making θ θ BF16 mantissa (7 bits 0.8% relative precision) cannot represent updates of magnitude 106 relative to weights of magnitude 100 Always use FP32 master weights for RL training. BF16-only training (no FP32 copy) reliably causes reward collapse in PPO/GRPO after 100500 steps.

sections.title: 11.15.5 Gradient Clipping is Critical for RL
sections.content: In PPO and GRPO, the reward signal can be highly variable, especially early in training. single bad batch can produce gradients with norm > 100, which would completely destroy the model weights. max_grad_norm=1.0 is the standard setting. For SFT, clipping is less critical but still recommended.

sections.title: Never Disable Gradient Clipping for RL
sections.content: Unlike SFT where gradient norms are typically stable (0.11.0 range), RL gradients are spiky because: (1) reward variance propagates through the policy gradient, (2) rare high-reward trajectories create outsized updates, and (3) the KL penalty term can produce large gradients when the policy drifts. single unclipped step with > 50 can undo hundreds of training steps. 219 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 11.15.6 Diagnosing RL Training Instability
sections.content: Red Flags and Fixes for RL Optimization Symptom Likely Cause and Fix Reward improves then collapses LR too high or KL coefficient too low. Reduce LR by 25 or increase βKL. Gradient norm constantly at clip threshold Updates too aggressive. Reduce LR (clipping means youre KL divergence explodes (>15 nats) Reward stuck at baseline Loss NaN after 100+ steps losing gradient direction info every step). LR too high. Reduce by 10 or add adaptive KL penalty. LR too low, or reward model has low signal. Try 25 higher LR. Check reward model calibration. FP32 master weights missing, or grad norm overflow. Enable FP32 master weights; verify BF16 mode.

sections.title: 11.15.7 HuggingFace TRL Configuration for RL
sections.content: The TRL library [176] provides production-ready implementations of PPO, DPO, and other RL methods for LLMs. from trl import PPOConfig , PPOTrainer , DPOConfig , DPOTrainer # --- PPO Configuration --- ppo_config = PPOConfig ( # Optimizer ( AdamW with RL - specific settings ) learning_rate =1 -6 , # 10 -100 smaller than SFT # PPO - specific ppo_epochs =4 , mini_batch_size =16 , batch_size =64 , # Gradient control max_grad_norm =1.0 , # mini - batch updates per rollout # rollout batch size # KL penalty ( replaces weight decay as regularizer ) init_kl_coef =0.2 , adap_kl_ctrl = True , target_kl =6.0 , # initial KL penalty coefficient # adaptive KL targeting # target KL divergence # Mixed precision bf16 = True , ) ppo_trainer = PPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , config = ppo_config , tokenizer = tokenizer , dataset = dataset , ) # --- DPO Configuration --- dpo_config = DPOConfig ( output_dir = " ./ dpo_output " , # Optimizer learning_rate =5 -7 , optim = " adamw_torch " , adam_beta1 =0.9 , adam_beta2 =0.95 , weight_decay =0.0 , # Schedule # BF16 compute , FP32 master weights # even smaller than PPO # shorter memory for RL # no WD -- KL provides regularization 220 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems lr_s cheduler_ty pe = " co stant_with_warmup " , warmup_steps =50 , # Gradient control max_grad_norm =1.0 , # DPO - specific beta =0.1 , loss_type = " sigmoid " , # Mixed precision bf16 = True , # KL constraint strength # standard DPO loss # Training num_train_epochs =1 , _ i _ i _ c _ e =4 , d t _ u a n _ p =8 , # DPO typically 1 epoch ) dpo_trainer = DPOTrainer ( model = model , ref_model = ref_model , args = dpo_config , train_dataset = dataset , tokenizer = tokenizer , ) dpo_trainer . train () Listing 11.1: Complete PPO and DPO optimizer configuration using TRL.

sections.title: 11.15.8 MoE Considerations for RL Training
sections.content: MoE for RLHF Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models [109] are increasingly used in RLHF: Advantage: 34 more capacity at same compute cost. Better for reward models (more capacity to judge). Challenge: Expert parallelism requires all-to-all communication (tokens routed across GPUs). This conflicts with pipeline parallelism. GRPO with MoE: Works well since generation cost is dominated by active params (not total params). LoRA for MoE: Can apply LoRA to router + shared layers only, or to all experts (expensive).

sections.title: The RL Optimizer Mantra
sections.content: For RL fine-tuning: small LR, no weight decay, constant schedule, FP32 master weights, aggressive clipping. Let the KL penalty handle regularizationthe optimizers job is just to follow the policy gradient without overshooting. 221 Chapter

sections.title: 12.1 Motivation: From Chatbots to Autonomous Agents
sections.content: Modern LLMs are increasingly deployed not just as conversational assistants but as autonomous agents that interact with external tools, APIs, databases, and environments over multiple steps. This shiftfrom single-turn chatbots to multi-step agentsintroduces fundamentally new RL challenges that require rethinking how we train, evaluate, and deploy language models. (a) Traditional Chatbot (b) Autonomous Agent User prompt response LLM Single turn, immediate feedback User task LLM Agent act obs Tools execute reward Environment Multi-step, sparse terminal reward Figure 12.1: From Chatbots to Autonomous Agents: Traditional LLM chatbots operate in single-step conversational loop with immediate human feedback. Autonomous agents plan across multiple tool interactions, receive feedback from real-world execution environments, and optimize for sparse terminal rewards (task success/failure). The key differences that demand new RL approaches: Multi-step reasoning: Agents must plan across 10100+ tool calls, not just generate single response. External environment feedback: Rewards come from real-world execution (test suites pass, web pages load, code compiles) not just human preference scores. Structured actions: Actions are not just tokens but structured outputs (JSON tool calls, API payloads, code blocks). Long horizons with sparse rewards: Success/failure may only be determined after many intermediate steps.

sections.title: Why Standard RLHF Falls Short for Agents
sections.content: Standard RLHF (PPO/DPO) optimizes for single-turn quality: given prompt, produce good response. But agents must: Decide when to use tools vs. reason internally Recover from errors mid-trajectory (self-correction) 222 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Balance exploration (try new approaches) with exploitation (use known-good patterns) Handle partial observability (tool outputs may be incomplete or noisy) This requires training methods that reason over entire trajectories, not individual turns.

sections.title: 12.2 Trajectory Buffers for LLM Agents
sections.content: In the context of LLM agents, traditional RL replay buffers undergo structural transformation. Instead of storing low-dimensional numerical tensors, agentic buffers often called Trajectory Buffers, Experience Pools, or Memory Banks manage complex textual histories, tool execution outputs, and explicit reasoning steps.

sections.title: 12.2.1 Mathematical Structure of an LLM Agent Buffer
sections.content: In classic RL, replay buffer stores flat tuple (s, a, r, s). For an LLM agent, this expands into high-dimensional tokenized text structures: et = (St, At, Rt, St+1) (12.1) St: The complete context state system prompt, user objective, conversation history, and current environmental variables (e.g., HTML source code, directory structures, database schemas). At: The agents generated output, typically composed of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning string followed by structured tool call: At = {textreasoning, jsontool_call} (12.2) Rt: Evaluation signals derived from external execution environments (unit test passes, compiler flags, API response codes) or verified by an LLM-as-a-judge system. St+1: The updated context window, which appends tool output text or error logs directly into the conversation history. Concrete Agent Trajectory: Code Debugging Step 1: S1 = Fix the failing test in utils.py A1 = Let me read the file first + read_file("utils.py") R1 = 0 (intermediate step) Step 2: S2 = [previous context + file contents] A2 = The bug is on line 42, off-by-one error + edit_file("utils.py", ...) R2 = 0 (intermediate step) Step 3: S3 = [previous context + edit confirmation] A3 = Let me verify the fix + run_tests() R3 = +1.0 (all tests pass sparse terminal reward) 223 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.3 Operational Paradigms
sections.content: LLM agents leverage specialized trajectory buffers through three primary optimization methodologies:

sections.title: 12.3.1 A. Self-Correction and Thought Refinement
sections.content: Two representative methods in this category are STaR [223] and Reflexion [224]. When an agent fails multi-step execution trace, the sub-optimal sequence is saved to the buffer. The framework later samples this trajectory and prompts the LLM to generate an explicit textual critique of its past performance: Critique LLM(Sfailed, Afailed, R=0) (12.3) Once corrected trajectory achieves positive reward, it is moved to an optimal experience pool used to update the network weights via fine-tuning (SFT on successful trajectories) or RL (GRPO [14] with binary pass/fail rewards). STaR: Self-Taught Reasoner 1. Generate reasoning traces for batch of problems 2. Filter: keep only traces that lead to correct answers 3. Fine-tune the model on successful traces (SFT) 4. Repeat: the improved model generates better traces in the next iteration Each iteration bootstraps the models reasoning ability using its own successful outputs as training data. Reflexion: Verbal Reinforcement Learning 1. Agent attempts task, fails 2. Agent generates verbal reflection: failed because didnt check the return type before calling the API 3. Reflection is stored in an episodic memory buffer 4. On the next attempt, reflections are injected into the prompt as lessons learned 5. No weight updates needed pure in-context learning from self-critique

sections.title: 12.3.2 B. Off-Policy Exploration
sections.content: This paradigm, exemplified by ReAct [127] and related tool-use frameworks, involves extensive autonomous exploration. During autonomous exploration (web navigation, database querying, code generation), agents log thousands of exploratory execution paths. The trajectory buffer acts as filter: Success filtering: Only trajectories achieving the goal are kept for training. Efficiency ranking: Among successful traces, prefer the shortest/most efficient tool-use paths. Diversity sampling: Maintain diverse set of solution strategies to prevent mode collapse. The optimization algorithm (typically GRPO [14] or filtered SFT) computes losses exclusively over efficient, successful trajectories while discarding meandering runs. 224 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.3.3 C. Non-Parametric In-Context Learning (RAG over Experiences)
sections.content: Instead of modifying neural network weights, the trajectory buffer can function as vector database. Given new user goal Gnew, the system retrieves the most relevant past experiences: Eretrieved = arg max eB sim(Embed(Gnew), Embed(e)) (12.4) The top-k similar successful historical runs are injected directly into the prompt context as few-shot demonstrations. This approach: Requires zero training pure retrieval-augmented generation Adapts instantly to new tasks if similar experiences exist in the buffer Scales with buffer size (more experiences = better coverage) Complements parametric learning (use retrieval for rare cases, weights for common patterns)

sections.title: 12.4 Paradigm Comparison
sections.content: Table 12.1: Traditional RL Buffers vs. LLM Agent Buffers

sections.title:
Data Format
