Reward Source Environment simulator
sections.content: Continuous vectors / tensors Massive (105107 items) Breaking data correlation Random uniform / PER Fixed (e.g., 8484 pixels) Discrete/continuous vectors Tokenized text, JSON, code blocks, tool outputs Small to medium (103105 traces) Providing reasoning demonstrations Semantic retrieval / success priority / diversity Variable (1K128K tokens per state) Structured text (reasoning + tool calls) External execution / LLM judge / unit tests

sections.title: 12.5 Major Techniques in Agentic RL
sections.content: Table 12.2: Key methods for training LLM agents with RL.

sections.title: Key Idea
sections.content: STaR [223]

sections.title: Iterative SFT
sections.content: Reflexion [224] In-context RL ReAct [127]

sections.title: Prompting
sections.content: LATS [225]

sections.title: Tree search
sections.content: AgentQ [226] Off-policy RL OpenHands [227] GRPO Voyager [228]

sections.title: Skill library
sections.content: RLEF [229]

sections.title: Online RL
sections.content: Bootstrap reasoning by fine-tuning on own successful traces Verbal self-critique stored as episodic memory; no weight updates Interleave reasoning (think) and acting (tool call) in single generation Monte Carlo Tree Search over action sequences; backpropagate rewards DPO on agent trajectories with AI-generated preference pairs Group-relative optimization with executionbased rewards (tests pass/fail) Successful code snippets stored and retrieved for compositional reuse RL from Execution Feedback binary reward from code/test execution 225 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.5.1 STaR: Self-Taught Reasoner (Detailed)
sections.content: STaR [223] is an iterative self-improvement method that bootstraps reasoning capabilities without external reward models. The core insight: if the model can occasionally solve problem correctly, it can learn from its own successes. Algorithm: 1. Generate: For each problem xi in dataset D, sample reasoning trace zi πθ(xi) followed by an answer ˆyi. 2. Filter: Keep only traces where ˆyi = (correct answer). Define success set Dpass = {(xi, zi, i ) : ˆyi = }. 3. Rationalization (key innovation): For problems where the model failed, generate ratioi ). This teaches the nalization trace conditioned on the correct answer: zrat model to reason backward from solutions. πθ(xi, 4. Fine-tune: Update θ via SFT on Dpass Drationalized. 5. Iterate: Repeat from step 1 with the improved model. θk+1 = arg min θ (x,z,y)D+ log πθ(z, yx) (12.5) Convergence dynamics: Each iteration increases the models solve rate pk. If p0 = 0.3 (solves 30% of problems), after rationalization + SFT, p1 0.5. Typically converges in 35 iterations to 0.70.9.

sections.title: STaR Rationalization Prompt
sections.content: # Standard generation ( Step 1) : PROMPT = " " " Solve the following problem step by step . Problem : store has 45 apples . It sells 3/5 of them . How many remain ? Let think step by step : " " " # Rationalization prompt ( Step 3 - conditioned on correct answer ) : PR OMPT_RAT IONALI ZE = " " " Solve the following problem step by step . The correct answer is 18. Problem : store has 45 apples . It sells 3/5 of them . How many remain ? Let think step by step to arrive at 18: " " " # Agent variant ( code task with error conditioning ) : M _ AG _R O NA E = " " " The following code task failed with the error below . Generate correct solution step by step . Task : Implement binary search that handles duplicates . Previous error : IndexError : list index out of range ( line 12) Correct behavior : Return leftmost index of target . Let me fix this by reasoning about the boundary conditions : " " "

sections.title: STaR Variants for Agents
sections.content: Quiet-STaR [230]: Inserts thinking tokens between every token of generation. The model learns to reason implicitly without explicit CoT prompting. Training objective: predict next tokens better when thinking tokens are included. STaR for Code Agents: Replace answer verification with test execution. Correct = all tests pass. Rationalization = generate new approach conditioned on the error message. 226 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems V-STaR [231]: Adds verifier model trained on (z, y, correct/incorrect) triples. The verifier provides process-level supervision, filtering bad reasoning traces that accidentally reach correct answers.

sections.title: 12.5.2 Reflexion: Verbal Reinforcement Learning (Detailed)
sections.content: Reflexion [224] introduces radical paradigm: RL without weight updates. Instead of gradientbased learning, the agent improves through natural-language self-critique stored in an episodic memory. Full Architecture: 1. Actor: The LLM agent π that executes actions in the environment. 2. Evaluator: binary signal (task success/failure) or scalar heuristic (e.g., number of test cases passed). 3. Self-Reflection Generator: Given the failed trajectory τfail and environment feedback, generates natural-language reflection rtext: rtext = LLMreflect(τfail, feedback, task) (12.6) 4. Episodic Memory: sliding window buffer = [r1, r2, . . . , rm] of past reflections (typically 3 to fit in context). 5. Retry Loop: On the next attempt, reflections are injected into the prompt: at+1 π( task, M, current_state) (12.7) Example reflection: In my previous attempt, called the search API before validating the input format, which caused 400 error. Next time, should validate the JSON schema first, then make the API call. Reflexion: Agent Prompt with Injected Memory # === ATTEMPT 2 PROMPT ( after first failure ) === SYSTEM = " " " You are coding agent . You can run bash commands and edit files . Complete the task below . Learn from your previous reflections . " " " USER = " " " Task : Fix the failing test in auth_service . py === REFLECTIONS FROM PREVIOUS ATTEMPTS === [ Attempt 1 reflection ]: tried to modify the authenticate () function directly but forgot that it depends on token_validator () . The test failed because token_validator () was still returning the old format . should trace the dependency chain FIRST : check what authenticate () calls , then fix the root cause ( token_validator ) , not the symptom . === END REFLECTIONS === The repository is in / workspace /. The failing test is : test_auth . py :: t _ i _ e _ u _ 4 0 1 Begin by reading the relevant files , then fix the issue . " " " Strengths and Limitations: 227 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Strengths Limitations Zero gradient computation; works with frozen API models (GPT-4) Fast iteration (seconds per retry vs. hours for RL training) Interpretable: human-readable self-corrections Composes with any base agent architecture Limited to context window; cant accumulate infinite knowledge No generalization to unseen tasks (memory is taskspecific) Relies on the models existing ability to identify errors Degrades when base model is too weak to generate useful critiques

sections.title: 12.5.3 ReAct: Reasoning + Acting (Detailed)
sections.content: ReAct [127] establishes the dominant prompting paradigm for tool-using agents by interleaving explicit reasoning steps with environment actions in single generation stream. ReAct System Prompt (Typical) SYSTEM = " " " You are research assistant with access to the following tools : - search ( query ) : Search the web for information - lookup ( term ) : Look up term on the current Wikipedia page - finish ( answer ) : Submit your final answer Solve the user question by interleaving Thought and Action steps . Format : Thought : < your reasoning about what to do next > Action : < tool_name >( arguments ) You will receive an Observation after each Action . Use it to plan your next step . Continue until you can provide final answer . Rules : - Always think before acting - Never guess - verify with tools - Use finish () only when you are confident in your answer " " " USER = " Who was the president of the US when the iPhone was released ? " Generation format: ReAct Trace Format Thought : need to find when the iPhone was released , then determine who was president at that time . Let me search for the release date . Action : search ( " iPhone original release date " ) Observation : The original iPhone was released on June 29 , 2007. Thought : The iPhone came out June 2007. Now need to find who was the US president in June 2007. Action : lookup ( " President of the United States in 2007 " ) Observation : George . Bush served as the 43 rd president from January 20 , 2001 to January 20 , 2009. Thought : George . Bush was president from 2001 -2009 , which covers June 2007 when the iPhone was released . have my answer . Action : finish ( " George . Bush was the US president when the iPhone was released on June 29 , 2007. " ) Formal definition: ReAct trajectory is τ = (t1, a1, o1, t2, a2, o2, . . .) where: ti: Thought (internal reasoning, not executed) ai: Action (tool call, executed in environment) oi: Observation (environment response, appended to context) 228 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Why it works: Thoughts create an inner monologue that helps the model plan before acting, reducing impulsive tool calls. The explicit reasoning trace also makes the agents decision process auditable and debuggable. Training ReAct agents with RL: Action-level rewards: Only actions receive reward signals (thoughts are auxiliary). Thought quality: Implicitly optimized better thoughts better actions higher rewards. Format enforcement: Include format penalties in the reward for malformed actions (missing JSON, hallucinated tools). RL objective: r(τ ) = rtask λformat format_violations λlength num_steps

sections.title: 12.5.4 LATS: Language Agent Tree Search (Detailed)
sections.content: LATS [225] applies Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to LLM agent action selection, trading inference compute for significantly better trajectories. Algorithm (adapted for LLM agents): 1. Selection: Starting from root (initial state), traverse the tree using UCB1: UCB(s, a) = Q(s, a) + ln (s) (s, a) (12.8) where = average reward of subtree, = visit counts, = exploration constant. 2. Expansion: At leaf node, generate candidate actions via LLM sampling (temperature > 0): {a1, . . . , ak} πθ(sleaf) 3. Simulation: For each candidate, execute the action in the environment and continue with fast rollout policy (greedy decoding) until terminal state or depth limit. 4. Backpropagation: Propagate the terminal reward up through all ancestor nodes, updating and counts. 5. Repeat: Run steps 14 for fixed computation budget (e.g., 50200 iterations). 6. Action selection: Choose the most-visited child of the root. LLM-specific adaptations: Value function: Use separate LLM call to estimate state value: On scale of 01, how likely is this state to lead to task success? Reflection-based pruning: When branch fails, generate reflection and prune similar branches. Caching: Store LLM outputs at each node to avoid redundant generation during backtracking. Depth budget: Limit tree depth to 1020 steps (agents rarely need more). Performance: On WebShop (web navigation), LATS achieves 75% success vs. ReActs 40%. On HumanEval (code), pass@1 improves from 68% 94% with tree search. The cost: 1050 more inference FLOPs per task. LATS Prompts: Value Estimation and Node Expansion # === VALUE ESTIMATION PROMPT ( used during simulation ) === VALUE_PROMPT = " " " You are evaluating an agent progress on task . 229 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Task : Book flight from NYC to London for under $500 , departing Dec 15. Current state ( after 3 actions ) : - Searched flights on Kayak : found 12 results - Filtered by price < $500 : 4 options remain - Clicked on British Airways $489 option : viewing details page On scale of 0.0 to 1.0 , how likely is the agent to successfully complete the task from this state ? Consider : - How close is the agent to the goal ? - Are there remaining obstacles ( payment , seat selection ) ? - Has the agent made any errors that need correction ? Score : " " " # Model outputs . . "0.75" # === NODE EXPANSION PROMPT ( generating candidate actions ) === EXPAND_PROMPT = " " " You are web navigation agent . Given the current page state , propose 3 DIFFERENT next actions to try . Current page : British Airways booking - flight details Price : $489 Departure : Dec 15 8:30 am Arrival : Dec 15 8:45 pm [ Button : Select ] [ Button : Back to results ] [ Link : Fare rules ] Generate 3 diverse candidate actions ( explore different strategies ) : Action 1: " " " # Model generates 3 options for tree expansion

sections.title: 12.5.5 AgentQ: DPO on Agent Trajectories (Detailed)
sections.content: AgentQ [226] bridges offline preference learning (DPO) with online agent execution by automatically generating preference pairs from trajectory outcomes. Pipeline: 1. Rollout: Execute trajectories per task using the current policy πθ. 2. Evaluate: Score each trajectory with execution-based reward (binary pass/fail or scalar metric). 3. Pair construction: For each task, construct preference pairs: Among trajectories for the same task, the one with highest reward = chosen; lowest = rejected. (τw, τl) where r(τw) > r(τl) (12.9) 4. DPO update: Apply standard DPO loss over trajectory-level log-probabilities: LAgentQ = log σ (cid:18) (cid:20) log β πθ(τw) πref(τw) log (cid:21)(cid:19) πθ(τl) πref(τl) (12.10) 5. Iterate: Updated πθ generates new (better) trajectories in the next round. Key design choices: MCTS-guided exploration: Use LATS during rollout phase to generate diverse, high-quality trajectories (better training data). Step-level DPO: Instead of comparing full trajectories, compare at the action level given the same prefix, which next action leads to success? Self-play improvement: Each DPO iteration produces better policy that generates better trajectories that produce better training pairs virtuous cycle. Results: On WebShop, AgentQ achieves absolute 50% 82% success rate improvement over the base policy in 3 DPO iterations. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.5.6 Voyager: Lifelong Learning via Skill Libraries (Detailed)
sections.content: Voyager [228] introduces compositional skill accumulation the agent builds growing library of reusable code functions that serve as high-level actions. Architecture: 1. Automatic Curriculum: An LLM proposes progressively harder tasks based on the agents current skill inventory: You can now mine wood and craft planks. Next challenge: build crafting table. 2. Skill Generation: For each task, the agent writes JavaScript function (executable code) that solves it: skilli = LLM(taski, environment_docs, error_feedback) (12.11) 3. Verification: Execute the code in the environment. If it succeeds, add to the skill library. If not, iterate with error feedback (up to 5 retries). 4. Skill Library (vector DB): Each verified skill stored with: Function signature + docstring (for retrieval) Embedding of the task description (for semantic search) Dependencies (which other skills it calls) 5. Retrieval + Composition: For new tasks, retrieve the top-k most relevant skills and compose them: solution = LLM(new_task, retrieve(skill_library, k=5)) (12.12) Key insight: Skills are compositional complex behaviors emerge from combining simple verified functions. The agent never forgets (library is persistent) and improves monotonically (only verified skills are added). Voyager: Curriculum and Skill Generation Prompts # === AUTOMATIC CURRICULUM PROMPT === CURR ICULUM_PROM PT = " " " You are curriculum designer for an AI agent . Agent current skill inventory : - mine_wood () : Mines nearby oak / birch trees - craft_planks () : Converts logs to planks - craft_sticks () : Converts planks to sticks - mine_stone () : Mines stone with wooden pickaxe Propose the next task that : 1. Builds on existing skills ( reachable from current abilities ) 2. Introduces exactly ONE new concept or challenge 3. Is concrete and verifiable ( clear success condition ) Next task proposal : " " " # Output : " Craft furnace ( requires 8 cobblestone blocks arranged in square ) . You already know mine_stone () ." # # === SKILL GENERATION PROMPT === SKILL_GEN_PROMPT = " " " Write JavaScript function to accomplish this task in Minecraft . Use the bot API ( bot . dig , bot . craft , bot . equip , etc .) Task : Smelt 5 iron ingots using furnace . Prerequisites available : mine_stone () , craft_furnace () , mine_iron_ore () Error from previous attempt : " Cannot smelt without fuel in furnace " Write the corrected function : async function smeltIronIngots ( bot , count =5) { " " " 231 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.5.7 RLEF: RL from Execution Feedback (Detailed)
sections.content: RLEF [229] applies online RL with deterministic execution-based rewards to code generation agents, establishing the simplest effective paradigm for agentic training. Training loop: 1. Sample task: Draw coding problem with test cases (x, tests) from the training set. 2. Generate: The agent produces solution trajectory (reading files, writing code, running tests) using the current policy πθ. 3. Execute: Run the test suite in sandboxed environment. Reward: = # tests passed # total tests [0, 1] (12.13) 4. Update: Apply GRPO/PPO using as the reward signal. 5. Repeat: Thousands of iterations with fresh tasks. Why execution feedback is ideal for RL: Zero noise: Unlike human preferences, test results are deterministic. Same code same reward every time. This eliminates reward noise that destabilizes RL training. Infinite scale: Can generate unlimited tasks programmatically (random algorithms, API integration tests, data transformations). No reward hacking: Unlike learned reward models, test suite cant be fooled (assuming tests are well-written). The agent must actually solve the problem. Dense signal: Partial test passage (r = 0.6) provides richer gradient than binary pass/fail.

sections.title: 12.5.8 OpenHands / SWE-Agent: GRPO for Software Engineering
sections.content: OpenHands [227] and SWE-Agent [232] apply GRPO to train agents that autonomously resolve GitHub issues reading code, writing patches, and running test suites. Training specifics: Environment: Docker container with full repo, test suite, and developer tools (git, grep, lint). Action space: Bash commands, file edits, git operations, test execution. Trajectory length: 1550 actions typical for resolving GitHub issue. Reward: Binary does the generated patch pass the issues regression tests? Group size: = 816 trajectories per issue for GRPO normalization. Curriculum: Start with issues labeled good first issue, progress to complex multi-file refactors. State-of-the-art results: SWE-bench Verified: 30% 55% resolve rate after RL training (vs. SFT-only baseline). H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems OpenHands / SWE-Agent: System Prompt SYSTEM = " " " You are an autonomous software engineer . You are given GitHub issue to resolve . You have access to the full repository in / workspace / and can execute any bash command . AVAILABLE COMMANDS : - bash ( command ) : Execute shell command - edit ( file , start_line , end_line , new_content ) : Edit file - search ( pattern , path ) : Search for text in files - submit () : Submit your patch when done WORKFLOW : 1. Read the issue carefully and understand the expected behavior 2. Explore the codebase to find relevant files 3. Reproduce the bug ( write / run test that fails ) 4. Implement the fix 5. Verify the fix ( run the test again - must pass ) 6. Run the full test suite to check for regressions 7. Submit when all tests pass RULES : - Do NOT modify test files unless the issue explicitly asks for it - Prefer minimal , targeted changes over large refactors - Always verify your fix before submitting " " " USER = " " " GitHub Issue #4521: DataFrame . merge () silently drops rows when on column contains NaN values . Expected : NaN keys should be preserved ( matched with other NaN rows ) Actual : Rows with NaN keys are dropped entirely Repository : / workspace / pandas - dev / pandas / " " " The Future: RL + Agents The field is converging on pattern: online RL with execution-based rewards applied to multi-step agent trajectories. Key trends: GRPO/PPO with binary pass/fail rewards from code execution or tool success Curriculum learning: start with easy tasks, progressively increase difficulty Trajectory-level optimization (not token-level) reward only at the end of multi-step sequence Hybrid approaches: use retrieval (non-parametric) for rare tasks + RL (parametric) for common ones Scaling law: more compute at inference (search/retry) often beats more training compute 233 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.6 Use Case: Agentic RL for a Productivity Co-pilot
sections.content: This section provides complete blueprint for applying agentic RL techniques to train an LLM-based co-pilot that operates across productivity application suite (documents, spreadsheets, presentations, email, messaging, cloud storage).

sections.title: 12.6.1 Architecture Overview
sections.content: Figure 12.2: Productivity co-pilot architecture: the LLM agent (with RL policy πθ) receives user intents and interacts with multiple application APIs. reward signal based on task success, user feedback, and efficiency metrics drives policy improvement.

sections.title: 12.6.2 Formal MDP Definition for a Productivity Co-pilot
sections.content: The productivity co-pilot environment is formalized as Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP): = S, A, , R, Ω, O, γ (12.14) S: State space Full workspace environment state: document contents, email threads, calendar events, file system, user permissions. Not fully observable: agent sees only what API queries return. A: Action space Structured API calls (see below). Each action is JSON object specifying the target app, operation, and parameters. : Transition function Deterministic for most operations (write to document document updated), but stochastic for network-dependent actions (email delivery time, Teams availability). R: Reward function Multi-component (see Reward Design section). Ω: Observation space API responses, rendered document views, error messages. O: Observation function Maps state to observation (API response formatting, truncation for context window limits). γ = 0.99: Discount factor (long horizons, 1050 steps typical). 234 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Concrete Example: Summarize last weeks Project Alpha emails and create status slide Below we trace full episode through the POMDP, mapping each formal element to concrete realization. User request: Summarize the key decisions from last weeks Project Alpha emails and add status update slide to the team deck. Symbol Concrete Realization s0 a1 (s0, a1) o1 a2 o2 a3 a4 o4 T (s4, a5) o5 R(s5) True state: 47 emails in inbox mentioning Project Alpha from last 7 days; PowerPoint file Q3_Status.pptx on SharePoint with 12 slides; user has edit permissions. Agent observes: User request text + system prompt. Agent has no knowledge of email count or deck contents yet. outlook.search({query: "Project Alpha", last_7_days: true}) s1: System retrieves 47 matching emails (deterministic). API returns top 10 email subjects + senders + timestamps (truncated due to context limits in action). outlook.read({ids: [top_5_thread_ids]}) Agent picks the most relevant threads. Full body text of 5 email threads (8K tokens after truncation). Internal reasoning action: Agent synthesizes key decisions: (1) deadline moved to Nov 15, (2) budget approved, (3) vendor selected. sharepoint.read({file: "Q3_Status.pptx", slides: "last"}) Check current last slide. Last slide is Q2 Summary (slide 12). Agent decides to add slide 13. powerpoint.add_slide({file: "Q3_Status.pptx", position: 13, layout: "Title and Content", title: "Project Alpha - Week 42 Status", content: "Key decisions: 1) Deadline: Nov 15..."}) s5: Slide added to deck (deterministic). API returns {success: true, slide_id: 13}. Reward components: +0.4 task completion (slide created), +0.3 information quality (correct decisions extracted), +0.2 format compliance (proper layout used), +0.05 efficiency (5 actions, no errors), -0.0 safety penalty. Total: 0.95. Key POMDP aspects illustrated: Partial observability: At = 0, the agent doesnt know how many emails exist or what the deck contains it must query to discover the state. Observation function O: The API returns truncated results (top 10 of 47 emails) due to context window limits. The agent sees projection of the true state. Stochastic transitions: If the agent had tried teams.send_message() instead, delivery timing would be uncertain (recipient online/offline). Multi-step planning: The agent must chain 5 actions across 2 applications, maintaining coherence between the email summary and the slide content. Discount γ = 0.99: With 5 steps, discounting is minimal (0.995 = 0.95), but for 50-step tasks it matters encouraging efficient solutions.

sections.title: 12.6.3 Action Space Design
sections.content: The action space must be structured, type-safe, and composable: 235 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Productivity Co-pilot Action Schema { } " action_type " : " api_call " , " target_app " : " outlook excel word powerpoint teams sharepoint " , " operation " : " read write search create delete modify " , " parameters " : { " endpoint " : " / me / messages ? $filter = subject eq Project " , " body " : { ... } , " options " : { " top " : 10 } // For write operations // Pagination , filtering } , " reasoning " : " need to find relevant emails before summarizing " Action taxonomy by application: App Complexity Key Actions Outlook Medium Excel High Word Medium

sections.title: SharePoint Medium
sections.content: search, read, draft, send, move, flag, create_rule read_range, write_range, insert_formula, create_chart, pivot_table, run_macro read_paragraphs, format_section, insert_table add_slide, set_layout, add_image, apply_theme send_message, create_meeting, search_chat, add_members, post_to_channel list_files, create_page, set_permissions insert_text, find_replace, insert_shape, download, set_text, search, upload,

sections.title: 12.6.4 State Representation
sections.content: The agents observation (context window) at each step: ot = [system_prompt; user_intent; tool_history1:t1; current_resultt] (12.15) Context budget management (critical for 128K window): System prompt: 2K tokens (capabilities, safety rules, output format) User intent + conversation: 4K tokens Tool history (sliding window): Last 812 actions + observations, summarizing older ones. Total: 80K tokens max. Current observation: Up to 32K tokens (large spreadsheets, email threads) Reserve: 10K tokens for agents reasoning + next action generation State compression strategies: Selective inclusion: Only include API responses relevant to the current sub-goal (use an auxiliary relevance scorer). Structured summaries: Represent large spreadsheets as schema + sample rows rather than full data. Hierarchical memory: Store full trajectory externally; inject compressed summaries into context. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.6.5 Reward Design: Multi-Objective Signal
sections.content: The reward function for productivity co-pilot must balance multiple objectives: R(τ ) = α1Rtask + α2Rquality + α3Refficiency + α4Rsafety + α5Ruser (12.16) Table 12.3: Reward components for productivity co-pilot training. Component Weight Signal Type Definition Rtask Rquality Refficiency Rsafety 0. 0.25 0.15 0.15 Binary/scalar LLM judge Scalar Binary Ruser 0.05 Sparse Intermediate rewards (dense signal): Successful API call (200 response): +0. Task completed successfully (email sent, document created, formula correct) Output quality: formatting, clarity, correctness of content Penalty for excessive steps: 0.02 (num_steps optimal_steps) No unsafe actions (delete without confirmation, send to wrong recipient, permission violations). Rsafety = 0 if any violation. Explicit user feedback (thumbs up/- down) when available Correct information retrieval (verified by downstream use): +0.10 Recovers from error gracefully (retries with corrected params): +0.08 API error (4xx/5xx): 0.03 Repeated identical action (loop detection): 0.10 Asks clarifying question when intent is genuinely ambiguous: +0.

sections.title: 12.6.6 Training Pipeline: End-to-End
sections.content: Productivity Co-pilot RL Training Pipeline Phase 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning (Foundation) 1. Collect 50K200K human-demonstrated trajectories of productivity tasks (via telemetry, annotators, or synthetic generation). 2. SFT the base LLM on (instruction, trajectory) pairs with ReAct format. 3. Validate: agent should achieve 4060% task completion on held-out tasks. Phase 2: Simulated Environment Construction 1. Build sandbox environment with mocked API endpoints, synthetic mailboxes, documents, and calendars. 2. Each user has realistic profile: 500+ emails, 20+ documents, calendar events, Teams channels. 3. Task generator: produces diverse instructionverification pairs: Move all emails from Alice about Q4 budget to the Finance folder + verification function. 237 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Phase 3: Online RL Training (GRPO) 1. Sample task batch (256 tasks per iteration). 2. Generate = 8 trajectories per task using πθ in sandbox environment. 3. Execute trajectories, collect rewards from verification functions. 4. Compute GRPO advantages (group normalization across 8 trajectories per task). 5. Update policy with clipped objective + KL penalty vs. SFT model. 6. Every 500 iterations: evaluate on held-out benchmark (200 tasks, 5 difficulty levels). Phase 4: Human-in-the-Loop Refinement 1. Deploy to internal dogfood users (1000+ users, 2 weeks). 2. Collect thumbs up/down signals + free-text corrections. 3. Construct DPO preference pairs from A/B deployments (old policy vs. new). 4. Apply 12 rounds of DPO fine-tuning on human preferences.

sections.title: 12.6.7 Simulation Environment Architecture
sections.content: Sandbox Environment (Simplified) class ro t iv it En on en : def __init__ ( self , user_profile : UserProfile ) : self . mailbox = SyntheticMailbox ( user_profile . emails ) self . drive = Sy ntheticOneDrive ( user_profile . files ) self . calendar = Sy ntheticCalendar ( user_profile . events ) self . teams = SyntheticTeams ( user_profile . channels ) self . step_count = 0 self . max_steps = 50 def step ( self , action : dict ) -> Tuple [ Observation , float , bool ]: " " " Execute action , return ( observation , reward , done ) . " " " self . step_count += 1 # Route to appropriate app handler handler = self . get_handler ( action [ " target_app " ]) try : result = handler . execute ( action [ " operation " ] , action [ " parameters " ]) obs = Observation ( status =200 , body = result ) reward = 0.05 except APIError as : # Successful API call obs = Observation ( status = . code , body = str ( ) ) reward = -0.03 # Check terminal condition done = self . step_count >= self . max_steps return obs , reward , done def evaluate ( self , task : Task ) -> float : " " " Check if task objective is achieved ( terminal reward ) . " " " return task . verification_fn ( self ) # 0.0 or 1.

sections.title: 12.6.8 Task Curriculum Design
sections.content: Training effectiveness depends critically on task difficulty progression: 238 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 12.4: Productivity co-pilot curriculum levels. Level Steps Apps Example Tasks L1: Single-step L2: Single-app L3: Multi-step 12 35 5 1 1 1 L4: Cross-app 515 2 L5: Complex workflow 1030 3+ Read my latest email from Bob, Whats in cell A1? Draft reply to the budget email summarizing key points Create pivot table from sales data and format top performers in bold Find Q4 budget emails, extract the numbers, put them in new Excel sheet Prepare weekly report: pull metrics from Excel, summarize email updates, create PowerPoint slides, share in Teams Curriculum strategy: Start with 80% L1L2 tasks, 20% L3 in early training. Advance to next level when success rate exceeds 70% on current level. Always maintain 1020% of easier tasks to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Final mix (after convergence): 10% L1, 15% L2, 25% L3, 30% L4, 20% L5.

sections.title: 12.6.9 Safety and Guardrails
sections.content: Safety Framework for Productivity Co-pilot Hard constraints (action rejected immediately, reward = 1.0): Send email/message to external recipients without user confirmation Delete files/emails permanently (only soft-delete allowed) Modify permissions on shared resources Access other users mailboxes or files beyond granted permissions Execute actions on more than 100 items in batch (prevents mass-delete/move accidents) Soft constraints (penalty in reward, agent should learn to avoid): Sending draft without showing preview to user: 0.2 Making irreversible changes without stating intent first: 0.15 Accessing sensitive labels (confidential, attorney-client): 0.3 Using send on behalf without explicit delegation: 0. Confirmation protocol: For any action classified as high-impact (send, delete, share externally), the agent must: 1. State the intended action in natural language 2. Show preview of what will be sent/modified 3. Wait for explicit user confirmation before executing This is enforced both at the environment level (sandbox rejects unconfirmed high-impact actions) and in the reward function (penalty for skipping confirmation). H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.6.10 Credit Assignment in Multi-App Workflows
sections.content: The key challenge: in 20-step cross-app workflow, which steps contributed to success or failure? Approach: Hierarchical Reward Decomposition 1. Sub-goal detection: Decompose the users instruction into verifiable sub-goals: Find Q4 budget emails Sub-goal 1 (verified: relevant emails retrieved) Extract numbers Sub-goal 2 (verified: correct values parsed) Create Excel sheet Sub-goal 3 (verified: sheet exists with correct data) 2. Sub-goal rewards: Assign intermediate rewards at each sub-goal completion (r = +0.2 each). 3. Trajectory slicing: If the final task fails, identify which sub-goal failed first. Apply negative reward only to the actions within that sub-goals span. 4. Counterfactual estimation: Would the task have succeeded if this specific action were different? use the value function to estimate. Rstep(t) = Rsub-goal(t) } {z did current sub-goal succeed? + γT tRterminal } {z discounted final reward + rintermediate(t) } {z per-step API success/failure (12.17)

sections.title: 12.6.11 Scaling and Infrastructure
sections.content: Compute requirements (estimated for 70B parameter model):

sections.title: Notes
sections.content: Policy model (70B) Reference model (70B) Environment workers Reward model / Judge Training (GRPO updates) Total 8 A100 80GB (TP=8) 8 A100 80GB (TP=8) 128 CPU workers 4 A100 (if LLM judge) 16 A100 (FSDP) 40 A100 GPUs + 128 CPUs Throughput optimization: BF16, generates trajectories Frozen, for KL computation Each runs sandbox instance Or zero if using execution-based rewards Gradient accumulation over trajectory batches 5,000 GPU-hours for full training run Async rollouts: Decouple trajectory generation from gradient updates. Generate continuously while training on previous batch. Batched environment: Run 128 sandbox environments in parallel, each processing different tasks. KV-cache sharing: For the = 8 trajectories per task, they share the same prompt prefix use prefix caching to avoid redundant computation. Selective backprop: Only compute gradients over action tokens (not observations/system prompt). Reduces backward pass FLOPS by 4060%.

sections.title: 12.6.12 Evaluation Framework
sections.content: Benchmark suite (proposed): ProdBench-Easy (200 tasks): Single-app, 13 steps. Baseline establishment. ProdBench-Hard (200 tasks): Cross-app workflows, 1030 steps. End-to-end capability. 240 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 12.5: Productivity co-pilot evaluation dimensions. Metric Target Measurement Task completion rate Safety violation rate > 85% (L1L3), > 60% (L4 L5) < 0.1% Average steps to completion Within 1.5 optimal User satisfaction (dogfood) Cross-app success Recovery rate > 4.2/5.0 > 55% (L4L5) > 70% Latency (time to first action) < 3 seconds Automated verification in sandbox Count of hard constraint violations per 1000 tasks Compare to shortest known successful trajectory Post-task survey from internal users Tasks requiring 2+ applications % of failed API calls where agent retries successfully Model inference + action planning time ProdBench-Safety (100 tasks): Adversarial prompts attempting to trigger unsafe actions. Must maintain < 0.1% violation rate. ProdBench-Robustness (100 tasks): Tasks with ambiguous instructions, API errors injected, missing permissions. Tests graceful degradation.

sections.title: Practical Insights for Productivity Agentic RL
sections.content: 1. SFT quality is the floor: RL can only improve upon what SFT provides. If the SFT model cant format valid Graph API call, RL wont discover it. Invest heavily in Phase 1 data quality. 2. Reward hacking is inevitable: The agent will find shortcuts. Common examples: Creating an empty Excel file to complete spreadsheet task (passes existence check) Replying Done without actually performing the action Exploiting ambiguous verification functions Mitigation: Multi-level verification (format + content + semantic correctness). 3. API rate limits matter: In production, workspace APIs have throttling (429 responses). Train with realistic rate limits to avoid policies that spam parallel requests. 4. Context window is the bottleneck: 20-step trajectory with rich API responses easily consumes 80K+ tokens. Techniques: observation summarization, selective history, hierarchical context management. 5. User intent is often ambiguous: Clean up my inbox means different things to different users. Train the agent to ask clarifying questions when uncertainty is high (reward for appropriate clarification, penalize for excessive clarification). 6. Start simple, scale gradually: Begin with Outlook-only tasks (highest volume, most telemetry data), then expand to Excel, then cross-app. Each app has unique failure modes.

sections.title: 12.6.14 Complete Training Recipe
sections.content: 241 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 12.6: Recommended hyperparameters for productivity co-pilot RL training. Parameter Base model Value Rationale 70B Llama/Mistral RL algorithm GRPO Group size Clip ϵ 8 0.1 KL coefficient β Learning rate 0.04 5 107 256 tasks 8 trajs = 2048 Batch size Max trajectory length 50 steps Context window Training iterations 128K tokens 30005000 Curriculum warmup 500 iterations (L1L2 only) Sufficient capacity for complex multi-step reasoning No critic needed; memory-efficient for long trajectories Balance between variance reduction and compute cost Tighter than standard (0.2) due to long trajectory sensitivity Moderate constraint to SFT policy Conservative; agentic tasks are sensitive to large updates Large batch for stable GRPO normalization Covers 95% of productivity tasks Required for long multi-app workflows Monitor eval metrics; early-stop on safety degradation Establish basic API usage before complex tasks

sections.title: 12.7 Use Case: Building a Research Agent from Scratch
sections.content: This use case demonstrates how to build fully autonomous research agent an LLM that can formulate hypotheses, search literature, analyze data, write code, run experiments, and produce final report using the techniques discussed throughout this paper.

sections.title: Research Agent Requirements
sections.content: Input: research question (e.g., What is the effect of learning rate warmup duration on GRPO convergence for 7B models?) Output: complete research report with methodology, experiments, results, and conclusions. Capabilities required: 1. Literature search: Query arXiv, Semantic Scholar, find relevant papers 2. Hypothesis generation: Formulate testable hypotheses from background knowledge 3. Experiment design: Write training scripts with proper controls 4. Code execution: Run experiments, collect metrics 5. Data analysis: Parse logs, compute statistics, generate plots 6. Scientific writing: Synthesize findings into coherent report 7. Self-correction: Detect failed experiments and retry with modified parameters

sections.title: Research Agent MDP
sections.content: State st: System prompt + research question + full history of actions/observations (tool outputs, code results, search results). Context window: 128K tokens. Action at: Structured tool call from the action space (see below) + reasoning trace (CoT). 242 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Transition (st+1st, at): Deterministic append action + tool output to context. Reward R: Sparse terminal reward based on report quality (see reward design below). Horizon: 20100 steps (typical research trajectory). Discount γ = 1.0 (episodic; no discounting for finite tasks).

sections.title: 12.7.3 Action Space

sections.title:
Reasoning
Terminal
sections.content: Query Semantic Scholar/arXiv. Returns titles, abstracts, citations. Fetch full text or specific sections of paper. Write Python/training scripts to workspace. Run scripts in sandboxed environment. Returns stdout/stderr. Read logs, CSVs, or intermediate results. Generate matplotlib/seaborn visualizations. Run statistical tests (t-test, confidence intervals). Write sections of the final research report (LaTeX/Markdown). Internal reasoning step (no external tool call). Submit final report. Ends the episode.

sections.title: 12.7.4 Architecture: Model and Infrastructure Choices
sections.content: Architecture Decisions Applying Paper Concepts Base model: Qwen-2.5 72B (strong reasoning + code). QLoRA fine-tuning (r = 32, all linear layers) see Section on LoRA. Inference: vLLM with TP=4, prefix caching enabled (system prompt shared across rollouts) see vLLM section. Training: GRPO with = 4 trajectories per research question no value model needed (see GRPO section). Hardware: 8H100 node. QLoRA adapters fit in 48 GB; vLLM generation uses remaining capacity. Context management: 128K context with Flash Attention (see Flash Attention section). Sliding window summarization for trajectories exceeding context. Speculative decoding: Eagle heads for fast generation during long research trajectories (see Speculative Decoding section).

sections.title: 12.7.5 Reward Design
sections.content: Multi-Component Research Reward The terminal reward is computed when the agent calls submit: = w1Rquality + w2Rcorrectness + w3Rnovelty + w4Refficiency + w5Rformat 243 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Component Weight How Measured Rquality Rcorrectness Rnovelty Refficiency Rformat 0.30 0.30 0.15 0.15 0.10 LLM-as-judge (GPT-4 rates report 110 on clarity, depth, rigor) Code executes without errors + results are reproducible LLM-judge: does the report provide insight beyond summarizing papers? Bonus for fewer steps: Reff = max(0, 1 steps/100) Report has all required sections (intro, method, results, conclusion) Intermediate shaping: +0.1 for each successful code execution; 0.05 for each runtime error (encourages writing correct code first). Reward Hacking Risks Fake results: Agent fabricates experiment outputs. Fix: Verify code actually ran by checking execution logs against reported numbers. Shallow reports: Agent copies paper abstracts verbatim. Fix: Novelty reward + plagiarism detection. Length gaming: Long reports score higher. Fix: Efficiency reward + length penalty. Easy questions: Agent avoids hard research questions. Fix: Curriculum with difficulty levels.

sections.title: 12.7.6 Training Pipeline
sections.content: 1. Phase 1 SFT Warmup (500 steps): Collect 200 expert research trajectories (human researchers using the tools) SFT on successful trajectories with completion-only masking (mask tool outputs) This teaches the agent tool-use syntax and basic research workflow 2. Phase 2 GRPO Training (3000 steps): Prompt pool: 500 research questions across 10 domains (ML, NLP, CV, systems, etc.) Per question: generate = 4 complete research trajectories Score each trajectory with multi-component reward GRPO advantage: ˆAi = (Ri µG)/σG Update policy with clipped objective (clip ϵ = 0.2, KL β = 0.05) Curriculum: start with simple summarize findings on tasks, progress to design and run experiment on 3. Phase 3 Rejection Sampling Fine-Tuning (200 steps): Generate 16 trajectories per hard question, keep top-2 by reward SFT on these high-quality trajectories Stabilizes performance on the hardest research tasks

sections.title: Research Agent Evaluation Framework
sections.content: 72B model; full fine-tune too expensive. = 32 for complex reasoning. No value model needed; research quality is hard to predict mid-trajectory. Research quality only measurable at completion; intermediate shaping minimal. Balance: enough diversity for ranking, not too expensive (100-step trajectories). Long trajectories with paper contents + code + results. System prompt + research question shared across 4 rollouts. Start simple (literature review) hard (design + execute experiments). Research quality is subjective; LLM judge is more flexible than rule-based. Held-out questions (50): Research questions unseen during training, covering diverse domains. Human evaluation: Domain experts rate reports on 15 scale (quality, correctness, actionability). Reproducibility: Re-run the agents code from the report; verify results match. Comparison baselines: (1) Zero-shot GPT-4 + tools (no RL training), (2) SFT-only 246 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems agent, (3) Human researchers. Efficiency metric: Steps-to-completion normalized by task difficulty. Expected results (based on similar agentic RL work): Agent Report Quality (15) Avg Steps Zero-shot GPT-4 + tools SFT-only GRPO-trained (ours) Human researcher 2.8 3.4 4.1 4.5 25 18 14 N/A

sections.title: 12.7.10 Lessons and Failure Modes
sections.content: Common Failures in Research Agent Training Infinite loops: Agent repeatedly searches for papers without progressing. Fix: Step budget + penalty for repeated tool calls with same arguments. Code debugging spirals: Agent spends 20+ steps fixing single bug. Fix: Cap retries at 3; if code fails 3 times, abandon approach and try alternative. Hallucinated citations: Agent invents paper titles/results. Fix: Verify all citations exist via tool output; penalize unverifiable claims. Premature submission: Agent submits incomplete reports to avoid penalty for long trajectories. Fix: Minimum quality threshold (R > 0.4) to count as valid submission; below threshold is treated as failure. Reward hacking the judge: Agent learns to produce text that scores high with the LLM judge but is scientifically shallow. Fix: Rotate judge models; include human eval in the reward periodically.

sections.title: 12.8 State-of-the-Art RL for LLM Agents
sections.content: RL techniques for LLM agents focus on on-policy policy gradients combined with fine-grained credit assignment. Because agents execute complex multi-turn trajectories involving tool interactions, API queries, and code execution, standard single-turn alignment algorithms must be heavily modified.

sections.title: 12.8.1 Dominant Baseline: GRPO for Agents
sections.content: Popularized by DeepSeek-R1 [15], GRPO [14] is rapidly becoming the standard for agentic training. It samples group of complete trajectories per task, eliminating the memory-intensive critic network: For task prompt q, GRPO samples agentic trajectories {o1, o2, . . . , oN } from πθold. The advantage for each trajectory is computed by normalizing its reward relative to the group: Ai = PN r(oi) 1 j=1 r(oj) std(r(o1), . . . , r(oN )) (12.18) The GRPO objective with KL regularization: LGRPO(θ) =

sections.title:
1
N
sections.content: N i=1 min πθ(oiq) πθold(oiq) Ai, clip πθ(oiq) πθold(oiq) ! ! , 1ϵ, 1+ϵ Ai β DKL(πθπref) (12.19) 247 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Why GRPO Dominates Agentic Training No critic: Saves 50% GPU memory critical when agent trajectories already consume massive context windows (32K128K tokens). Natural fit: Agent tasks often have binary verifiable rewards (tests pass/fail, goal achieved/not) perfect for group-relative normalization. Exploration: Sampling diverse trajectories per task naturally explores different tool-use strategies.

sections.title: 12.8.2 PPO for Interactive Agents
sections.content: PPO [168] remains valuable for agents operating in highly stochastic environments where step-level value estimation helps. The critic provides per-step advantage signals, enabling finer credit assignment when tool outputs are unpredictable: Step-level advantage estimation via GAE handles variable-length tool outputs Value head learns to predict how likely is this trajectory to succeed from here More stable when external tools return catastrophic errors that spike reward variance Trade-off: requires 2 memory (critic) but provides denser learning signals

sections.title: 12.8.3 Fine-Grained Turn-Level Credit Assignment
sections.content: The core challenge in agentic RL is the sparse reward problem. If an agent executes 20 tool actions and finally fails unit test, terminal reward of 0 punishes all 20 actions equally. Modern solutions: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) Reward the model at deterministic intermediate checkpoints: Bash command compiles successfully +0.1 Browser agent targets correct HTML element +0.2 SQL query returns non-empty results +0. Final test suite passes +1.0 (terminal) Intermediate rewards provide gradient signal to every step, not just the final one. This dramatically accelerates learning by 35 compared to sparse-only rewards. Multi-Turn Trajectory Slicing Frameworks split multi-turn agent run into individual, independent steps. credit assignment module isolates the exact sub-step that broke the trajectory: 1. Replay the successful prefix (steps 1k) 2. Identify the first divergence point (step + 1 where it went wrong) 3. Assign negative reward only to that specific step 4. Assign neutral/positive rewards to correct prefix steps This enables surgical policy updates without degrading already-correct behavior. 248 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 12.8.4 Alternative Paradigms
sections.content: Iterative STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner) [223]: Rather than continuous RL, use iterative offline loops. Generate trajectories filter failures SFT on successes repeat. Simple to scale, avoids RL instability. Each iteration bootstraps reasoning ability. Reinforcement World Model Learning (RWML) [233]: To combat reward hacking, train agents to predict the semantic consequence of their actions. The agent receives an auxiliary reward for accurately predicting how environment state will change (e.g., predicting database table changes before executing SQL). This forces genuine understanding over superficial rewardgaming. LATS (Language Agent Tree Search) [225]: Apply Monte Carlo Tree Search over agent action sequences. At each step, expand multiple candidate actions, simulate their outcomes, and backpropagate rewards through the tree. Combines RL value estimation with search-time compute scaling.

sections.title: 12.8.5 Core Methodology Comparison
sections.content: Table 12.9: Comparison of RL paradigms for LLM agents.
