sections.title: Reasoning
sections.content: 250 Chapter

sections.title: RL for Large Reasoning Models
sections.content: The emergence of large reasoning models represents one of the most significant developments in modern AI. Unlike standard language model training, which optimizes for next-token prediction, reasoning-focused RL teaches models to think before answeringallocating additional computation at inference time to explore, verify, and refine intermediate steps. This section provides comprehensive technical treatment of the methods, architectures, and scaling laws that underpin this paradigm.

sections.title: 13.1.1 Why Reasoning Requires Different RL Approaches
sections.content: Standard RLHF (Section 4.3) optimizes single scalar reward over complete response. For tasks requiring multi-step reasoningmathematics, formal verification, competitive programming, scientific derivationthis formulation is insufficient for several reasons: Sparse rewards: math problem may require 20 intermediate steps; single outcome reward provides no gradient signal for the intermediate steps that led to an error. Long horizons: Reasoning chains can span hundreds to thousands of tokens, creating severe credit assignment problems. Combinatorial search: The space of valid reasoning paths is exponentially large; the model must learn to search this space efficiently. Verifiability: Unlike subjective text quality, mathematical and logical correctness is objectively verifiable, enabling automated reward computation without human annotation. Key Insight: Reasoning as Search Problem Multi-step reasoning can be framed as search problem over tree of partial solutions. Each node in the tree is reasoning state (prefix of the chain-of-thought), each edge is reasoning step (a token or sentence), and the leaves are final answers. RL for reasoning teaches the model to navigate this tree efficientlyexploring promising branches, backtracking from dead ends, and allocating compute where it matters most.

sections.title: 13.1.2 Chain-of-Thought: Emergent Behavior vs. Trained Capability
sections.content: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning was first observed as an emergent capability in sufficiently large language models [122]: when prompted with step-by-step examples, large models (typically 100B parameters) spontaneously produced intermediate reasoning steps that improved accuracy. This raised fundamental question: is CoT an emergent property of scale, or can it be explicitly trained? The answer, as demonstrated by DeepSeek-R1 and related work, is bothbut with important nuances: 251 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Emergent CoT arises from in-context learning and requires large base models. It is brittle, prompt-sensitive, and does not generalize robustly. Trained CoT via RL produces models that intrinsically generate reasoning chains as part of their generation process, independent of prompting style. These chains are longer, more exploratory, and exhibit qualitatively different behaviors (self-correction, backtracking, verification). The Aha Moment Phenomenon (DeepSeek-AI et al. 2025) During RL training of reasoning models, researchers at DeepSeek observed striking emergent behavior: at certain point in training, models spontaneously began to reconsider their initial approaches mid-chain, using phrases like Wait, let me reconsider. . . or Actually, think made an error. . . . This self-correction behaviorwhich was not explicitly trainedemerged purely from the RL objective of maximizing final-answer accuracy. It suggests that RL can discover meta-cognitive strategies that are instrumentally useful for solving hard problems.

sections.title: 13.1.3 Test-Time Compute Scaling Laws
sections.content: A central empirical finding motivating reasoning model research is that test-time compute scales predictably with performance. Let Ctrain denote training compute (FLOPs) and Ctest denote inference compute (tokens generated). The key observation is: Accuracy(Ctrain, Ctest) (α log Ctrain + β log Ctest) (13.1) for some monotone function and constants α, β > 0. This implies that smaller model with more inference compute can match larger model with less inference computea fundamental shift in the compute-performance tradeoff. Figure 13.1: Schematic test-time compute scaling curves. Performance improves log-linearly with inference tokens across model sizes, and smaller models with more compute can approach larger models with less compute. The practical implication is profound: reasoning models trade training compute for inference compute. Rather than always deploying the largest possible model, one can deploy smaller, reasoning-capable model and allocate more tokens to thinking on hard problems. 252 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 13.2 Test-Time Scaling Methods
sections.content: The scaling laws above show that investing more compute at inference can dramatically improve reasoning performance. This section provides comprehensive treatment of the methods that operationalize test-time scaling from simple chain-of-thought to sophisticated tree and graph search algorithms. These methods form spectrum trading inference cost for accuracy, and understanding their structure is essential for designing modern reasoning systems. Figure 13.2: Spectrum of test-time scaling methods. Each method trades additional inference compute for improved reasoning accuracy. Methods build on each other conceptually: CoT introduces explicit reasoning, Self-Consistency adds sampling, ToT adds structured search, GoT adds merging operations, and MCTS adds learned value guidance.

sections.title: 13.2.1 Chain-of-Thought (CoT)
sections.content: Chain-of-Thought prompting [122] is the foundation of all test-time scaling methods. Rather than directly outputting an answer, the model generates intermediate reasoning steps that decompose complex problems into manageable sub-problems. Zero-Shot CoT. Kojima et al. [123] demonstrated that appending Lets think step by step to prompt elicits reasoning behavior without any exemplars. This simple trigger activates latent reasoning capabilities in sufficiently large models (100B parameters). Few-Shot CoT. Wei et al. [122] showed that providing few exemplars with explicit reasoning traces enables smaller models to reason effectively: Prompt = [(x1, z1, y1), (x2, z2, y2), . . . , (xk, zk, yk), (xtest, ?)] (13.2) where zi are hand-written reasoning traces for exemplar (xi, yi). Formal characterization. CoT converts single-step prediction p(yx) into multi-step sequential generation: p(yx, z) p(zx) p(yx, z) p(zx) (13.3) p(yx) = where = (z1, z2, . . . , zT ) is the greedy reasoning chain. The summation over all possible chains is intractable; standard CoT uses single sample (greedy or temperature sampling). Limitations. Single-chain CoT is fragile: if an early reasoning step is wrong, all subsequent steps build on flawed foundation with no mechanism for recovery.

sections.title: 13.2.2 Self-Consistency (Majority Voting)
sections.content: Self-Consistency [124] addresses CoTs single-chain fragility by sampling multiple independent reasoning chains and taking majority vote over the final answers: ˆy = arg max i=1 Key properties: 1[yi = y], where (zi, yi) p(x), > 0 (13.4) 253 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Uses temperature > 0 sampling to generate diverse chains (typically = 0.71.0) No interaction between chains fully parallelizable Accuracy improves monotonically with (diminishing returns after 40) On GSM8K: CoT = 56.5%, Self-Consistency (N =40) = 74.4% (with PaLM-540B [221]) Equivalent to Best-of-N with outcome reward (majority vote acts as implicit ORM) Why Majority Voting Works If the model has probability > 0.5 of generating correct reasoning chain, then by the law of large numbers, majority voting over independent samples approaches 100% accuracy as . Even with = 0.3 (model is usually wrong), if correct answers concentrate on one value while incorrect answers are diverse, majority voting still recovers the correct answer. This is the statistical foundation of test-time scaling.

sections.title: 13.2.3 Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT)
sections.content: Tree-of-Thoughts [234] generalizes CoT from linear chain to tree structure, enabling the model to explore multiple reasoning paths, evaluate intermediate states, and backtrack from unpromising branches. This introduces deliberate planning into the reasoning process. Core Abstraction. reasoning problem is decomposed into search over tree where: Root: Initial problem statement Nodes: Partial reasoning states = (x, z1, . . . , zk) Edges: Individual reasoning steps (thoughts) zk+1 Leaves: Complete solutions with final answers Value function: (s) estimates how promising partial solution is Formal Definition. where: ToT = (G, E, V, πθ, Search) (13.5) G: Thought generator produces candidate next thoughts: {z(1), . . . , z(b)} πθ(s) E: State evaluator scores partial solutions: (s) {sure, maybe, impossible} or (s) [0, 1] πθ: The language model generating thoughts Search: Search algorithm (BFS or DFS) Search Algorithms. BFS (Breadth-First Search): 1. Generate candidate thoughts for each node at current depth 2. Evaluate all candidates with () 3. Keep top-k most promising states (beam search) 4. Advance all states to the next level 254 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Figure 13.3: Tree-of-Thoughts on the Game of 24 task: use operations on 4, 9, 10, 13 to make 24. At each level, the model generates = 3 candidate thoughts, evaluates each (sure/maybe/impossible), prunes unpromising branches, and expands the most promising ones. The green path leads to solution; red paths are pruned early. 5. Repeat until solution is found or depth limit reached DFS (Depth-First Search): 1. Generate candidate thoughts for current state 2. Evaluate: if (s) = impossible, backtrack immediately 3. If (s) = sure/maybe, recurse deeper (pick the most promising) 4. If depth limit reached without solution, backtrack 5. Continue until solution found or all branches explored ToT: Value Evaluation Prompt # The LLM evaluates partial reasoning states : EVAL_PROMPT = " " " Evaluate if this partial solution can reach 24. Numbers remaining : [4 , 4 , 10] Steps so far : 13 - 9 = 4 Can these remaining numbers (4 , 4 , 10) be combined using + , - ,* ,/ to make 24? Analysis : 4 * (10 - 4) = 4 * 6 = 24. Yes ! Judge : sure / maybe / impossible Answer : sure " " " # Thought generation prompt : GEN_PROMPT = " " " Input : 4 9 10 13 Possible next steps : 1. 13 - 9 = 4 ( left : 4 4 10) 2. 10 + 13 = 23 ( left : 4 9 23) 3. 9 - 4 = 5 ( left : 5 10 13) ... " " " Computational Cost. For ToT with branching factor b, depth d, and beam width k: LLM calls (BFS) = {z} generation + {z} evaluation = 2kb per level = Total = 2kbd (13.6) For the 24 game: = 3, = 2, = 3 = 36 LLM calls vs. 1 for standard CoT. 255 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Results. On the Game of 24 (a challenging arithmetic reasoning task), ToT achieves 74% success rate vs. CoTs 4% massive improvement from structured search over the same base model (GPT-4).

sections.title: 13.2.4 Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT)
sections.content: Graph-of-Thoughts [235] extends ToT from tree to directed acyclic graph (DAG), introducing critical capability: merging partial solutions from different branches. This allows the model to synthesize insights from multiple reasoning paths into single refined solution. Key Operations. GoT introduces three operations beyond ToT: Generate: Produce new thoughts from state (same as ToT) Aggregate/Merge: Combine multiple thoughts into one refined thought this is impossible in tree Refine: Iteratively improve thought based on feedback Score: Evaluate thought quality (same as ToTs value function) Figure 13.4: Comparison of CoT (linear chain), ToT (tree branches but no merging), and GoT (DAG branches can merge). For sorting task, GoT can split the array into sub-problems, solve them independently (parallel), then merge the results impossible in pure tree structure. This enables divide-and-conquer reasoning. Graph Operations (formal). Let = {v1, . . . , vn} be thought vertices and be directed edges. GoT supports: Generate(v) : {vc1, . . . , vcb} (create children) Aggregate(v1, . . . , vk) vmerged (merge thoughts into one) Refine(v, n) (improve through iterations) Score(v) [0, 1] (evaluate thought quality) (13.7) (13.8) (13.9) (13.10) The Aggregate operation is the key differentiator: it creates edges from multiple parent nodes to single child, forming DAG rather than tree. This enables: Divide-and-conquer: Split problem solve sub-problems in parallel merge solutions Ensemble reasoning: Generate multiple perspectives, then synthesize the best ideas Iterative refinement: Feed evaluation results back to improve earlier thoughts 256 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Results. On sorting (a task requiring merging), GoT achieves 62% cost reduction vs. ToT at equivalent quality. On set intersection and keyword counting, GoT matches ToT quality with 3040% fewer LLM calls due to the merge operation enabling more efficient decomposition.

sections.title: 13.2.5 Best-of-N with Reward Models
sections.content: Best-of-N (BoN) [196, 236] is the simplest scaling method that uses learned reward model to select among candidates: = arg max y{y1,...,yN } Rϕ(x, y), yi πθ(x) (13.11) Variants by reward model type: BoN with ORM: Score complete solutions; select the highest-scoring one. Equivalent to Self-Consistency when ORM correctness check. BoN with PRM: Score at each reasoning step; select the solution with highest minimum step score (least likely to have an error at any step). Weighted BoN: Weight candidates by reward: softmax(R(y1)/τ, . . . , R(yN )/τ ).

sections.title: BoN Scaling Law
sections.content: For model with per-sample accuracy p, the probability of at least one correct sample in tries: (success with BoN) = 1 (1 p)N (13.12) With perfect reward model (oracle that always selects correctly): = 0.3, = 10: success = 97% = 0.1, = 50: success = 99.5% In practice, imperfect reward models cap the effective beyond 64256, reward model errors dominate and accuracy plateaus or decreases (reward hacking).

sections.title: 13.2.6 Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for Reasoning
sections.content: MCTS [19, 237] combines the structured exploration of ToT with learned value estimates and visit-count statistics to allocate inference compute optimally. Originally developed for game-playing (AlphaGo [19]), MCTS has been adapted for LLM reasoning by systems including AlphaProof [238] and rStar [239]. Algorithm (adapted for LLM reasoning). Each MCTS iteration consists of four phases: UCB for Reasoning. Node selection uses PUCT (Predictor + UCB applied to Trees): " = arg max Q(s, a) + cpuct (s, a) # pP (s, b) 1 + (s, a) (13.13) where (s, a) = πθ(as) is the LLMs prior probability of generating step from state s. This biases exploration toward steps the LLM already considers likely, while the UCB term encourages trying under-explored alternatives. MCTS for Math Reasoning: Running Example Problem: Prove that Iteration 1 (Selection root, Expansion): 2 is irrational. 257 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Figure 13.5: Four phases of MCTS for reasoning: (1) Selection: traverse tree using UCB to find promising leaf; (2) Expansion: generate new reasoning steps from the leaf; (3) Simulation: complete the reasoning to terminal state and evaluate; (4) Backpropagation: update value estimates along the path. Generate 3 candidate first steps: 1. Assume for contradiction that 2. Consider the decimal expansion of 2 = 1.414... (P = 0.15) 2 = p/q in lowest terms. (P = 0.7) 3. Use the fundamental theorem of arithmetic. (P = 0.10) Rollout from z1: reaches correct proof in 4 steps = 1.0 Rollout from z2: fails (decimal doesnt prove irrationality) = 0.0 Backprop: Q(s0, z1) = 1.0, (s0, z1) = Iteration 2 (Selection: pick z1 by UCB): Expand from state Assume 2 = p/q...: 1. Then 2 = p2/q2, so p2 = 2q2. (P = 0.8) 2. Then and share no common factors. (P = 0.15) Rollout from z4: correct continuation = 1.0 Backprop: Q(s0, z1) = 1.0, Q(s1, z4) = 1.0 After 20 iterations: The tree has explored 8 distinct reasoning paths. The most-visited path is selected as the final proof: z1 z4 z6 z8 (classical proof by contradiction via even/odd argument). Comparison: ToT vs. MCTS.

sections.title: 13.2.7 Beam Search over Reasoning Steps
sections.content: Beam search long standard in NMT and text generation can be applied at the reasoning step level rather than the token level. Instead of tracking the top-k token sequences, we track the top-k reasoning prefixes: ( Bd = top-k (s1, . . . , sd) : i= log πθ(sis<i) + λ Vϕ(s1, . . . , sd) (13.14) ) where the scoring combines the LLM log-probability (fluency) with value model estimate (correctness). This is effectively ToT-BFS with learned value function rather than prompted one. 258 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 13.1: Tree-of-Thoughts vs. Monte Carlo Tree Search for reasoning. Dimension ToT MCTS Exploration Value estimation LLM prompt be/impossible) Fixed beam width; no revisiting Compute allocation Uniform across depth levels (sure/mayTraining integration No training; pure prompting Best for Simple branching problems (24 game) Learned value network + rollout statistics UCB adaptively allocates budget to promising nodes Focused: more simulations on harder subproblems Can distill MCTS policy into the base model [19] Complex problems requiring deep exploration (proofs, code) 13.2.8 Iterative Refinement and Self-Correction Rather than exploring breadth (multiple parallel chains), iterative refinement invests compute in depth repeatedly improving single solution: (cid:16) y(t+1) = LLM Improve this solution:, y(t), Errors found:, e(t)(cid:17) (13.15) where e(t) may come from: Self-verification: Ask the model to check its own answer External verification: Run code, check math symbolically Critic model: separate model identifies errors Notable methods: Self-Refine [240] (iterative self-feedback), Reflexion [224] (verbal RL via reflections stored in memory), and LATS [225] (tree search + reflection-based pruning).

sections.title: 13.2.9 Method Comparison and Selection Guide
sections.content: Table 13.2: Comprehensive comparison of test-time scaling methods. Method Structure LLM Calls Parallelizable Needs RM? Best For Chain CoT [122] Self-Consistency [124] Parallel chains Parallel chains Best-of-N + ORM Parallel chains Best-of-N + PRM Tree (BFS/DFS) O(kbd) ToT [234] O(kbd) DAG GoT [235] O(Nsim d) Tree + values MCTS [237] 2T Linear (iterative) Self-Refine [240] Tree + reflection O(N d) LATS [225] 1 + 1 + N/A Fully Fully Fully Partial Partial Partial No Partial Easymedium problems No No (majority vote) Math with discrete answers Yes (ORM) Yes (PRM) LLM-as-judge LLM-as-judge Yes (value net) Self-critic LLM-as-judge General tasks with good RM Complex multi-step reasoning Structured search problems Decomposable problems Hard proofs, coding Open-ended generation Agent tasks

sections.title: When to Use Which Method
sections.content: Budget < 5 base cost: Use CoT or Self-Consistency. Maximum bang for the buck. Budget 550: Use Best-of-N with PRM (if you have good reward model) or ToT-BFS with = 3, = 2. Budget 50500: Use MCTS with trained value function. This is the regime where DeepSeek-R1 and OpenAI o1 operate long reasoning chains with implicit tree search. Parallelism required: Self-Consistency and Best-of-N are fully parallel; ToT/MCTS require sequential depth expansion. No reward model available: Use Self-Consistency (majority vote) or ToT with LLM-asjudge evaluation. 259 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Decomposable problems: GoT excels when the problem has natural sub-problems (sorting, multi-document synthesis, code with modules). The Implicit Test-Time Scaling in Reasoning Models Modern reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1 [15], OpenAI o1/o3 [241, 242]) perform implicit testtime scaling via long chain-of-thought generation. Their thinking tokens serve function analogous to MCTS rollouts: the model explores multiple approaches, backtracks (Wait, let me reconsider...), verifies intermediate steps, and allocates more tokens to harder sub-problems. The key insight of R1/o1 training is that GRPO/RL teaches the model to perform this implicit search within single generation, eliminating the need for external orchestration (ToT prompts, MCTS infrastructure). The model becomes its own search algorithm.

sections.title: 13.3 DeepSeek-R1
sections.content: DeepSeek-R1 [15] is the first fully open-source large reasoning model to match or exceed OpenAI o1 on major benchmarks. Its training pipeline is technically transparent and has become the de facto reference implementation for RL-based reasoning.

sections.title: 13.3.1 Two-Stage Training Pipeline
sections.content: Stage 1: Cold-Start Supervised Fine-Tuning The base model (DeepSeek-V3) is first fine-tuned on small, carefully curated dataset of long chain-of-thought examples. This cold start phase serves two purposes: 1. Format initialization: The model learns to produce reasoning in the <think>...</think> format before emitting final answer. 2. Stability: Without cold-start SFT, pure RL from scratch on the base model produces unstable training dynamics and degenerate outputs (e.g., language mixing, repetitive loops). The cold-start dataset contains only thousands of examples, deliberately kept small to avoid over-constraining the reasoning style that RL will later discover. Stage 2: GRPO-Based Reinforcement Learning After cold-start SFT, the model undergoes large-scale RL using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). The full GRPO objective as used in R1 is described in Section 13.3.3. 260 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems R1 Training Pipeline Summary 1. Base model: DeepSeek-V3 (671B MoE, 37B active parameters) 2. Cold-start SFT: thousands of long-CoT examples, format: <think>...</think><answer>...</answer> 3. RL phase: GRPO with verifiable rewards on math + code problems 4. Rejection sampling: Generate multiple solutions, keep correct ones 5. SFT on RL outputs: Fine-tune on high-quality RL-generated chains 6. Final RL: Second RL phase for alignment + helpfulness

sections.title: 13.3.2 Reward Design: Accuracy and Format Rewards
sections.content: A key design choice in R1 is the absence of process reward model. Instead, R1 uses two simple, automatically computable rewards: Accuracy Reward For math problems with verifiable answers: racc(y, y) = (1 0 if verify(y, y) = True otherwise (13.16) where is the models final answer (extracted from <answer> tags) and is the ground-truth answer. The verify function uses symbolic math comparison (e.g., SymPy) to handle equivalent forms. For code problems, the accuracy reward is determined by passing test cases: rcode acc (y, ) = 1 tT 1[execute(y, t) = expected(t)] (13.17) Format Reward To enforce the <think>...</think> structure: rfmt(y) = (1 has valid <think> and <answer> tags 0 otherwise

sections.title: Combined Reward
sections.content: r(y, y) = racc(y, y) + λfmt rfmt(y) (13.18) (13.19) with λfmt = 0.1 in the original implementation (small enough not to dominate, large enough to prevent format collapse). No Process Reward Model notable and surprising finding of R1 is that no process reward model (PRM) is needed. Despite the long reasoning chains, outcome-only rewards are sufficient for RL to discover highquality reasoning strategies. The authors hypothesize that the verifiable nature of math/code rewards provides sufficient signal, and that PRMs introduce their own failure modes (reward hacking at the step level). This contrasts with the approach taken by OpenAI (Section 13.4).

sections.title: 13.3.3 GRPO Formulation for R1
sections.content: GRPO [14] is policy gradient method that avoids training separate value network by estimating advantages from group of sampled responses. For question q, GRPO samples responses {y1, y2, . . . , yG} from the current policy πθ and computes advantages relative to the group mean. 261 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Group Sampling and Advantage Normalization Given question q, sample outputs: Compute rewards {ri}G i=1 using the reward function from Eq. [eq:r1_combined_reward]. The normalized advantage for response is: {yi}G i=1 πθ( q) (13.20) ˆAi = ri µr σr + ϵ (13.21) where µr = 1 PG i=1 ri, σr = 1 PG i=1(ri µr)2, and ϵ = 108 for numerical stability. GRPO Objective The GRPO objective clips the probability ratio (as in PPO) and adds KL penalty against reference policy πref: LGRPO(θ) = EqD, {yi}πθ(q) " 1 X i=1 1 yi yi t=1 (cid:16) min ρi,t ˆAi, clip(ρi,t, 1ε, 1+ε) ˆAi (cid:17) β DKL[πθ πref] (13.22) # where: ρi,t = πθ(yi,t q, yi,<t) πθold(yi,t q, yi,<t) is the per-token probability ratio ε {0.1, 0.2} is the PPO clipping parameter β > 0 controls the KL penalty strength yi is the length of response (length normalization prevents bias toward short responses) KL Penalty Formulation The KL divergence term is computed token-by-token: DKL[πθ πref] = Eyπθ(q) t= log πθ(yt q, y<t) πref(yt q, y<t) (13.23) In practice, R1 uses an unbiased estimator of the KL that avoids computing πref at every step by using the approximation: DKL[πθ πref] πref(yt q, y<t) πθ(yt q, y<t) log πref(yt q, y<t) πθ(yt q, y<t) (13.24) which is always non-negative and equals zero when πθ = πref. GRPO in Practice: Group Size and Stability In R1s training, = 8 responses are sampled per question. This is critical hyperparameter: Too small (G = 2): High variance in advantage estimates; training is noisy. Too large (G = 32): Computational cost scales linearly; diminishing returns. = 8: Empirically found to balance variance reduction and compute cost. The group sampling also provides natural curriculum signal: as training progresses, the models average reward µr increases, and the variance σr decreases. Problems where all responses are correct (or all wrong) contribute zero gradient, naturally focusing learning on problems at the frontier of the models capability. 262 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 13.3.4 Distillation: The R1-Distill Series
sections.content: A major practical contribution of R1 is demonstrating that reasoning capabilities can be distilled into much smaller models via supervised fine-tuning on R1-generated chains. The R1-Distill series (1.5B, 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, 70B parameters) is trained by: 1. Generating long-CoT solutions to large problem set using R1 (671B) 2. Filtering to keep only correct solutions 3. Fine-tuning smaller base models (Qwen2.5, Llama-3) on these solutions Distillation vs. RL for Small Models striking finding: distillation outperforms RL training from scratch on small models. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B achieves higher MATH benchmark scores than 7B model trained with GRPO directly. This suggests that: Small models lack the capacity to discover reasoning strategies via RL exploration But they can learn to imitate reasoning strategies discovered by larger models The bottleneck for small models is exploration, not representation The distillation approach raises an important question about the nature of reasoning: is the small model truly reasoning, or is it pattern-matching on the surface form of reasoning chains? Empirically, distilled models show some generalization to novel problem types, suggesting genuine internalization of reasoning strategies rather than pure memorization.

sections.title: 13.4 OpenAI o1/o3 Series
sections.content: OpenAIs o1 [241] (released September 2024) and subsequent o3/o4-mini [242] models represent the commercial frontier of reasoning model development. While full technical details remain proprietary, the published system cards, technical reports, and empirical observations provide substantial insight into the methodology.

sections.title: 13.4.1 Chain-of-Thought RL with Hidden Reasoning Tokens
sections.content: The defining architectural choice of o1 is the use of hidden reasoning tokens: the model generates an internal chain-of-thought (called reasoning trace or thinking tokens) that is not shown to the user. Only the final answer is returned. This design has several implications: No format constraints: The hidden reasoning can use any format, including scratchpad notation, pseudocode, or even non-English reasoning. No reward hacking on style: Since users never see the reasoning, there is no pressure to make it look good rather than be useful. Proprietary protection: The reasoning process is not exposed, preventing direct imitation. The training procedure is described as training models to reason using RL, with the RL objective applied to the complete (hidden reasoning + final answer) sequence, rewarded only on the quality of the final answer.

sections.title: 13.4.2 Process Reward Models vs. Outcome Reward Models
sections.content: OpenAIs approach is believed to use Process Reward Models (PRMs) [243] in addition to outcome rewards, in contrast to DeepSeek-R1s outcome-only approach. This inference is based on OpenAIs published PRM research (PRM800K dataset, Lets Verify Step by Step) and the o1 system cards description of RL training on reasoning chains, though the exact o1/o3 training recipe has not been publicly disclosed. 263 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Outcome Reward Model (ORM) An ORM scores the complete response (q, y): For verifiable tasks (math, code), this reduces to exact-match verification. For open-ended tasks, learned reward model is used. RORM(q, y) [0, 1] (13.25) Process Reward Model (PRM) PRM assigns reward to each reasoning step sk in the chain = (s1, s2, . . . , sK): RPRM(q, y) = k=1 γKk rk(q, s1, . . . , sk) (13.26) where rk [0, 1] is the step-level reward and γ (0, 1] is discount factor. The step-level reward rk estimates the probability that the partial solution (s1, . . . , sk) leads to correct final answer: rk(q, s1, . . . , sk) = (correct final answer q, s1, . . . , sk) (13.27) PRM vs. ORM: The Credit Assignment Tradeoff ORM provides clean, unambiguous rewards but suffers from severe credit assignment problems: single wrong step early in 50-step chain receives the same zero reward as completely random response. PRM provides dense rewards that directly address credit assignment, but introduces new challenges: Training data: Step-level labels require human annotation or automated generation (MathShepherd, Section 13.6.2). Reward hacking: Models can learn to produce steps that look correct to the PRM without actually being correct. Distribution shift: PRMs trained on one distribution of reasoning chains may not generalize to the novel chains produced by RL. The empirical evidence suggests PRMs are beneficial for search (selecting among candidate solutions) but their benefit for training is less clear. 13.4.3 Inference-Time Compute Scaling The o1 technical report demonstrates clear scaling law: more thinking tokens monotonically improve performance on hard reasoning tasks. This is operationalized through thinking budget parameter that controls the maximum number of hidden reasoning tokens. Let be the thinking token budget. The empirical scaling law observed is approximately: Pass@1(T ) (13.28) for constants a, b, > 0, where represents the asymptotic accuracy ceiling and characterizes the rate of improvement. For AIME 2024, o1 with full thinking budget achieves 83% accuracy, compared to 13% for GPT-4o (which uses no extended thinking).

sections.title: 13.4.4 Training Compute vs. Test-Time Compute
sections.content: A fundamental insight from the o1/o3 series is the compute equivalence principle: there exists tradeoff curve between training compute Ctrain and test-time compute Ctest such that points on the curve achieve similar performance: Performance(Ctrain, Ctest) = g(αCp train + βCq test) (13.29) Empirically, for reasoning tasks, suggesting that training and test-time compute are roughly substitutable. This has profound implications for deployment: smaller, cheaper model with extended thinking can match larger model on hard problems, at the cost of higher latency. 264 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems 13.4.5 o3 and o4-mini Architecture Insights While o3 and o4-mini details remain largely proprietary, several observations have emerged: o3: Substantially larger thinking budgets than o1; achieves near-human performance on ARCAGI (87.5% with high compute). Believed to use more sophisticated search strategies during inference. o4-mini: Demonstrates that smaller models with RL-trained reasoning can be highly competitive. Achieves 93% on AIME 2025 with extended thinking, suggesting that model size is less important than reasoning capability for math. Tool use: o3/o4-mini integrate tool use (code execution, web search) into the reasoning process, allowing the model to verify intermediate steps programmatically.

sections.title: 13.5 QwQ and Qwen Reasoning Models
sections.content: Alibabas Qwen team has developed series of reasoning models (QwQ-32B [244], Qwen3 [245]) that represent the open-source frontier alongside DeepSeek-R1. Their approach differs in several key respects.

sections.title: 13.5.1 Multi-Stage RL Pipeline
sections.content: The Qwen reasoning pipeline uses more elaborate multi-stage approach: 1. Base pretraining: Qwen2.5 base model with strong mathematical and coding capabilities 2. SFT on diverse reasoning: Fine-tuning on broad mixture of reasoning tasks (math, code, science, logic) 3. Rejection sampling fine-tuning (RFT): Generate solutions per problem, keep correct ones, fine-tune 4. RL phase 1: GRPO on math and code with verifiable rewards 5. RL phase 2: Broader RL including instruction following and safety

sections.title: 13.5.2 Rejection Sampling + RL Combination
sections.content: A key innovation in the Qwen approach is the iterative combination of rejection sampling and RL: 1. Initialize: Policy π0 from SFT model. 2. Rejection Sampling: Sample solutions: {yi}N i=1 πk1( q). Keep correct solutions: +(q) = {yi : r(yi, y) = 1}. 3. SFT update: πSFT SFT(πk1, +(q)) 4. RL update: πk GRPO(πSFT , D) 5. Repeat steps 24 for iterations to obtain final policy πK. The rejection sampling step provides high-quality positive examples that anchor the policy, while RL explores beyond the current distribution. This combination is more stable than pure RL and more capable than pure SFT. 265 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 13.5.3 Tool-Integrated Reasoning
sections.content: QwQ-32B and Qwen3 models support tool-integrated reasoning: the model can invoke external tools (Python interpreter, search engine, calculator) during its reasoning chain. This is implemented via special tokens: < think > Let me solve this step by step . First , ll compute the eigenvalues of the matrix . < tool_call > {" name ": " python " , " arguments ": {" code ": " import numpy as np nA = np . array ([[2 ,1] ,[1 ,3]]) neigenvalues = np . linalg . eigvals ( ) nprint ( eigenvalues ) "}} </ tool_call > < tool_response > [1.38196601 3.61803399] </ tool_response > The eigenvalues are approximately 1.382 and 3.618. These are (5 +/ - sqrt5 ) /2 , which are the golden ratio and its conjugate ... </ think > < answer > The eigenvalues are (5 +/ - sqrt5 ) /2 </ answer > Listing 13.1: Tool-integrated reasoning format in QwQ The RL training reward is computed on the final answer, but the model learns to use tools strategically because tool use improves the probability of reaching the correct answer.

sections.title: 13.6.1 Monte Carlo Tree Search for Reasoning
sections.content: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) provides principled framework for reasoning as tree search. In the AlphaProof [238] and related systems, MCTS is applied over reasoning steps rather than game moves.

sections.title: State and Action Space
sections.content: State sk: The partial reasoning chain (q, r1, r2, . . . , rk) where ri are reasoning steps Action a: The next reasoning step (a sentence or paragraph) Terminal state: state containing final answer Reward: R(sterminal) = racc (Eq. [eq:r1_accuracy_reward]) Value Function for Partial Solutions value function (sk) estimates the probability of reaching correct answer from partial state sk: (sk) = (correct answer sk)

M
sections.content: M m=1 R(rolloutm(sk)) (13.30) where rolloutm(sk) is Monte Carlo rollout from sk to terminal state using the current policy. UCB Exploration Node selection uses the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) formula adapted for reasoning: pN (sk) 1 + (sk, a) (13.31) UCB(sk, a) = Q(sk, a) + cpuct πθ(a sk) where: 266 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Q(sk, a) = 1 (sk,a) visits (sk+1) is the mean value of child states πθ(a sk) is the policy prior (language model probability of step a) (sk) is the visit count of state sk (sk, a) is the visit count of the (sk, a) edge cpuct is the exploration constant MCTS-Guided Training MCTS can be used to generate high-quality training data: LMCTS(θ) = πMCTS(a sk) log πθ(a sk) (13.32) where πMCTS(a sk) (sk, a)1/τ is the MCTS policy (visit count distribution with temperature τ ).

sections.title: 13.6.2 Process Reward Models
sections.content: Math-Shepherd: Automated PRM Training Math-Shepherd [246] proposes an automated method for training PRMs without human step-level annotations. The key insight is to use outcomebased estimation: step sk is labeled as correct if there exists completion from sk that reaches the correct answer. Formally, for partial solution (s1, . . . , sk): ˆrk = 1[ (sk+1, . . . , sK) : verify(sK, y) = 1] (13.33) In practice, this is estimated by sampling completions from sk and checking if any are correct: " ˆrk 1 # verify(completem(sk), y) > 0 m=1 (13.34) The PRM is then trained with binary cross-entropy: LPRM(ϕ) = k=1 [ˆrk log rϕ(sk) + (1 ˆrk) log(1 rϕ(sk))] (13.35) PRM for Best-of-N Selection primary application of PRMs is best-of-N selection: generate candidate solutions and select the one with the highest PRM score: = arg max y{y1,...,yN } RPRM(q, y) (13.36) This is more effective than majority voting (which uses ORM) because PRM can distinguish between solutions that reach the same answer via different quality reasoning paths.

sections.title: 13.6.3 Outcome Reward Models and Majority Voting
sections.content: Majority Voting (Self-Consistency) The simplest form of test-time compute scaling is majority voting [124]: generate solutions and return the most common answer: = arg max i=1 1[yi = a] (13.37) Under the assumption that each solution is independently correct with probability > 0.5, the probability that majority voting is correct is: (majority correct) = k=N/

sections.title: 13.6.4 Self-Play for Reasoning
sections.content: Self-play methods generate training data by having the model play both the generator and verifier roles. STaR: Self-Taught Reasoner STaR [223] bootstraps reasoning capabilities iteratively: 1. Generate reasoning chains for problem set 2. Keep chains that lead to correct answers (rejection sampling) 3. Fine-tune on kept chains 4. Repeat with the improved model The key insight is that the model can rationalize correct answers: even if it cannot solve problem from scratch, it can generate plausible reasoning chain given the answer, which can then be used as training data. Self-Play RL In self-play RL for reasoning, the model generates both problems and solutions: Lself-play(θ) = qπgen θ yπsolve θ (q) [r(y, y)] (13.40) where πgen generates problems and πsolve problems that are challenging but solvable. θ θ solves them. The generator is rewarded for producing

sections.title: 13.6.5 Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)
sections.content: RLVR [247] is framework that uses ground-truth verification as the reward signal, applicable to any domain where correctness can be automatically checked.

sections.title: Verifiable Domains
sections.content: Mathematics: Symbolic verification via SymPy, Lean, or Isabelle Code: Unit test execution Formal logic: Proof checking Factual QA: Database lookup Games: Win/loss outcome

sections.title: RLVR Objective
sections.content: LRLVR(θ) = E(q,y)DEyπθ(q) [verify(y, y)] + βDKL[πθ πref] (13.41) The key advantage of RLVR over RLHF is the absence of reward model error: since the reward is computed by deterministic verifier rather than learned model, there is no reward hacking against flawed reward model. The only failure mode is if the model finds solutions that pass verification but are not genuinely correct (e.g., exploiting test case weaknesses in code evaluation). 268 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems RLVR for Code: Reward Hacking Challenges In code generation, the verifier is test suite. model trained with RLVR can learn to: Hardcode test outputs: Return the expected output for each test input without implementing the actual algorithm Exploit weak tests: Pass all provided tests while failing on edge cases Mitigations include: using large, diverse test suites; including adversarial test cases; using executionbased rewards that penalize hardcoding (e.g., checking that the solution runs in O(n log n) time).

sections.title: 13.6.6 Journey Learning
sections.content: Journey Learning [248] proposes training on the full reasoning trajectory, including failed attempts and corrections, rather than only successful final solutions. Motivation Standard rejection sampling discards failed attempts. But failed attempts contain valuable information: Which approaches dont work (negative examples) How to recognize and recover from errors (correction patterns) The structure of the problem space (exploration data) Journey Learning Objective Given trajectory τ = (s0, a0, s1, a1, . . . , sT ) that may include backtracking: Ljourney(θ) = t=0 wt log πθ(at st) (13.42) where the weights wt are designed to emphasize: Steps that lead to eventual success (wt > 1) Correction steps after errors (wt > 1) Steps in failed branches (wt < 1, but > 0)

sections.title: 13.6.7 Quiet-STaR: Reasoning at Every Token
sections.content: Quiet-STaR [230] extends the reasoning paradigm to every token position: rather than generating reasoning chain only before the final answer, the model generates thought at every token position. Formulation For each token position t, the model generates hidden thought zt before predicting the next token xt+1: (xt+1 xt) = Eztπθ(xt) [πθ(xt+1 xt, zt)] (13.43) In practice, this is approximated by mixing the predictions with and without the thought: (xt+1 xt) = α πθ(xt+1 xt, zt) + (1 α) πθ(xt+1 xt) (13.44) Training with REINFORCE Since the thought zt is discrete latent variable, the gradient is estimated using REINFORCE: θLQS = Ezt [θ log πθ(zt xt) (log (xt+1 xt, zt) bt)] (13.45) where bt is baseline (e.g., the no-thought prediction log πθ(xt+1 xt)). H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Computational Cost of Quiet-STaR Quiet-STaR increases inference cost by factor of Lz + 1 where Lz is the thought length, applied at every token position. For sequence of length with thoughts of length Lz = 8, this is 9 increase in compute. This makes Quiet-STaR impractical for long sequences without significant engineering optimizations (e.g., speculative decoding for thoughts, caching).

sections.title: 13.7 Scaling Laws for Reasoning
sections.content: Recent work [249, 250] has established that test-time compute scales predictably with reasoning performance, extending the classical scaling laws [251] into the inference regime.

sections.title: 13.7.1 Training Compute vs. Test-Time Compute Tradeoff
sections.content: The fundamental scaling question for reasoning models is: given fixed total compute budget Ctotal = Ctrain + Ctest (where is the number of queries), how should compute be allocated? Let A(Ctrain, Ctest) denote the accuracy of model trained with Ctrain FLOPs and given Ctest inference FLOPs per query. Empirically: (cid:16) A(Ctrain, Ctest) 1 exp Cα train Cβ test (cid:17) (13.46) for constants a, α, β > 0. The optimal allocation for fixed total budget Ctotal satisfies the condition that marginal return per FLOP is equalized between training and inference: Ctrain =

sections.title:
1
N
sections.content: A Ctest (13.47) Intuitively: one FLOP of training benefits all queries, while one FLOP of test-time benefits only one query. At the optimum, the per-query marginal value of test-time compute is times larger than training compute (because training is amortized). Applying this to Eq. [eq:reasoning_scaling_law] gives the optimal training compute fraction: train Ctotal = α α + β (13.48) For the specific budget structure Ctotal = Ctrain + Ctest, this fraction is independent of under the multiplicative accuracy model. However, in practice α and β are problem-dependent: for high-volume deployments (large ), even small improvements in the base model dominate, favoring training investment. For low-volume, high-stakes queries (small ), test-time compute is more cost-effective.

sections.title: 13.7.2 When to Invest in Longer Chains vs. Better Base Models
sections.content: Reasoning Chain Length vs. Model Capacity The optimal reasoning chain length for model of capacity on problem of difficulty satisfies: (13.49) for some γ > 0. This implies: Cγ Hard problems require longer chains regardless of model size Larger models require shorter chains for the same problem difficulty Diminishing returns: Beyond L, additional tokens provide no benefit and may hurt (overthinking) 270 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems The overthinking phenomenonwhere models with very long reasoning chains perform worse than those with moderate chainshas been empirically observed and is attributed to: Accumulation of errors in long chains (error propagation) Distraction from the main solution path Overconfidence in incorrect intermediate conclusions

sections.title: 13.7.3 Optimal Token Budget Allocation
sections.content: For model with fixed token budget B, the allocation between thinking tokens Tthink and answering tokens Tanswer should satisfy: think = arg max A(T, ) (13.50) Empirically, the optimal split is problem-dependent: Simple problems: think/B 0.3 (30% thinking) Hard problems: think/B 0.8 (80% thinking) Very hard problems: think/B 0.95 (95% thinking, minimal answer) This motivates adaptive thinking budgets: allocating more tokens to harder problems, which can be estimated by the models uncertainty on initial solution attempts.

sections.title: 13.9 Summary and Open Problems
sections.content: The field of RL for reasoning models has advanced remarkably rapidly. Several key lessons have emerged: 1. Verifiable rewards are sufficient: For domains with ground-truth verification (math, code), outcome-only rewards are sufficient for RL to discover sophisticated reasoning strategies, without requiring process reward models. 2. Test-time compute is new axis: Reasoning models introduce new dimension of scaling inference computethat is roughly substitutable with training compute for hard reasoning tasks. 3. Distillation is highly effective: Large reasoning models can transfer their capabilities to much smaller models via supervised fine-tuning on generated chains, often outperforming direct RL training of small models. 4. Emergent meta-cognition: RL training on reasoning tasks produces emergent self-correction and verification behaviors that were not explicitly trained. 271 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Open Problems in RL for Reasoning Several fundamental questions remain open: Generalization: Do reasoning capabilities trained on math/code transfer to other domains (scientific reasoning, planning, social reasoning)? Faithfulness: Are the generated reasoning chains causally responsible for the final answer, or are they post-hoc rationalizations? Optimal search: What is the optimal search strategy during inferencebeam search, MCTS, or something else? Reward design: For domains without ground-truth verifiers, how can we design reliable reward signals for reasoning? Overthinking: How can models learn to allocate the right amount of thinkingneither too little nor too much? Compositional reasoning: Can RL-trained reasoning models solve problems that require composing multiple distinct reasoning skills? The development of reasoning models represents paradigm shift: from language models that know things to language models that can figure things out. The RL methods described in this section are the primary engine driving this shift, and their continued development is likely to be central focus of AI research in the coming years.

sections.title: Evaluation
sections.content: 273 Chapter

sections.title: LLM Evaluation
sections.content: Evaluation is the backbone of any rigorous machine learning pipeline, yet it is perhaps the most underappreciated component in the development of large language models. Unlike classical supervised learning, where held-out test set with ground-truth labels provides clean signal, evaluating LLMs requires grappling with open-ended generation, subjective quality judgments, multi-step reasoning chains, and the ever-present risk of benchmark contamination. This section provides systematic treatment of the evaluation landscape: from the taxonomy of evaluation types and the mechanics of human annotation, through the mathematics of ranking metrics and the practicalities of LLM-as-judge, to the pitfalls that silently corrupt evaluation pipelines.

sections.title: Why Evaluation is Hard for LLMs
sections.content: Three fundamental challenges distinguish LLM evaluation from classical ML evaluation: 1. Output space is unbounded. language model can produce any string; there is rarely single correct answer. 2. Quality is multidimensional. Helpfulness, factuality, safety, coherence, and style are distinct axes that may trade off against each other. 3. Evaluation is itself language task. Judging whether response is good requires understanding, which means evaluation is susceptible to the same failure modes as generation.

sections.title: 14.1 Evaluation Scheme Design
sections.content: Before collecting single data point, practitioners must decide what to measure and how to measure it. principled taxonomy prevents the common mistake of choosing metrics by convenience rather than by alignment with the deployment objective.

sections.title: 14.1.1 Taxonomy of Evaluation Types
sections.content: Intrinsic evaluation measures properties of the model output Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Evaluation. in isolation, without reference to downstream application. Perplexity on held-out corpus, BLEU score against reference translations, and pass@k on coding benchmarks are all intrinsic. Extrinsic evaluation measures the impact of the model on real-world task or system: does integrating the LLM into customer-service pipeline reduce ticket escalation rates? Does the coding assistant increase developer velocity? The IntrinsicExtrinsic Gap Intrinsic metrics are cheap and reproducible but often poorly correlated with real-world utility. model with lower perplexity is not necessarily more helpful. Extrinsic metrics are expensive and slow but directly measure what we care about. mature evaluation strategy uses intrinsic metrics for rapid iteration and extrinsic metrics for final validation. 274 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Automatic vs. Human Evaluation. Automatic evaluation uses deterministic functions (BLEU, exact match) or learned models (BERTScore, LLM-as-judge) to score outputs without human involvement. Human evaluation involves annotators rating or ranking model outputs. Table 14.1 summarises the trade-offs. Table 14.1: Taxonomy of evaluation approaches with key trade-offs. Type Cost Very low Automatic (rule-based) Automatic (model-based) Low Crowdsourced human Expert human Extrinsic / A/B test Medium High Very high Speed Very fast Fast Days Weeks Months Reproducibility Validity Perfect High Medium LowMedium Low LowMedium MediumHigh Medium High Very high Reference-Based vs. Reference-Free Evaluation. Reference-based metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore) compare model output to one or more gold-standard references. Reference-free metrics (perplexity, LLM-as-judge, human preference) assess quality without reference. Reference-free approaches are essential when the output space is too large for exhaustive reference collection, as in open-ended dialogue.

sections.title: Evaluation Strategy for a Dialogue Assistant
sections.content: Development phase: Use automatic metrics (perplexity, ROUGE on summarisation sub-tasks, pass@k on tool-use) for rapid iteration. Run nightly benchmarks on standard suites (MMLU, HellaSwag, HumanEval). Pre-release phase: Conduct human preference study comparing the new model to the previous checkpoint. Use LLM-as-judge for scalable pairwise comparison on diverse prompt set. Post-release phase: Monitor extrinsic metrics (user satisfaction scores, task completion rates) and watch for distribution shift in production prompts. useful decision framework: If the task has clear correct answer (math, code, factual QA): use exact match or executionbased metrics. If the task is open-ended but has reference outputs: use reference-based metrics as lower bound, supplement with LLM-as-judge. If the task is subjective (helpfulness, tone, creativity): use human evaluation or well-calibrated LLM judge. If the task involves multi-step agent behaviour: use task success rate and trajectory efficiency (Section 14.6).

sections.title: 14.2 Data Collection for Evaluation
sections.content: High-quality evaluation data is the foundation of trustworthy benchmarks. This section covers the design of human annotation pipelines, statistical measures of annotation quality, and the choice between crowdsourcing and expert annotation.

sections.title: 14.2.1 Human Annotation Pipelines
sections.content: A robust annotation pipeline consists of five stages: 1. Task definition. Specify the annotation task precisely: what is being rated, on what scale, and with what criteria. Ambiguity at this stage propagates into noisy labels. 275 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems 2. Guideline development. Write annotation guidelines with worked examples covering edge cases. Iterate with small pilot group before full deployment. 3. Annotator recruitment and training. Select annotators with appropriate background knowledge. Conduct calibration session where annotators label the same examples and discuss disagreements. 4. Quality control. Embed gold-standard examples with known labels into the annotation queue. Flag annotators whose accuracy on gold examples falls below threshold. 5. Aggregation. Combine multiple annotations per item using majority vote, averaging, or probabilistic model (e.g., DawidSkene). 14.2.2 Inter-Annotator Agreement Raw agreement (fraction of items where all annotators agree) is an inadequate measure because it does not account for chance agreement. Two standard chance-corrected measures are Cohens κ [252] (two annotators) and Fleiss κ [253] (multiple annotators). Cohens Kappa. Given two annotators labelling items into categories, let po be the observed agreement and pe be the expected agreement under independence: κ = po pe 1 pe (14.1) where and po =

Cohens κ ranges from 1 (perfect disagreement) through 0 (chance agreement) to 1 (perfect agreement). Values above 0.6 are generally considered acceptable; above 0.8 is strong agreement. Fleiss Kappa. sections.title: Kappa Limitations
sections.content: Kappa is sensitive to the prevalence of categories: when one category dominates, kappa can be low even when raw agreement is high (the kappa paradox). For ordinal scales, weighted kappa (which penalises disagreements proportionally to their distance) is more appropriate. For LLM evaluation, where ratings are often on 15 Likert scale, always report weighted kappa. 276 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 14.2.3 Annotation Guideline Design
sections.content: Effective annotation guidelines share several properties: Operationalised criteria. Replace vague terms like helpful with concrete, observable behaviours: The response directly addresses the users question and provides all information needed to complete the stated task. Worked examples. Provide at least two examples per rating level, including borderline cases. Decision trees. For complex tasks, flowchart that guides annotators through sequence of binary decisions reduces cognitive load and improves consistency. Explicit scope. State what annotators should not consider (e.g., Do not penalise for stylistic preferences; focus only on factual accuracy).

sections.title: 14.2.4 Crowdsourcing vs. Expert Annotation
sections.content: Table 14.2: Comparison of crowdsourcing and expert annotation for LLM evaluation.

sections.content: Low (0.01 0.10) Very high Variable Simple preference, fluency MTurk, Prolific, Scale AI Gold examples, checks attention High (1 50) Low High High Technical accuracy, safety Domain specialists, in-house Calibration sessions, peer review For safety-critical evaluation (e.g., detecting harmful outputs, evaluating medical advice), expert annotation is non-negotiable. For large-scale preference collection (e.g., building reward model training set), crowdsourcing with rigorous quality control is often the only feasible option.

sections.title: 14.3 Synthetic Data Generation for Evaluation
sections.content: Human annotation is expensive and slow. Synthetic data generation uses LLMs themselves to produce evaluation data at scale. This section covers the major paradigms.

sections.title: 14.3.1 LLM-as-Judge for Calibration
sections.content: When using an LLM to generate evaluation labels, calibration is essential: the judges scores must be aligned with human judgments. Let hi [0, 1] be the human preference score for item and ˆhi be the judges predicted score. Calibration error is measured by the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) [254]: ECE = b=1 Bb acc(Bb) conf(Bb) (14.7) where Bb is the b-th confidence bin, acc(Bb) is the fraction of items in the bin where the judge agrees with humans, and conf(Bb) is the mean judge confidence in that bin. well-calibrated judge satisfies E[ˆhi ˆhi = p] = for all [0, 1]. Calibration can be improved by temperature scaling: replacing the judges raw logit with z/T where is tuned on held-out calibration set to minimise negative log-likelihood. 277 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 14.3.2 Self-Instruct
sections.content: Self-Instruct [255] bootstraps instruction-following data from seed set of human-written tasks. The algorithm: 1. Maintain task pool initialised with 175 seed tasks. 2. Sample 8 tasks from the pool; use them as few-shot examples to prompt the LLM to generate new tasks. 3. Filter generated tasks: remove near-duplicates (ROUGE-L similarity > 0.7 with any existing task), classify as classification vs. non-classification, and generate inputoutput instances. 4. Add accepted tasks to the pool. 5. Repeat until the desired pool size is reached. Self-Instruct Prompt Template system_prompt = " " " Come up with series of tasks : Task 1: { d _ k_ 1 _i ns ru ti on } Task 2: { d _ k_ 2 _i ns ru ti on } ... Task 8: { d _ k_ 8 _i ns ru ti on } Task 9: " " " The model completes the prompt, generating new task instruction. separate prompt then generates inputoutput pairs for the new task.

sections.title: 14.3.3 Evol-Instruct
sections.content: Evol-Instruct [256] evolves seed instruction set by iteratively rewriting instructions to be more complex or diverse. Two evolution operators are applied: In-depth evolution: Add constraints, increase reasoning steps, concretise abstractions, deepen domain knowledge requirements. In-breadth evolution: Generate new instruction on related but different topic, increasing topic diversity. An instruction is accepted if it passes an elimination filter: the evolved instruction must not be simple copy, must not contain Im sorry or similar refusals, and must not be shorter than the original.

sections.title: 14.3.4 Constitutional AI Data Generation
sections.content: Constitutional AI (CAI) [129] generates preference data by having the model critique and revise its own outputs according to set of principles (the constitution). The pipeline: 1. Supervised learning phase: Sample harmful prompt, generate an initial response, then prompt the model to critique the response according to constitutional principle and revise it. Use the revised response as supervised fine-tuning target. 2. RL phase: Generate pairs of responses (original vs. revised), use the model to label which is more constitutional, and train preference model on these labels. Use the preference model as reward signal for RLHF. This approach generates preference data without human labelling of harmful content, reducing annotator exposure to distressing material. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 14.3.5 Distillation for Evaluation Data
sections.content: A powerful teacher model (e.g., GPT-4) can generate high-quality evaluation data for training smaller judge model. The distillation pipeline: 1. Collect diverse set of prompts and model responses. 2. Use the teacher to generate detailed judgments (scores + rationales). 3. Fine-tune smaller model on (prompt, response, judgment) triples. 4. Validate the student judge against held-out human annotations. Distillation Bias student judge distilled from single teacher inherits the teachers biases, including verbosity bias (preferring longer responses), self-enhancement bias (if the teacher is also the model being evaluated), and positional bias. Always validate distilled judges against independent human annotations.

sections.title: 14.3.6 Arena-Style Pairwise Generation
sections.content: Chatbot Arena [257] generates evaluation data through crowdsourced battle platform where users submit prompts and vote on which of two anonymised model responses they prefer. This produces large-scale, naturally diverse dataset of pairwise preferences. The key design choices: Anonymisation: Model identities are hidden to prevent brand bias. User-submitted prompts: Ensures prompt diversity and real-world relevance. Tie handling: Users can declare tie or indicate that both responses are bad. Deduplication: Near-duplicate prompts are filtered to prevent over-representation of common queries.

sections.title: 14.4 Metrics for Ranking Tasks
sections.content: When the goal is to rank models by quality, pairwise comparison data is more reliable than absolute scores. This section derives the major ranking systems used in LLM evaluation.

sections.title: 14.4.1 ELO Rating System
sections.content: The ELO system [258], originally developed for chess, assigns each player (model) scalar rating such that the expected score of player against player is: EA = 1 1 + 10(RBRA)/400 (14.8) Derivation. The ELO model assumes that each players performance on given game is random variable drawn from logistic distribution centred at their rating. The probability that beats is: (A B) = σ (cid:18) RA RB (cid:19) = 1 1 + e(RARB)/s (14.9) where = 400/ ln(10) 173.7 is scale parameter chosen so that 400-point difference corresponds to 10 : 1 odds ratio. After each game with outcome SA {0, 0.5, 1} (loss, draw, win), ratings are updated: RA RA + K(SA EA), RB RB + K(SB EB) (14.10) where is the K-factor controlling the learning rate. In Chatbot Arena, = 4 is used. 279 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems ELO Intuition ELO is stochastic gradient descent update on the log-likelihood of the observed outcomes under the logistic model. Each game provides noisy gradient signal; the K-factor controls the step size. large adapts quickly but is noisy; small is stable but slow to reflect true skill changes. Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for ELO. Because ELO ratings depend on the order in which games are processed, confidence intervals are computed by bootstrap resampling: resample the battle log with replacement = 1000 times, recompute ELO ratings from scratch for each resample, and report the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles as the 95% confidence interval.

sections.title: 14.4.2 Bradley–Terry Model
sections.content: The BradleyTerry (BT) model [198] is maximum-likelihood alternative to ELO. Given models with strength parameters β1, . . . , βn > 0, the probability that model beats model is: (i j) = βi βi + βj (14.11) Given set of pairwise outcomes {(ik, jk, yk)}M k=1 where yk = 1 if ik beats jk and yk = 0 otherwise, the log-likelihood is: ℓ(β) = " k=1 yk log βik βik + βjk + (1 yk) log # βjk βik + βjk (14.12) The MLE ˆβ is found by iterative scaling or gradient ascent. The BT model is identifiable only up log βi = 0. Working in log-space with to multiplicative constant; common normalisation is θi = log βi gives: (i j) = σ(θi θj) (14.13) which is equivalent to logistic regression with item-specific intercepts. The BT model is preferred over ELO when the full battle history is available, as it uses all data simultaneously rather than processing games sequentially.

sections.title: 14.4.3 TrueSkill
sections.content: TrueSkill [259] is Bayesian skill rating system that models each players skill as Gaussian random variable si (µi, σ2 ). The performance of player in game is pi = si + ϵi where ϵi (0, β2) is game-specific noise. Player beats player if pi > pj. The posterior update after observing is computed via expectation propagation (EP). The key update equations for the winner are: µi µi + σ2 v (cid:19) (cid:18) µi µj " σ2 σ2 1 σ2 c2 (cid:18) µi µj (cid:19)# (14.14) (14.15) 2β2 + σ + σ2 where = , and v(t) = ϕ(t)/Φ(t), w(t) = v(t)(v(t) + t) are the truncated Gaussian correction factors (ϕ and Φ are the standard normal PDF and CDF). TrueSkills uncertainty estimate σi is particularly useful for identifying models that need more evaluation data. 280 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 14.4.4 Win Rate with Confidence Intervals
sections.content: The simplest ranking metric is the win rate: the fraction of pairwise comparisons in which model is preferred. Given comparisons with wins, the win rate is ˆp = w/n. Wilson score confidence interval [260] is preferred over the naive Wald interval because it has better coverage near = 0 and = 1: ˆp + z2 2n ˆp(1ˆp) + z2 4n2 CI = 1 + z2 (14.16) where = 1.96 for 95% interval. For multi-way comparisons, win rate should be computed against fixed baseline model to ensure comparability.

sections.title: 14.4.5 Chatbot Arena Methodology
sections.content: Chatbot Arena [257] combines the above elements into production-scale evaluation system: 1. Users submit prompts and receive responses from two anonymised models. 2. Users vote for the preferred response (or declare tie). 3. Votes are aggregated using the BT model to produce leaderboard. 4. Bootstrap confidence intervals are reported for each models score. 5. Models with overlapping confidence intervals are considered statistically indistinguishable. As of 2024, Chatbot Arena has collected over one million human preference votes, making it the largest publicly available LLM preference dataset.

sections.title: 14.5 Metrics for Generation Tasks
sections.content: Generation metrics quantify the quality of model outputs for tasks with reference answers or welldefined correctness criteria.

sections.title: 14.5.1 BLEU
sections.content: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) [261] measures n-gram precision between hypothesis and one or more references R: BLEU = BP exp ! wn log pn n= (14.17) where pn is the modified n-gram precision, wn = 1/N are uniform weights, and BP is the brevity penalty: (1 BP = e1r/h if > if r (14.18) with being the length of the closest reference. Modified n-gram precision clips each n-gram count to its maximum count in any reference: pn = ngramh min(count(ngram, h), maxrR count(ngram, r)) ngramh count(ngram, h) (14.19) BLEU Limitations BLEU was designed for machine translation with multiple references. For open-ended generation with single reference, BLEU scores are often near zero even for high-quality outputs. BLEU does not capture semantic similarity, penalises valid paraphrases, and is sensitive to tokenisation. Use 281 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems BLEU only when multiple diverse references are available and the task has low output diversity.

sections.title: 14.5.2 ROUGE
sections.content: ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) [262] is family of recall-oriented metrics designed for summarisation: ROUGE-N = rR ngramr min(count(ngram, h), count(ngram, r)) ngramr count(ngram, r) rR ROUGE-L = LCS(h, r) (14.20) (14.21) where LCS denotes the longest common subsequence. ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2 measure unigram and bigram recall; ROUGE-L captures sentence-level structure. The F-measure variant balances precision and recall: ROUGE-NF = (1 + β2) β2P + (14.22) with β = 1 for equal weighting.

sections.title: 14.5.3 BERTScore
sections.content: BERTScore [263] computes token-level similarity using contextual embeddings from pre-trained BERT model. Given hypothesis tokens ˆx = ˆx1, . . . , ˆxm and reference tokens = x1, . . . , xn with embeddings ˆei and ej: RBERT = PBERT = 1 1 ˆx xj max ˆxiˆx ˆe ej ˆeiej ˆxiˆx max xj ˆe ej ˆeiej FBERT = 2 PBERT RBERT PBERT + RBERT (14.23) (14.24) (14.25) BERTScore correlates better with human judgments than BLEU and ROUGE, particularly for paraphrases and semantically equivalent but lexically different outputs. Importance weighting using inverse document frequency (IDF) further improves correlation:

sections.title: Ridf
sections.content: BERT =

sections.title: 14.5.4 METEOR
sections.content: P xj idf(xj) maxˆxi cos(ˆei, ej) xj idf(xj) (14.26) METEOR [264] addresses BLEUs recall blindness by computing an F-score over unigram matches, with additional modules for stemming and synonym matching: METEOR = Fmean (1 Pen) (14.27) where Fmean = 10P R+9P (recall-weighted harmonic mean) and the fragmentation penalty Pen = 0.5 (c/um)3 penalises non-contiguous matches (c = number of chunks, um = number of matched unigrams). 282 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 14.5.5 Perplexity
sections.content: Perplexity measures how well language model predicts held-out text sequence w1, w2, . . . , wT : PPL(w1:T ) = exp log Pθ(wt w1:t1) ! 1 T t=1 (14.28) Lower perplexity indicates better predictive performance. Perplexity is useful for comparing models on the same tokenisation and test set, but is not directly comparable across models with different vocabularies or tokenisers. For evaluation purposes, perplexity is most useful as sanity check and for detecting distribution shift.

sections.title: 14.5.6 Pass@k for Code
sections.content: For code generation, functional correctness is measured by executing generated code against test cases. The pass@k metric [265] estimates the probability that at least one of generated samples passes all tests: pass@k = Eproblems 1 " # (cid:1) (cid:0)nc (cid:1) (cid:0)n (14.29) where is the total number of samples generated per problem and is the number that pass. This unbiased estimator avoids the high variance of the naive estimator (which samples exactly solutions and checks if any pass). In practice, = 200 samples are generated and pass@1, pass@10, pass@100 are reported. Pass@k Computation import numpy as np from scipy . special import comb def pass_at_k ( : int , : int , : int ) -> float : " " " Unbiased estimator for pass@k . Args : : total samples generated per problem : number of samples that pass all tests : number of samples to consider " " " if - < : return 1.0 return 1.0 - comb ( - , , exact = True ) / comb (n , , exact = True ) # Example : 200 samples , 15 pass , compute pass@1 , pass@10 , pass@100 for in [1 , 10 , 100]: score = pass_at_k ( =200 , =15 , = ) print ( " pass@ { }: { score :.4 } " ) 0.0750 # pass@1 : # pass@10 : 0.5391 # pass@100 : 0.

sections.title: 14.5.7 Exact Match and F1
sections.content: For extractive question answering (e.g., SQuAD), two standard metrics are: Exact Match (EM): Binary indicator of whether the predicted answer string exactly matches any gold answer after normalisation (lowercasing, removing articles and punctuation). Token-level F1: Treats prediction and gold answer as bags of tokens and computes the F1 score: 1 = 2 pred gold pred + gold 283 (14.30) H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems For multi-answer settings, the maximum F1 over all gold answers is reported. Table 14.3: Summary of generation metrics: applicability and key properties. Metric Task Reference-free? Human correlation Translation Summarisation General NLG Translation LM quality Code generation BLEU ROUGE BERTScore METEOR Perplexity Pass@k Exact Match Extractive QA Extractive QA Token No No No No Yes No (tests) No No LowMedium Medium High MediumHigh Low Very high Very high High

sections.title: 14.6 Metrics for Agentic Tasks
sections.content: Agentic LLMs operate in environments, take sequences of actions, and must complete multi-step tasks. Standard generation metrics are insufficient; agentic evaluation requires metrics that capture task completion, efficiency, and the quality of intermediate steps.

sections.title: 14.6.1 Task Success Rate
sections.content: The primary metric for agentic tasks is the task success rate (TSR): the fraction of tasks for which the agent achieves the specified goal state: TSR = 1 τ 1[goal(τ ) achieved] (14.31) Goal achievement is typically verified by deterministic oracle (e.g., checking database state, file system state, or test case execution). For tasks with partial credit, graded success metric can be defined: score(τ ) [0, 1] (14.32) TSRgraded =

sections.title: 14.6.2 Trajectory Efficiency
sections.content: 1 τ successful agent should complete tasks with minimal unnecessary actions. Trajectory efficiency measures the ratio of the optimal trajectory length to the agents actual trajectory length: η = Lagent (14.33) where is the length of the shortest successful trajectory (computed by an oracle or human expert) and Lagent is the number of actions taken by the agent. η (0, 1] with η = 1 indicating optimal efficiency. For failed trajectories, η = 0. complementary metric is the redundancy rate: the fraction of agent actions that are not present in any optimal trajectory.

sections.title: 14.6.3 Tool-Use Accuracy
sections.content: For agents that invoke external tools (APIs, code interpreters, search engines), tool-use accuracy measures the correctness of tool calls: TUA = # correct tool calls # total tool calls (14.34) tool call is correct if (a) the correct tool is selected, (b) the arguments are valid, and (c) the call is made at the appropriate point in the trajectory. Partial credit can be assigned for correct tool selection with incorrect arguments. H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems

sections.title: 14.6.4 Multi-Step Reasoning Accuracy
sections.content: For tasks requiring chains of reasoning (e.g., multi-hop QA, mathematical problem solving), step-level accuracy measures the fraction of reasoning steps that are correct: SRA = 1 τ 1 Sτ sSτ 1[s is correct] (14.35) where Sτ is the set of reasoning steps in trajectory τ . Step correctness can be verified by process reward model (PRM) or by human annotation.

sections.title: 14.6.5 SWE-bench Methodology
sections.content: SWE-bench [266] evaluates LLMs on real-world software engineering tasks: given GitHub issue description and the repository codebase, the model must generate patch that resolves the issue. Evaluation proceeds as follows: 1. The model is given the issue description and relevant code context. 2. The model generates patch (unified diff format). 3. The patch is applied to the repository. 4. The repositorys test suite is executed; the task is successful if all tests pass. The primary metric is % Resolved: the fraction of issues for which the generated patch passes all tests. SWE-bench Verified is curated subset of 500 problems verified by human annotators to be solvable and unambiguous. SWE-bench Lite is 300-problem subset designed for faster evaluation. SWE-bench Key Statistics (as of 2024) Full benchmark: 2,294 tasks from 12 popular Python repositories. Best open-source agent: 43% resolved (SWE-bench Verified). Human performance: 87% resolved (with 15 minutes per task). Evaluation cost: $0.25 per task for API-based models.

sections.title: 14.6.6 WebArena Methodology
sections.content: WebArena [267] evaluates agents on realistic web navigation tasks in sandboxed browser environment. The benchmark includes 812 tasks across five web applications (e-commerce, social forum, collaborative development, content management, and maps). Evaluation: Functional evaluation: The task outcome is verified by checking the application state (e.g., Was the item added to the cart?, Was the post created?). URL-based evaluation: For navigation tasks, the final URL is compared to the expected URL. Program-based evaluation: custom evaluator script checks complex conditions (e.g., Is the price less than $50?). The primary metric is task success rate. Human performance is approximately 78%; state-of-the-art agents achieve approximately 3545%.

sections.title: 14.7 LLM-as-Judge
sections.content: LLM-as-judge [257] uses capable LLM to evaluate the outputs of other (or the same) LLMs. This approach scales to large evaluation sets without human annotation and can provide detailed rationales for its judgments. 285 H. Roitman The Hitchhikers Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems Table 14.4: Comparison of agentic evaluation benchmarks. Benchmark Domain # Tasks Eval Method SOTA (%) SWE-bench SWE-bench Lite Software engineering Software engineering Web navigation Household tasks 2,294 300 812 3,553 1,091 Test execution Test execution 50 State/URL/program 40 90 Simulator state 45 Task-specific WebArena ALFWorld [268] AgentBench [269] Multi-domain

sections.title: 14.7.1 Setup and Prompt Templates
sections.content: The judge is presented with prompt, one or more model responses, and an evaluation rubric. Three common formats: Pointwise scoring. The judge assigns an absolute score to single response: Pointwise Judge Prompt POINTWISE_PROMPT = " " " You are an expert evaluator . Rate the following response on scale of 1 -10 for helpfulness , accuracy , and clarity . [ Question ] { question } [ Response ] { response } Provide your evaluation in the following format : Reasoning : < step - by - step analysis > Score : < integer from 1 to 10 > " " " Pairwise comparison. The judge compares two responses and selects the better one:

sections.title: Pairwise Judge Prompt
sections.content: PAIRWISE_PROMPT = " " " You are an expert evaluator . Compare the two responses below and determine which is better . Consider helpfulness , accuracy , and depth of explanation . [ Question ] { question } [ Response ] { response_a } [ Response ] { response_b } Output exactly one of : [[ ]] , [[ ]] , or [[ ]] ( tie ) .
